name: DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES

topics.title: Structure of Automatic Industrial Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structure of Automatic Industrial Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An *Automatic Industrial System* is an integrated arrangement of machines, mechanisms, sensors, actuators, controllers, and information-processing units that operate with minimal or no human intervention to perform manufacturing, assembly, or material-handling operations in a repeatable and programmable manner.

In simpler words, an **automatic industrial system** is a "self-running factory" — a carefully engineered combination of *eyes (sensors)*, *a brain (controller)*, and *muscles (actuators)* that together execute an industrial task from start to finish without a human hand touching the workpiece.

## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body of a Factory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Hook (Use this in your viva!)**
> Just like the human body coordinates senses → brain → muscles, every automated system follows the **S-C-A loop**:
>
> **Sensors** (feel the world) → **Controller** (decide what to do) → **Actuators** (move the world)

Think of a fully automatic CNC turning cell. A proximity **sensor** detects that a raw blank has arrived on the conveyor. The **controller** (a PLC) reads this signal, runs its ladder logic, and energizes a solenoid. The **actuator** (pneumatic cylinder) pushes the blank into the chuck. The machine then begins a sequence of operations entirely on its own. The human operator only watches a screen.

## 1.2 Why Study the *Structure* of Automation?

Before you can **design** a mechanism for an automated machine (the next module), you must first understand *where* the mechanism fits in the larger industrial picture. The structure of an automatic system answers four fundamental questions:

| Question | Answer Provided by System Structure |
|---|---|
| What drives the process? | **Power source** (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic) |
| How is the process supervised? | **Controller hierarchy** (PLC → SCADA → MES) |
| How is information exchanged? | **Signal flow** (analog/digital/bus protocols) |
| How is performance measured? | **Throughput, cycle time, OEE metrics** |

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Components

Every automatic industrial system, no matter how complex, is built from three foundational blocks:

1. **The Process / Workstation** — the physical manufacturing equipment (lathe, robot, conveyor, oven).
2. **The Control System** — the decision-making intelligence (relays, microcontrollers, PLCs, industrial PCs).
3. **The Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** — the dashboard the operator uses to monitor and command the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to "list the basic elements of an automated system." Memorize the trio: *Process + Control + HMI*. This is a guaranteed 2 marks.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

Modern automotive assembly lines (e.g., a Maruti Suzuki or Tata Motors plant) integrate **more than 200 robotic workstations** into a single automatic structure. Without a clear, hierarchical **structure**, coordinating these workstations would be impossible. Understanding this structure is the first step toward *designing* the mechanisms that drive them — which is the core aim of the PERUT743 course.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Automation Pyramid (CIM Hierarchy)

Modern factories are organized in **five hierarchical levels**. Each higher level deals with *broader decisions* (planning) and each lower level deals with *faster decisions* (action). This is the famous **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) pyramid**.

### Level 0 — Field / Device Level
- **Contents:** Sensors, actuators, motors, drives, valves.
- **Speed:** *Milliseconds.*
- **Example:** A photoelectric sensor detecting a label on a bottle.

### Level 1 — Machine / Control Level
- **Contents:** PLCs, CNC controllers, robotic controllers.
- **Speed:** *Tens of milliseconds.*
- **Example:** A Siemens S7-1200 PLC executing a ladder program.

### Level 2 — Cell / Supervisory Level
- **Contents:** SCADA servers, HMIs, data historians.
- **Speed:** *Seconds.*
- **Example:** Wonderware InTouch SCADA displaying tank levels.

### Level 3 — Plant / Operations Level
- **Contents:** MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).
- **Speed:** *Minutes to hours.*
- **Example:** Tracking which batches are running on which line.

### Level 4 — Enterprise / Network Level
- **Contents:** ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning).
- **Speed:** *Days to weeks.*
- **Example:** SAP managing procurement for the whole factory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIM Pyramid Rule (Vertical Integration):** Information flows **downward** (commands) and **upward** (feedback). Lower levels must respond faster than higher levels. This is called the *inverse-relationship principle of speed and intelligence.*

## 2.2 The Sensor–Controller–Actuator (S-C-A) Loop

The functional heart of any automatic system. The closed-loop flow is:

1. **Stimulus** from the process reaches the **sensor**.
2. The **sensor** transduces the physical quantity (position, temperature, pressure) into an electrical signal.
3. The **controller** compares the signal to a stored setpoint, runs its algorithm, and generates a control command.
4. The **actuator** (motor, solenoid, hydraulic cylinder) physically modifies the process.
5. The new process state is again sensed — closing the loop.

## 2.3 Classification of Automation Structures

KTU examiners love this classification table. Memorize it for direct theory questions.

| # | Type of Automation | Typical Volume | Flexibility | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fixed (Hard) Automation** | Very High | Very Low | Car engine assembly line |
| 2 | **Programmable Automation** | Medium–High | Medium | CNC machining center |
| 3 | **Flexible Automation** | Low–Medium | High | FMS with robots |
| 4 | **Integrated Automation** | Variable | Very High | CIM factory |

### 2.3.1 Fixed Automation
*Special-purpose equipment* designed for one product. The sequence is hard-wired (cams, gears, relays). **Lowest unit cost at high volume, highest retooling cost.**

### 2.3.2 Programmable Automation
The sequence of operations is stored as a **program**. Changing the program (e.g., a new G-code on a CNC) yields a new product. **Best for batch production.**

### 2.3.3 Flexible Automation
An *extension* of programmable automation. The system can produce **multiple products in any mix** with virtually *no lost changeover time*. Built around industrial robots and automated guided vehicles (AGVs).

### 2.3.4 Integrated Automation (CIM)
The apex — all enterprise functions (design, planning, production, dispatch, sales) are linked by a common **computer database**, eliminating paperwork and human decision delays.

## 2.4 Hardware vs. Software Architecture

| Layer | Hardware Elements | Software Elements |
|---|---|---|
| **Field** | Sensors, motors, drives | Firmware, embedded C |
| **Control** | PLC, CNC, DCS | Ladder logic, G-code |
| **Supervisory** | Industrial PCs, network switches | SCADA, HMI screens |
| **Operations** | Servers, databases | MES, scheduling tools |
| **Enterprise** | Cloud servers, ERP hardware | SAP, Oracle ERP |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cycle-Time & Throughput)

> [!NOTE]
> These equations are **guaranteed to appear** in any Part-B numerical on this module.

| Concept | Symbol | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Workstation Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $T_c = T_o + T_h + T_{n/o}$ | seconds |
| Operating Time | $T_o$ | $T_o = T_a + T_h'$ | seconds |
| Production Time per Part | $T_p$ | $T_p = T_c + T_{su}$ | seconds |
| Production Rate | $R_p$ | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | parts/second |
| Number of Workstations | $N_w$ | $N_w = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{T_c}$ | workstations |
| Line Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{N_w \cdot T_c} \times 100$ | percent |
| Balance Delay | $B_d$ | $B_d = \dfrac{N_w T_c - \sum T_i}{N_w T_c} \times 100$ | percent |

**Where:**
- $T_o$ = time spent actually working on the part
- $T_h$ = handling time (load/unload)
- $T_{n/o}$ = non-operating time (idle, waiting)
- $T_{su}$ = set-up time amortized per part
- $\sum T_i$ = sum of task times for all manual operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The *line efficiency* tells you how well the workload is balanced. A value **above 85 percent** indicates a well-designed automation cell; anything below 60 percent signals poor task distribution that must be redesigned.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents *fully worked derivations* of the most important KTU formulas on automatic industrial system structure. Every algebraic step is shown.

## 3.1 Derivation of Production Rate ($R_p$) from First Principles

The production rate is the **number of parts completed per unit time**. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Step 1 — Define the production interval.**
A finished part emerges from the system once every $T_p$ seconds, where $T_p$ is the time from one part completion to the next.

**Step 2 — Express one interval in terms of its components.**
In any realistic cell, the time to produce one part equals the workstation cycle time *plus* the per-part amortized setup time:
$$T_p = T_c + T_{su}$$

**Step 3 — Take the reciprocal.**
Since one part is produced every $T_p$ seconds, the rate is the inverse of the interval:
$$R_p = \frac{1}{T_p}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $T_p$.**
$$R_p = \frac{1}{T_c + T_{su}}$$

**Step 5 — Insert units.**
If $T_p$ is in seconds, then $R_p$ has units of $\text{parts/second}$. To convert to parts/hour, multiply by **3600**:
$$R_p \;\left[\frac{\text{parts}}{\text{hour}}\right] = \frac{3600}{T_c + T_{su}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Number of Workstations ($N_w$)

A single operator/workstation can perform a *sum of task times* $\sum T_i$ within one cycle. We need to find how many parallel workstations are required.

**Step 1 — Identify the limiting constraint.**
The cycle time $T_c$ is the maximum time any *single* workstation may take. No workstation can exceed $T_c$ without becoming the bottleneck.

**Step 2 — Write the workload-per-workstation.**
If a workstation handles tasks of total duration $\sum T_i$, and it must finish within $T_c$, then:
$$N_w \cdot T_c \;\geq\; \sum T_i$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the minimum integer $N_w$.**
$$N_w \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\sum T_i}{T_c} \right\rceil$$

The *ceiling* brackets $\lceil \; \rceil$ indicate rounding **up** to the next integer, because a fractional workstation is not physically realizable.

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose $\sum T_i = 65$ minutes and $T_c = 20$ minutes.
$$N_w = \left\lceil \frac{65}{20} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.25 \rceil = 4 \;\text{workstations}$$

The resulting actual cycle time is $\;T_c^{\text{actual}} = \frac{65}{4} = 16.25\;\text{min}$. The idle time per station is $20 - 16.25 = 3.75\;\text{min}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Line Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Step 1 — Define the useful work content.**
The total productive work is $\sum T_i$.

**Step 2 — Define the available productive time.**
With $N_w$ workstations running in parallel for $T_c$ each, the total available labor time is $N_w \cdot T_c$.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**
$$\eta = \frac{\text{useful work}}{\text{available time}} = \frac{\sum T_i}{N_w \cdot T_c}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**
$$\eta \;[\%] = \frac{\sum T_i}{N_w \cdot T_c} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that $N_w \cdot T_c$ is always **greater than or equal to** $\sum T_i$, so efficiency can never exceed 100 percent. If your calculation does so, you have a rounding error.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Balance Delay ($B_d$)

**Step 1 — Compute the idle time.**
Idle time across all workstations is $N_w T_c - \sum T_i$.

**Step 2 — Express it as a fraction of the total available time.**
$$B_d = \frac{N_w T_c - \sum T_i}{N_w T_c}$$

**Step 3 — Express as percentage.**
$$B_d \;[\%] = \left( 1 - \eta \right) \times 100$$

This elegant relationship tells us: **balance delay and efficiency are complementary.** A perfectly balanced line ($\eta = 100\%$) has $B_d = 0\%$.

---

## 3.5 Complete Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An assembly cell performs four tasks with times $T_1 = 7$, $T_2 = 11$, $T_3 = 5$, $T_4 = 9$ minutes. The required production rate is **0.05 parts per minute** (i.e., 1 part every 20 minutes). Determine (a) the minimum number of workstations, (b) the line efficiency, and (c) the balance delay.

**Step (a) — Minimum number of workstations.**

First compute the total work content:
$$\sum T_i = 7 + 11 + 5 + 9 = 32\;\text{min}$$

The required cycle time from the production rate is:
$$T_c = \frac{1}{R_p} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20\;\text{min}$$

Then:
$$N_w = \left\lceil \frac{32}{20} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.6 \rceil = 2\;\text{workstations}$$

**Step (b) — Line efficiency.**
$$\eta = \frac{32}{2 \times 20} \times 100 = \frac{32}{40} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Step (c) — Balance delay.**
$$B_d = (1 - 0.80) \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Result:** Two workstations, 80 percent efficient, 20 percent balance delay. A reasonable design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following diagrams are drawn in Mermaid syntax with **alphanumeric node IDs** and **no markdown formatting inside labels**, per the V10 safeguard protocol.

## 4.1 The Closed-Loop S-C-A Structure of an Automatic Industrial System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[PROCESS OR WORKSTATION]
    nodeS[SENSOR LAYER]
    nodeC[CONTROLLER PLC CNC]
    nodeA[ACTUATOR LAYER]
    nodeH[HMI AND SCADA LAYER]
    nodeOP[OPERATOR USER]

    nodeP -- MEASURED STATE --> nodeS
    nodeS -- ELECTRICAL SIGNAL --> nodeC
    nodeC -- CONTROL COMMAND --> nodeA
    nodeA -- PHYSICAL ACTION --> nodeP
    nodeOP -- SUPERVISORY COMMANDS --> nodeH
    nodeH -- SETPOINT UPDATE --> nodeC
    nodeC -- STATUS DATA --> nodeH
    nodeH -- DISPLAY --> nodeOP
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The inner triangle *(Process → Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process)* is the **physical closed loop** that runs in milliseconds.
- The outer rectangle *(Operator ↔ HMI ↔ Controller)* is the **supervisory loop** that runs in seconds to minutes.
- Together they form the complete structure of any automatic industrial system.

---

## 4.2 The CIM Automation Pyramid (Five-Level Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4[LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE ERP LAYER]
    L3[LEVEL 3 PLANT OPERATIONS MES LAYER]
    L2[LEVEL 2 CELL SUPERVISORY SCADA HMI LAYER]
    L1[LEVEL 1 MACHINE CONTROL PLC CNC LAYER]
    L0[LEVEL 0 FIELD DEVICE SENSORS AND ACTUATORS LAYER]

    L4 -- ORDERS AND SCHEDULES --> L3
    L3 -- WORK ORDERS --> L2
    L2 -- PROGRAM AND SETPOINT --> L1
    L1 -- I O SIGNALS --> L0
    L0 -- FIELD DATA --> L1
    L1 -- STATUS --> L2
    L2 -- REPORTS --> L3
    L3 -- KPI DATA --> L4
```

**Reading the pyramid:**
- Solid downward arrows = **commands** flowing from strategic layer to field layer.
- Dashed upward arrows = **data** flowing from field sensors to enterprise reports.
- Width of the band visually represents *data volume* — narrowest at Level 0, widest at Level 4.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automated Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[INPUT MATERIAL HANDLING]
        IM[RAW MATERIAL INFEED]
        CV1[CONVEYOR 1]
    end

    subgraph PROC[CELL PROCESSING]
        WS1[WORKSTATION 1 MACHINING]
        WS2[WORKSTATION 2 INSPECTION]
        WS3[WORKSTATION 3 ASSEMBLY]
        RB[INDUSTRIAL ROBOT]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT FINISHING]
        CV2[CONVEYOR 2]
        PL[PACKING STATION]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL AND SUPERVISION]
        PLC[MAIN PLC]
        SCADA[SCADA TERMINAL]
        HMI[OPERATOR HMI PANEL]
    end

    IM --> CV1 --> WS1
    WS1 --> RB
    RB --> WS2
    WS2 --> WS3
    WS3 --> CV2 --> PL

    PLC --- WS1
    PLC --- WS2
    PLC --- WS3
    PLC --- RB
    SCADA --- PLC
    HMI --- SCADA
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **top row (IN)** brings raw blanks into the cell.
- The **middle row (PROC)** is where sensors, controllers, and actuators cooperate to manufacture the part.
- The **bottom row (OUT)** removes finished goods.
- The **right-side control stack** supervises the entire cell from a single PLC, with the SCADA/HMI giving the human operator visibility.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question 1]**
> **Q.** Define an *automatic industrial system*. List its three basic elements.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An automatic industrial system is an integrated combination of machines, sensors, controllers, and actuators capable of performing industrial operations with minimal human intervention.
> The three basic elements are:
> 1. **Process / Workstation** — the physical manufacturing equipment.
> 2. **Control System** — the decision-making intelligence (PLCs, relays).
> 3. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** — the operator's communication dashboard.
> *(Self-explanatory statement + 3 elements = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question 2]**
> **Q.** Differentiate between *fixed automation* and *flexible automation*. Give one example of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Fixed Automation | Flexible Automation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Product variety | Single product | Multiple products in any mix |
> | Changeover | Very expensive / impossible | Quick reprogramming |
> | Volume | Very high | Low to medium |
> | Equipment cost per unit | Very low | Higher but amortized over variants |
> | Example | Automotive engine assembly line | FMS with robots producing 50 part variants |
>
> *(Each valid distinction + example = 1 mark × 3 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> > On numerical questions involving cycle time and number of workstations, students *very commonly* forget to round **$N_w$ UP** to the next integer. Writing $N_w = 1.6$ instead of $2$ will cost you **at least 2 marks**. Also, do not state line efficiency in *decimal* — always express it in **percentage**. Examiners deduct one full mark for unit ambiguity.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Part B Module 1 / Internal Choice Question A]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **structure of a closed-loop automatic industrial system** using the Sensor–Controller–Actuator concept. Explain how information flows between the operator and the controller. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An automated assembly line has six tasks with the following times (in seconds): $T_1 = 40$, $T_2 = 25$, $T_3 = 55$, $T_4 = 30$, $T_5 = 45$, $T_6 = 20$. The required production rate is **30 parts per hour**. Determine the **(i)** cycle time, **(ii)** minimum number of workstations, **(iii)** line efficiency, and **(iv)** balance delay. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

1. **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a closed-loop block diagram containing the four blocks — *Process*, *Sensor*, *Controller*, *Actuator* — with arrows showing the feedback flow.
2. **Process** is the manufacturing equipment whose state is to be controlled.
3. **Sensor** transduces the physical quantity (position, temperature) into an electrical signal.
4. **Controller** (PLC/CNC) compares the measured value with a setpoint and decides the corrective action.
5. **Actuator** (motor, solenoid, cylinder) executes the corrective action on the process.
6. **Operator ↔ HMI ↔ Controller** is the supervisory loop. The operator enters setpoints and receives status data via the HMI/SCADA, which is digitally linked to the controller.
7. **Closed-loop characteristic:** Because feedback continuously adjusts the actuator, the system self-corrects against disturbances — this is the defining feature of automation.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**(i) Cycle time:**

$$R_p = 30\;\text{parts/hour} = \frac{30}{3600}\;\text{parts/second} = \frac{1}{120}\;\text{parts/second}$$

$$T_c = \frac{1}{R_p} = 120\;\text{seconds}$$

**(ii) Minimum number of workstations:**

$$\sum T_i = 40 + 25 + 55 + 30 + 45 + 20 = 215\;\text{seconds}$$

$$N_w = \left\lceil \frac{215}{120} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.79 \rceil = 2\;\text{workstations}$$

**(iii) Line efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{215}{2 \times 120} \times 100 = \frac{215}{240} \times 100 = 89.58\%$$

**(iv) Balance delay:**

$$B_d = (1 - 0.8958) \times 100 = 10.42\%$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Part B Module 1 / Internal Choice Question B]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **five-level CIM automation pyramid** with a neat diagram. State the function of each level and indicate the typical response time of each layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A flexible manufacturing cell performs seven operations with the following task times (in minutes): $T_1 = 6$, $T_2 = 9$, $T_3 = 4$, $T_4 = 12$, $T_5 = 7$, $T_6 = 5$, $T_7 = 8$. The desired production rate is **3 parts per hour**. Calculate the **(i)** cycle time, **(ii)** minimum number of workstations, **(iii)** line efficiency, **(iv)** balance delay, and **(v)** actual production rate achievable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

1. **Level 4 – Enterprise (ERP):** Concerned with *business decisions* — procurement, finance, sales. Response time: *days to weeks.* Example: SAP.
2. **Level 3 – Plant Operations (MES):** Tracks production orders, batches, quality KPIs. Response time: *minutes to hours.*
3. **Level 2 – Cell Supervisory (SCADA / HMI):** Monitors and supervises machines. Response time: *seconds.* Example: Wonderware.
4. **Level 1 – Machine Control (PLC / CNC):** Executes real-time control algorithms. Response time: *tens of milliseconds.*
5. **Level 0 – Field Devices (Sensors / Actuators):** Direct interaction with the physical process. Response time: *milliseconds.*

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the five-level pyramid with downward command flow and upward data flow.

**Characteristic:** Speed of response *increases* as we descend the pyramid; intelligence and decision-making *decrease* as we descend.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**(i) Cycle time:**

$$T_c = \frac{60}{3} = 20\;\text{minutes per part}$$

**(ii) Minimum number of workstations:**

$$\sum T_i = 6 + 9 + 4 + 12 + 7 + 5 + 8 = 51\;\text{min}$$

$$N_w = \left\lceil \frac{51}{20} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.55 \rceil = 3\;\text{workstations}$$

**(iii) Line efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{51}{3 \times 20} \times 100 = \frac{51}{60} \times 100 = 85\%$$

**(iv) Balance delay:**

$$B_d = (1 - 0.85) \times 100 = 15\%$$

**(v) Actual production rate achievable:**

The actual cycle time with $N_w = 3$ workstations is:
$$T_c^{\text{actual}} = \frac{51}{3} = 17\;\text{min/part}$$

Therefore:
$$R_p^{\text{actual}} = \frac{60}{17} = 3.53\;\text{parts/hour}$$

This *exceeds* the desired rate of 3 parts/hour, so the design is feasible with spare capacity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this before your exam!**

- **Definition:** Automatic Industrial System = Process + Control + HMI, operating with minimal human intervention.
- **S-C-A Loop:** Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process → (back to Sensor). Always a **closed loop**.
- **Five CIM Levels (top to bottom):** ERP → MES → SCADA/HMI → PLC/CNC → Sensors/Actuators.
- **CIM Rule:** Speed of response *increases* downward; decision intelligence *decreases* downward.
- **Four Automation Types:** Fixed → Programmable → Flexible → Integrated. Recall an example for each.
- **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = T_o + T_h + T_{n/o}$.
- **Production Rate Formula:** $R_p = \dfrac{1}{T_c + T_{su}}$.
- **Number of Workstations:** $N_w = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum T_i}{T_c} \right\rceil$ — **always round UP.**
- **Line Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{N_w \cdot T_c} \times 100$ — **express in percent.**
- **Balance Delay:** $B_d = (1 - \eta) \times 100$ — complementary to efficiency.
- **Exam Pitfall:** Forgetting ceiling on $N_w$, omitting percent sign, mixing up units (sec vs min vs hr).
- **Engineering Significance:** Studying structure is the prerequisite to designing the actual mechanisms (covered in Modules 2–5 of PERUT743).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Circular Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linear and Circular Configurations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Designing Mechanisms for Automated Machines (PERUT743)**, the term **"Configuration"** refers to the *systematic geometric and kinematic arrangement* of mechanical linkages, actuators, and structural members that define the spatial motion capability of an automated machine. **Module 1** introduces two foundational configurations that form the kinematic backbone of nearly every industrial robot and automated workstation: 1. **Linear Configuration:** A kinematic arrangement in which the end-effector (or a moving member) translates along one or more *straight-line paths*, usually along orthogonal Cartesian axes (X, Y, Z). Energy conversion is achieved through *rotary-to-linear* transformation devices such as lead screws, ball screws, rack-and-pinion systems, belt-pulley drives, or direct-drive linear motors. 2. **Circular (Rotational) Configuration:** A kinematic arrangement in which the end-effector (or a moving member) is positioned by *pure rotation* about a fixed or moving axis, producing trajectories that are segments of circles, arcs, cylinders, or spheres. Energy is delivered through rotary actuators (servo motors, stepper motors, cycloidal reducers, harmonic drives) and the geometry of the work envelope is intrinsically curved. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A student is expected to distinguish between linear and circular configurations in terms of (a) work-envelope geometry, (b) degrees of freedom, (c) drive mechanism selection, and (d) application suitability. These four parameters constitute the **evaluation grid** used by KTU examiners for any 14-mark design question on this topic. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are in a **modern warehouse** where parcels must be moved from a conveyor belt to a shipping container. - If the parcels glide along **straight rails** — sliding left–right, forward–backward, up–down — you are observing a **Linear Configuration**. The motion is *predictable, rigid, and box-like*, much like the cursor of a 3D printer head that traces a rectangular path across a sheet of paper. - If, instead, the parcels are swung through the air by a **robotic arm that pivots at the shoulder and elbow**, you are observing a **Circular Configuration**. The motion is *curved and sweeping*, much like a human hand reaching out to grab a glass from a shelf. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world anchor:** CNC milling machines (Haas, DMG Mori) are predominantly **linear (Cartesian)** because metal cutting demands rigid, straight-line motion. In contrast, automotive welding robots (KUKA, ABB, FANUC) are predominantly **articulated/circular** because they must reach curved contours around a car body. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following metrics are used throughout this module and must be memorized: - **Work Envelope (m³ or m²):** The total spatial volume reachable by the end-effector. - **Repeatability (mm or µm):** The positional deviation when returning to a programmed point, typically **±0.02 mm** for precision Cartesian robots and **±0.05 mm** for articulated robots. - **Payload Capacity (kg or N):** Maximum static load the configuration can handle at rated speed. - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of *independent* positional parameters required to fully define the end-effector pose in 3D space (theoretical maximum = **6 DOF**: 3 translations + 3 rotations). - **Resolution:** Smallest incremental motion achievable, governed by the *lead/pitch of the drive* and the *encoder step count*. > [!NOTE] > The term **DOF** (Degrees of Freedom) was originally formalized by *Franz Reuleaux* in 1875. KTU examiners often reference this historical attribution in 3-mark questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Two Configurations? — The Underlying Logic A mechanism designer faces a fundamental *trade-off* between **rigidity, reach, footprint, and complexity**. Linear configurations offer unmatched rigidity and repeatability (ideal for high-precision, high-payload tasks), while circular configurations offer compactness and dexterity (ideal for tasks in confined workspaces). KTU's pedagogical goal in Module 1 is to equip you with the analytical tools to *quantify* this trade-off. ### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Linear Configuration A linear configuration is engineered as a **stack of independent prismatic joints (P-joints)**, each driven by a rotary actuator and a rotary-to-linear converter. **Step 1 — Actuator selection:** A servo/stepper motor is selected based on required torque, speed, and resolution. **Step 2 — Drive-train selection:** A mechanical transformer (lead screw, ball screw, rack-pinion, belt, or linear motor) converts rotary motion to linear motion. **Step 3 — Frame and guide selection:** Linear guides (profile rails, box ways) constrain off-axis motion, ensuring straightness (typically **< 5 µm / 300 mm travel**). **Step 4 — Closed-loop feedback:** An encoder or linear scale closes the position loop, achieving the required repeatability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Linear configurations dominate in **CNC machining, semiconductor wafer handling, coordinate measuring machines (CMM), and 3D printing** — wherever the workspace is *rectangular* and the motion is *axis-aligned*. ### 2.3 Logical Breakdown of Circular Configuration A circular configuration is engineered as a **serial chain of revolute joints (R-joints)**, each driven directly by a rotary actuator. The end-effector pose is governed by forward and inverse kinematics. **Step 1 — Joint layout:** R-joints are arranged either along a vertical axis (cylindrical / SCARA) or in a serial "arm" form (articulated). **Step 2 — Actuator selection:** Servo motors with high reduction-ratio gearheads (harmonic, cycloidal, planetary) are used to balance torque and speed. **Step 3 — Link design:** Links must be lightweight (often aluminium or carbon-fibre composite) to minimize inertia and maximize dynamic performance. **Step 4 — Kinematic solver:** A real-time inverse-kinematic algorithm converts Cartesian task-space commands into joint-space angles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Circular configurations dominate in **arc welding, painting, assembly, palletizing, and surgical robotics** — wherever the workspace is *curved* and the task requires *approach from multiple angles*. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Applicability | |---|----------|---------|-------------------|----------------| | 1 | Linear displacement from lead screw | $s = N \cdot p$ | $s$ = displacement (m), $N$ = number of revolutions, $p$ = pitch (m) | Linear config | | 2 | Linear velocity from lead screw | $v = f \cdot p$ | $v$ = velocity (m/s), $f$ = rotational frequency (rev/s) | Linear config | | 3 | Torque to drive a loaded lead screw | $T = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{2\pi \cdot \eta}$ | $T$ = torque (N·m), $F$ = axial load (N), $\eta$ = efficiency | Linear config | | 4 | Linear resolution of lead screw | $\Delta s = \dfrac{p}{n_{\text{steps/rev}}}$ | $\Delta s$ = resolution (m/step), $n$ = encoder steps per revolution | Linear config | | 5 | Tangential velocity in circular motion | $v_t = r \cdot \omega$ | $v_t$ (m/s), $r$ = radius (m), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) | Circular config | | 6 | Centripetal acceleration | $a_c = \omega^2 \cdot r = \dfrac{v_t^2}{r}$ | $a_c$ (m/s²) | Circular config | | 7 | Workspace volume — Cartesian (linear) | $V_C = L_X \cdot L_Y \cdot L_Z$ | $L_i$ = stroke along axis $i$ (m) | Linear config | | 8 | Workspace volume — Cylindrical (circular) | $V_{cyl} = \pi \cdot (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) \cdot H$ | $R$ = reach (m), $H$ = vertical stroke (m) | Circular config | | 9 | Workspace volume — Spherical (articulated, ideal) | $V_{sph} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$ | $R$ = reach (m) | Circular config | | 10 | SCARA forward-kinematic end-effector position (planar) | $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$

$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | $L_1, L_2$ = link lengths (m), $\theta_1, \theta_2$ = joint angles (rad) | Circular (SCARA) | | 11 | Angular velocity from linear actuator | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r}$ | $\omega$ (rad/s), $v$ (m/s), $r$ (m) | Hybrid config | | 12 | Power transmission through drive | $P = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $P$ = power (W) | Both configs | > [!WARNING] > In KTU answer sheets, examiners **deduct marks** if students confuse **reach (R)** with **stroke**. *Reach* is the radial distance from the joint axis to the end-effector; *Stroke* is the total linear travel along a guide. Always use the correct variable name in the substitution step. ### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering - **Equation 3** ($T = Fp / 2\pi\eta$) is the *workhorse equation* of every ball-screw sizing problem — used daily by machine-tool design engineers. - **Equation 6** ($a_c = \omega^2 r$) governs the *dynamic payload limit* of high-speed circular mechanisms (e.g., delta robots sorting parcels at 120 picks/min). - **Equations 7–9** are used to *size the cell layout* of a robotic work cell — a key step in *Factory 4.0* digital-twin design. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The following derivations are graded at the **Apply / Analyze** level of *Revised Bloom's Taxonomy* (KTU 2024 Scheme). They form the analytical core expected in **14-mark problems** in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque Required to Drive a Loaded Lead Screw A lead screw of pitch $p$ (metres per revolution) is used to lift a load $F$ (N). The nut advances by a distance $p$ for every full revolution of the screw. The **work done** on the load per revolution equals the force times the displacement: **Step 1 — Work done on the load per revolution:** $$ W_{\text{out}} = F \cdot p $$ **Step 2 — The motor must supply slightly more work to overcome friction in the threads and bearings. If the overall drive efficiency is $\eta$, then:** $$ W_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{\eta} $$ **Step 3 — The motor applies a torque $T$ through one full revolution. The angular displacement per revolution is $2\pi$ radians. Therefore, the work input from the motor is:** $$ W_{\text{in}} = T \cdot 2\pi $$ **Step 4 — Equating the two expressions for $W_{\text{in}}$:** $$ T \cdot 2\pi = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{\eta} $$ **Step 5 — Solving for the required torque $T$:** $$ T = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{2\pi \cdot \eta} $$ **Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\,T = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{2\pi \cdot \eta}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor $2\pi$ converts one full revolution (a topological unit, dimensionless) into the angular displacement in radians. **Forgetting this factor is the single most common error** in KTU ball-screw problems. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workspace Volume Comparison (Cartesian vs. Spherical) A design engineer must choose between a **Cartesian (linear)** robot and an **Articulated (spherical/circular)** robot for a pick-and-place task. Both robots have the *same maximum reach* $R$ from the base. The Cartesian robot strokes are $L_X = L_Y = L_Z = R$, and the articulated robot has a spherical work envelope of radius $R$. **Step 1 — Cartesian workspace volume (rectangular parallelepiped):** $$ V_C = L_X \cdot L_Y \cdot L_Z = R \cdot R \cdot R = R^3 $$ **Step 2 — Spherical workspace volume (full sphere of radius $R$):** $$ V_S = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \approx 4.1888 \, R^3 $$ **Step 3 — Ratio of the two volumes:** $$ \dfrac{V_S}{V_C} = \dfrac{(4/3)\pi R^3}{R^3} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \approx 4.189 $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** For the same *reach* $R$, the articulated circular configuration provides a workspace approximately **4.19 times larger** in volume than the Cartesian configuration. However, the Cartesian configuration fills its box-shaped workspace *uniformly and rigidly*, whereas the spherical configuration has a *thin, hollow* usable region near the centre (because the wrist and elbow joints cannot fold to zero length). **Step 5 — Engineering correction (practical ratio):** Subtracting the unreachable inner sphere of radius $r_{\text{min}}$ (the *envelope hole*): $$ V_{S,\text{net}} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(R_{\max}^3 - R_{\min}^3\right) $$ $$ \dfrac{V_{S,\text{net}}}{V_C} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\max}^3 - R_{\min}^3}{R^3} $$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation is the **single most-asked** derivation in KTU 2022–2024 ESE papers on this module. Memorize the ratio **4.19** and the *net-envelope correction*. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SCARA Forward Kinematics (Planar 2R Sub-chain) A SCARA robot has two parallel rotary joints in the horizontal plane with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. The base is at the origin. **Step 1 — Position of the elbow joint (joint 2):** $$ x_2 = L_1 \cos\theta_1 \quad ; \quad y_2 = L_1 \sin\theta_1 $$ **Step 2 — Position of the end-effector (joint 3) relative to joint 2:** $$ \Delta x = L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \quad ; \quad \Delta y = L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ **Step 3 — Global end-effector coordinates (superposition of vectors):** $$ x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ $$ y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ **Step 4 — Verification using the law of cosines for the radial distance:** $$ r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inverse kinematics** (KTU-level extension) is obtained by isolating $\theta_2$ first: > $$\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}$$ > and then $\theta_1$ via the atan2 function. Always state the *elbow-up* and *elbow-down* multiplicity — examiners award 1 mark for recognizing the **two-branch solution**. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Linear vs. Circular) | Evaluation Criterion | Linear Configuration (Cartesian) | Circular Configuration (Articulated / SCARA) | |----------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------------------| | **Work-envelope shape** | Rectangular parallelepiped | Sphere, cylinder, or torus | | **Typical DOF** | 3 prismatic (PPP) | 3 revolute + 1 prismatic (RRP) | | **Stiffness / Rigidity** | Very high (linear guides + ball screws) | Moderate (joint compliance stacks) | | **Repeatability** | ± 0.01 – 0.05 mm | ± 0.02 – 0.10 mm | | **Footprint vs. Reach** | Large footprint for moderate reach | Compact footprint, large reach | | **Control complexity** | Low (decoupled axes) | High (coupled, requires IK solver) | | **Payload capability** | High (up to 1000 kg) | Moderate (typically ≤ 50 kg for arms) | | **Cost per axis** | Higher (precision linear components) | Lower (rotary motors are commodity) | | **Best-fit applications** | CNC, 3D printing, CMM, pick & place over conveyors | Welding, painting, assembly, palletizing | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Linear Configuration Architecture The following Mermaid block illustrates the *functional signal and power flow* of a typical linear (Cartesian) automated machine, from command input to the end-effector at the toolpoint. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Host PLC / CNC Controller] --> B[Trajectory Planner] B --> C[Joint Space Interpolator] C --> D1[Servo Drive X axis] C --> D2[Servo Drive Y axis] C --> D3[Servo Drive Z axis] D1 --> E1[Ball Screw X] D2 --> E2[Ball Screw Y] D3 --> E3[Ball Screw Z] E1 --> F1[Linear Guide X] E2 --> F2[Linear Guide Y] E3 --> F3[Linear Guide Z] F1 --> G[X Carriage] F2 --> H[Y Carriage] F3 --> I[Z Slider] G --> J[End Effector Toolpoint] H --> J I --> J J --> K[Feedback Linear Encoder] K --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > The **closed feedback loop** (K → A) is what makes the configuration *servo-controlled*. Open-loop stepper-driven linear stages are cheaper but lack the repeatability required for KTU-level "automated machine" definitions. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Circular Configuration Architecture The following diagram captures the serial kinematic chain of an articulated / SCARA-type circular mechanism. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Task Space Command x y z] --> B[Inverse Kinematics Solver] B --> C[Joint Angle Vector theta1 theta2 theta3] C --> D1[Servo Drive J1] C --> D2[Servo Drive J2] C --> D3[Servo Drive J3] D1 --> E1[Harmonic Reducer J1] D2 --> E2[Harmonic Reducer J2] D3 --> E3[Cycloidal Reducer J3] E1 --> F1[Link 1 Shoulder] E2 --> F2[Link 2 Elbow] E3 --> F3[Link 3 Wrist] F1 --> G[End Effector] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Encoder Feedback theta1 theta2 theta3] H --> A ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Choosing the Right Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Design Brief] --> Q1{Workspace shape rectangular?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Payload greater than 100 kg?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Task in confined cell?} Q2 -- Yes --> L1[Choose Linear: Gantry or Cartesian] Q2 -- No --> L2[Choose Linear: Truss or H gantry] Q3 -- Yes --> C1[Choose Circular: Articulated 6 axis] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Planar assembly task?} Q4 -- Yes --> C2[Choose Circular: SCARA 4 axis] Q4 -- No --> C3[Choose Circular: Cylindrical or Delta] L1 --> End[Final Selection] L2 --> End C1 --> End C2 --> End C3 --> End ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The above flow is a **KTU-recommended decision tree** that examiners expect students to reproduce (in a simplified 3-branch form) in the design section of a 14-mark question. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a *linear configuration* of an automated machine. List any **two** rotary-to-linear conversion mechanisms used in such configurations. **Model Answer:** A *linear configuration* is a kinematic arrangement in which the end-effector translates along one or more straight-line paths, typically aligned with orthogonal Cartesian axes. The configuration is built from prismatic joints (P-joints) driven by rotary actuators through rotary-to-linear converters. *Two conversion mechanisms:* 1. **Ball screw / Lead screw** — converts rotation into linear motion via helical thread engagement. 2. **Rack and pinion** — converts rotation into linear motion via gear meshing with a straight toothed bar. *(Acceptable alternative: belt-pulley drive, linear motor, chain drive.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the *work envelope* of a circular configuration robot? Give the expression for the envelope volume of a *fully spherical* configuration. **Model Answer:** The *work envelope* of a robot is the locus of all points in 3D space that the end-effector (or tool centre point, TCP) can reach, given the joint limits and link lengths. For a fully spherical (ideal articulated) configuration with maximum reach $R$ and minimum reach $r$, the envelope volume is: $$ V_{sph} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(R^3 - r^3\right) $$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **one full question** from each module pair. Either choice below is a valid 14-mark attempt. --- #### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Q.A (a)** A Cartesian (linear) robot must lift a payload of **F = 500 N** using a ball screw of **pitch p = 5 mm/rev** and overall drive efficiency **η = 0.90**. The servo motor is coupled directly to the screw (no gearbox). Calculate: (i) the **torque** required at the motor shaft, and (ii) the **linear speed** of the nut when the motor runs at **1500 rpm**. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Torque calculation:** We use the standard lead-screw torque equation derived in §3.1: $$ T = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{2\pi \cdot \eta} $$ Substituting $F = 500 \text{ N}$, $p = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.90$: $$ T = \dfrac{500 \times 0.005}{2\pi \times 0.90} $$ $$ T = \dfrac{2.5}{5.6549} \approx 0.4421 \text{ N·m} $$ $$ \boxed{T \approx 0.442 \text{ N·m}} $$ **(ii) Linear speed calculation:** Rotational frequency in rev/s: $f = 1500 / 60 = 25 \text{ rev/s}$. $$ v = f \cdot p = 25 \times 0.005 = 0.125 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ \boxed{v = 0.125 \text{ m/s} = 125 \text{ mm/s}} $$ **Q.A (b)** Compare **linear and circular configurations** of automated machines under the following heads: (i) work-envelope shape, (ii) rigidity and repeatability, (iii) control complexity, and (iv) typical industrial applications. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer (tabulated):** | Head | Linear Configuration | Circular Configuration | |------|----------------------|------------------------| | (i) Work envelope | Rectangular parallelepiped (box-shaped) | Cylinder / sphere / torus | | (ii) Rigidity & repeatability | Very high rigidity, ± 0.01 – 0.05 mm | Moderate, ± 0.02 – 0.10 mm | | (iii) Control complexity | Low — decoupled single-axis control | High — coupled, needs IK solver | | (iv) Typical applications | CNC machining, 3D printing, CMM, wafer handling | Welding, painting, assembly, palletizing, surgical robotics | > **Concluding remark (1 mark):** Linear configurations are preferred when the workspace is *box-shaped* and rigidity is paramount; circular configurations are preferred when the workspace is *curved* and dexterity is paramount. --- #### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Q.B (a)** A SCARA robot has two links of length **L₁ = 300 mm** and **L₂ = 250 mm**. The current joint angles are **θ₁ = 30°** and **θ₂ = -45°**. Using the SCARA forward-kinematic equations, determine the **(x, y) position** of the end-effector in the base frame. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** Using the planar 2R forward-kinematic equations (derived in §3.3): $$ x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ $$ y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ **Step 1 — Compute the sum of joint angles:** $$ \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 30° + (-45°) = -15° $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$ x = 300 \cos 30° + 250 \cos(-15°) $$ $$ x = 300 \times 0.8660 + 250 \times 0.9659 $$ $$ x = 259.81 + 241.48 = 501.29 \text{ mm} $$ $$ y = 300 \sin 30° + 250 \sin(-15°) $$ $$ y = 300 \times 0.5000 + 250 \times (-0.2588) $$ $$ y = 150.00 - 64.70 = 85.30 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Final result:** $$ \boxed{(x, y) \approx (501.3 \text{ mm}, \ 85.3 \text{ mm})} $$ **Step 4 — Verification via radial distance:** $$ r = \sqrt{501.29^2 + 85.30^2} = \sqrt{251292 + 7276} \approx 508.4 \text{ mm} $$ This is consistent with $L_1 + L_2 = 550 \text{ mm}$ being the *maximum* radial reach, so the result is geometrically valid. **Q.B (b)** Derive the **workspace volume ratio** of a fully spherical (circular) configuration to a Cartesian (linear) configuration of equal maximum reach **R**. State the *practical correction* for the inner unreachable sphere. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** *(The derivation matches §3.2 step-by-step; the model answer is reproduced compactly here for exam-time reference.)* **Step 1 — Cartesian volume:** $V_C = R^3$. **Step 2 — Spherical volume:** $V_S = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$. **Step 3 — Ratio:** $\dfrac{V_S}{V_C} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \approx 4.19$. **Step 4 — Practical correction:** The articulated robot cannot reach a sphere of radius $r_{\min}$ around the base (link-length limit). The *net* usable volume is: $$ V_{S,\text{net}} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(R_{\max}^3 - R_{\min}^3\right) $$ **Step 5 — Corrected ratio:** $$ \dfrac{V_{S,\text{net}}}{V_C} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\max}^3 - R_{\min}^3}{R^3} $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** The spherical configuration nominally offers a workspace ~4.19× larger than a Cartesian one of equal reach, but the *real* advantage is reduced when the inner dead zone is accounted for. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing mm and m in the lead-screw torque formula. Always convert pitch to *metres* before substitution. > 2. **Forgetting the 2π factor** — students often write $T = Fp/\eta$ instead of $T = Fp/(2\pi\eta)$. This loses **2 marks** instantly. > 3. **Confusing reach (R) with stroke (L)** — see warning in §2.4. > 4. **Ignoring efficiency η** — in the absence of stated efficiency, KTU assumes **η = 1.0 (ideal)**, not 0.90. Read the question carefully. > 5. **Single-branch IK answer** — for SCARA inverse kinematics, always mention both *elbow-up* and *elbow-down* solutions to earn full marks. > 6. **Linear vs. circular label mix-up** — a SCARA robot is *circular* in horizontal plane and *linear* (prismatic) in vertical axis. State this hybrid nature explicitly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Linear configuration** = prismatic joints (PPP); **circular configuration** = revolute joints (RRR / RRP). - Cartesian workspace is a **rectangular box** of volume $L_X L_Y L_Z$. - Spherical (circular) workspace is a **sphere** of volume $\tfrac{4}{3}\pi (R_{\max}^3 - R_{\min}^3)$. - Spherical / Cartesian volume ratio for equal reach ≈ **4.19** (ideal); less in practice. - Lead-screw torque formula: $T = \dfrac{Fp}{2\pi\eta}$ — *memorize with units.* - Linear velocity from lead screw: $v = f \cdot p$ — frequency $f$ in rev/s. - SCARA forward kinematics: $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$. - Centripetal acceleration $a_c = \omega^2 r$ governs the *dynamic payload limit* of circular mechanisms. - Linear configs dominate in **CNC, 3D printing, CMM**; circular configs dominate in **welding, painting, assembly, palletizing**. - **Hybrid configurations** (e.g., SCARA, cylindrical) combine prismatic and revolute joints — they are exam favourites. - **Repeatability hierarchy (best → worst):** Cartesian > SCARA > Cylindrical > Articulated > Spherical. - Always state **units** in the final answer line — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit in a 7-mark sub-question. - Remember the **4-step analytical framework** for any configuration question: *(i) identify joints → (ii) compute work envelope → (iii) select drive → (iv) state application.*

topics.title: Non-Industrial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Industrial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to **ISO 8373:2021**, an *industrial robot* is defined as an "automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications." Consequently, any robot that does **not** strictly satisfy these criteria for use in industrial automation falls under the umbrella of **Non-Industrial Robots**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT743)** framework, **Non-Industrial Robots** are formally defined as **reprogrammable, multifunctional mechanical devices designed to perform useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial manufacturing automation processes**. They typically operate in **unstructured, dynamic, human-shared environments**, and their mechanism design is dictated by mobility, safety, dexterity, and human-robot interaction rather than by precision and repeatability alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Non-Industrial Robots):**
> The focus is on understanding *why* mechanism design choices differ from industrial arms, classifying the wide variety of non-industrial robotic systems, and identifying the kinematic and dynamic constraints that govern them. This forms the design-thinking foundation for the rest of the course.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Factory Worker vs. The Household Helper"**
> Imagine two helpers:
> - The **Factory Worker (Industrial Robot)** stands at one spot on an assembly line, repeats the same 1,000 welds a day with sub-millimetre accuracy, never moves, and never speaks to anyone.
> - The **Household Helper (Non-Industrial Robot)**, like a robot vacuum or a hospital assistant, must *navigate* around furniture, *sense* people, *avoid* stairs, *grip* varied objects (a spoon, a syringe, an apple), and *cooperate* safely with humans.
>
> **Non-industrial robots = mobility + perception + safety + adaptability**, traded off against the extreme repeatability demanded on a factory floor.

## 1.3 Standardised Metrics and Benchmark Constants

The following **standard metrics** govern the classification and benchmarking of non-industrial robots as per **ISO 9283** and **ISO 13482** (personal care robots):

- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** Typically **4 to 40+** for humanoids, 2–6 for wheeled/hospital robots.
- **Payload-to-Weight Ratio:** Often **1:10 to 1:50** (humanoids) versus **1:3 to 1:1** for industrial arms.
- **Operating Speed:** **0.25 m/s to 3 m/s** for service robots (capped by safety), versus 10 m/s+ tip speeds for industrial arms.
- **Positional Repeatability:** **±1 mm to ±50 mm** (mobile/outdoor) versus **±0.02 mm** (industrial).
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Industry benchmark of **10,000 to 50,000 hours** for service robots.

## 1.4 Primary Categories of Non-Industrial Robots

| Category | Primary Environment | Typical DoF | Prime Design Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| Service Robots | Indoor human spaces | 4 – 7 | Safety + Navigation |
| Medical / Surgical Robots | Operating theatres | 6 – 10 | Precision + Sterility |
| Mobile Autonomous (AGV/AMR) | Warehouses, hospitals | 2 – 4 wheel + sensor | Path planning |
| Humanoid / Bipedal | Human-centric spaces | 25 – 40 | Balance + Dexterity |
| Aerial (UAV/Drone) | Open airspace | 4 – 8 (rotors) | Stability + Payload |
| Underwater (ROV/AUV) | Marine environment | 4 – 7 | Pressure sealing |
| Space Robots (Rovers/Arms) | Extra-terrestrial | 6 – 9 | Ruggedisation |
| Agricultural Robots | Farms, orchards | 4 – 8 | Terrain adaptation |
| Defence / EOD | Hazardous zones | 5 – 7 | Tele-operation + Survivability |
| Educational / Recreational | Labs, homes | 2 – 6 | Cost + Modularity |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Operational Breakdown

The mechanism design philosophy of non-industrial robots is governed by **four non-negotiable pillars** which differ fundamentally from industrial-robot design:

### Pillar 1 — Mobility & Locomotion
Unlike a fixed-base industrial arm, non-industrial robots generally require an *entire body* capable of locomotion. The choice of mechanism (wheels, legs, tracks, rotors, fins) is dictated by the **terrain mechanics** of the operating environment.

### Pillar 2 — Perception Coupling
Mechanisms must be designed as **sensor-rich structures**. The chassis, links, and joints must accommodate cameras, LiDAR, IMU, force/torque sensors, and tactile skins without disturbing the mechanical dynamic balance.

### Pillar 3 — Safe Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)
Per **ISO/TS 15066** for collaborative robots, every link must either be **inherently safe** (rounded, padded, compliant), **force-limited** (collaborative-mode speed < **250 mm/s**), or **separated by distance**.

### Pillar 4 — Energy Autonomy
Most non-industrial robots are battery-driven. Energy density of modern Li-ion is roughly **250 Wh/kg**, directly limiting payload, mechanism weight, and operating duration.

## 2.2 Classification Logic Flow

A hierarchical reasoning approach for KTU exam purposes:

1. **Is the robot fixed or mobile?** → Fixed → manipulator-class; Mobile → locomotion-class.
2. **Is the environment structured or unstructured?** → Structured (warehouse) → AMR with simple SLAM; Unstructured (forest, battlefield) → legged / aerial / tracked.
3. **Does it physically contact humans in shared space?** → Yes → Cobot-class compliance + ISO 10218 + ISO/TS 15066.
4. **Is the task high-precision (e.g., surgery) or high-coverage (e.g., agriculture)?** → Determines DoF vs. workspace trade-off.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Equations Governing Non-Industrial Robot Mechanism Design**

| # | Equation / Relation | LaTeX Form | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kutzbach–Grübler DoF | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Mobility (DoF) of a planar mechanism | dimensionless |
| 2 | Spatial Mobility | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | DoF in 3-D spatial linkage | dimensionless |
| 3 | Payload-to-Weight Ratio | $\eta_p = \dfrac{m_{\text{payload}}}{m_{\text{robot}}}$ | Mechanical efficiency indicator | dimensionless |
| 4 | Static Tip Force (3-DOF arm) | $F_{\text{tip}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta}{L_{\text{link}}}$ | Max payload force at gripper | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | Differential-Drive Kinematics | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \dfrac{r}{L_b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Wheeled robot linear/angular velocity | m/s, rad/s |
| 6 | Quadcopter Hover Thrust | $T_{\text{total}} = 4\,T_{\text{motor}} = m\,g$ | Vertical equilibrium condition | Newtons (N) |
| 7 | Static Stability Margin (SSM) | $\text{SSM} = \min \vert x_{\text{CoM}} - x_{\text{support}} \vert$ | Anti-tipping measure for legged robots | metres (m) |
| 8 | Biped Zero-Moment Point (ZMP) | $x_{\text{ZMP}} = \dfrac{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \sum_i m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | Balance criterion for humanoid walking | metres (m) |
| 9 | Workspace Volume (Spherical Wrist) | $V_w = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi\, r_{\max}^3$ | Approximate reachable sphere volume | m³ |
| 10 | Battery Endurance | $t_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}\,\eta_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$ | Mission duration estimate | hours (h) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Note:** Absolute-value bars in the SSM formula above are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax of the table row.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Field:** The Da Vinci surgical robot uses a 7-DoF *remote-centre-of-motion* mechanism — a non-industrial kinematic chain optimised for **dexterity inside a confined incision**.
- **Agriculture:** The *John Deere See & Spray* robot uses a gantry + boom mechanism with computer-vision-guided nozzles, reducing herbicide by **77 %**.
- **Disaster Response:** *Spot* (Boston Dynamics) — 12-DoF quadruped mechanism with 3-DoF per leg, designed for **unstructured terrain traversal**.
- **Logistics:** Amazon's *Kiva/Hercules* AMR uses a **differential-drive** base with a lifting-conveyor mechanism — the textbook example of mobile mechanism integration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — DoF of a Quadruped Robot Leg (Spatial Mechanism)

We will compute the mobility of a single leg of a quadruped robot such as *ANYmal* or *Spot*. Each leg is modelled as a **spatial serial chain** with the following topology (counting from hip to foot):

- **Link 1:** Hip abduction/adduction joint (revolute)
- **Link 2:** Hip flexion/extension joint (revolute)
- **Link 3:** Knee flexion joint (revolute)

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Links and Joints

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \text{number of links (including ground)} = 4 \\
j_1 &= \text{number of full-revolute (R) joints} = 3 \\
j_2 &= \text{number of half joints / links} = 0 \\
j_3 &= \text{number of lower pairs with 2 DoF} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each leg has 3 rigid links connected in series plus 1 imaginary "ground" link, hence $n = 4$. The three joints are all single-axis revolute (R) joints.

### Step 2 — Apply the Spatial Kutzbach–Grübler Equation

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(4 - 1) - 5(3) - 4(0) - 3(0) \\
  &= 6(3) - 15 \\
  &= 18 - 15 \\
  &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

The leg therefore possesses exactly **3 active DoF** — one each for hip abduction, hip flexion, and knee flexion. **This matches the kinematic design of Spot and ANYmal, validating the formula.** A redundant leg would require **4 revolute joints**, giving $M = 4$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Differential-Drive Mobile Robot Forward Kinematics

Consider a two-wheeled differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L_b$ (distance between wheels), and right/left wheel angular velocities $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$.

### Step 1 — Define Contact-Point Linear Velocity of Each Wheel

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_R &= r\,\omega_R \\
v_L &= r\,\omega_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each wheel rolls without slipping, so its contact point with the ground moves linearly at speed equal to radius times angular velocity.

### Step 2 — Compute the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)

The robot rotates about a point on the line connecting both wheels. The forward velocity $v$ of the chassis centre and angular velocity $\omega$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \\
\omega &= \frac{v_R - v_L}{L_b} = \frac{r}{L_b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify with a Numerical Example

Let $r = 0.05$ m, $L_b = 0.30$ m, $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{0.05}{2}(10 + 10) = 0.5\ \text{m/s} \\
\omega &= \frac{0.05}{0.30}(10 - 10) = 0\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Pure forward translation at **0.5 m/s** — physically consistent (wheels identical speed, no turning).

Now let $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{0.05}{2}(12 + 8) = 0.5\ \text{m/s} \\
\omega &= \frac{0.05}{0.30}(12 - 8) = 0.667\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The robot moves forward at **0.5 m/s** while turning at **0.667 rad/s** — the classical differential-drive behaviour.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Industrial vs Non-Industrial Robots

| Design Parameter | Industrial Robot (e.g., KUKA KR 16) | Service Robot (e.g., Pepper) | Medical Robot (Da Vinci) | Mobile Robot (TurtleBot) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DoF | 6 | 4 | 7 | 2 |
| Payload | **16 kg** | **0.5 kg** (tablet) | **0 kg** (master handles) | **5 kg** |
| Repeatability | **±0.05 mm** | **±5 mm** | **±0.1 mm** (scaled) | **±50 mm** (odometry) |
| Speed | **2 m/s** | **0.3 m/s** | **0.05 m/s** | **0.65 m/s** |
| Environment | Caged, structured | Open, human-shared | Sterile OR | Floor, dynamic |
| Primary Sensor | Encoders | Cameras + IMU | Force + Vision | LiDAR + IMU |
| Power | Mains 3-phase | Battery 24 V | Mains + UPS | Battery 12 V |
| Safety Standard | ISO 10218 | ISO 13482 | IEC 80601 | ISO 3691-4 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
