> (2) When asked to "compare", marks are awarded **per row** of a comparison matrix. Students who write paragraphs instead of bullet-matrix lose structure marks.
> (3) In numerical problems on breakeven volume, students forget to **state the decision rule** explicitly (i.e., "if N > N\*, choose automation") and lose 1 mark.
> (4) Confusing *programmable* with *flexible* is the single biggest differentiator KTU examiners test. Always mention the **stoppage** keyword for programmable and **real-time** keyword for flexible.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the architecture of an automated production line. Discuss its main components and the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer is comparing manual production with a fixed automation transfer line for a precision gear. Given:

- Manual setup cost: ₹ 80,000
- Automation fixed cost: ₹ 22,00,000
- Manual variable cost: ₹ 250/unit
- Automation variable cost: ₹ 50/unit
- Annual demand: 12,000 units
- Expected life: 7 years

Determine the **breakeven volume**, decide whether automation is justified, and compute the **payback period**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

An automated production line consists of a series of workstations connected by an automated material-handling system, with each station performing a specific processing, assembly, or inspection operation.

**Components:**

1. **Workstations / Process Machines** — perform the actual transformation (e.g., CNC mills, robotic welders).
2. **Material Handling System** — transfer parts between stations (e.g., conveyors, AGVs, gantry robots).
3. **Transfer Mechanism** — physically indexes parts (e.g., pallet conveyor, rotary dial indexer).
4. **Control System** — PLCs, CNC controllers, SCADA coordinating machine cycles.
5. **Sensors & Feedback Devices** — limit switches, vision systems, encoders verifying part presence and quality.
6. **Loading/Unloading Station** — infeed and outfeed stations, often robotic.

A labelled sketch would show: `Raw Infeed → Station 1 → Transfer → Station 2 → Transfer → Inspection → Outfeed`, with arrows back to the central PLC.

*(Draw a labelled block diagram showing the six components above. Full marks require a labelled diagram and explanation of one functional role per block.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate Breakeven Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^* &= \frac{C_{FA} - C_{MA}}{C_{MU} - C_{FU}} \\
&= \frac{22{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000}{250 - 50} \\
&= \frac{21{,}20{,}000}{200} \\
&= 10{,}600 \;\text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decision Rule**

Annual demand = 12,000 units, which exceeds $N^* = 10{,}600$ units. **Therefore, fixed automation is economically justified.**

**Step 3 — Compute Annual Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= (C_{MU} - C_{FU}) \cdot N \\
&= (250 - 50) \cdot 12{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot 12{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹} \, 24{,}00{,}000 \;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Net Investment**

$$
\Delta I = C_{FA} - C_{MA} = 22{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = \text{₹} \, 21{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Payback Period**

$$
P = \frac{\Delta I}{\Delta S} = \frac{21{,}20{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.883 \;\text{years} \approx 10.6 \;\text{months}
$$

**Conclusion:** The firm should invest in fixed automation. The investment recovers in less than 11 months, well within the 7-year equipment life.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 1, Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Classify the **three approaches to automating manufacturing processes** with neat examples. Construct a comparison matrix covering: production volume, flexibility, investment, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **levels of automation** in a manufacturing enterprise as per Groover's automation pyramid. How does each level integrate with the others? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The three approaches are:

1. **Fixed (Hard) Automation:** Equipment designed for one product family with no possibility of changeover. *Example:* automotive engine block transfer line.
2. **Programmable Automation:** Equipment whose sequence of operations is controlled by software; changeover requires reprogramming and production stoppage. *Example:* CNC milling centre, PLC-controlled robot cell.
3. **Flexible (Soft) Automation:** Extension of programmable automation in which the system can reconfigure on-the-fly without stopping. *Example:* Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS), CIM cell.

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Criterion | Fixed | Programmable | Flexible |
|-----------|-------|--------------|----------|
| Production Volume | Very High | Medium | Medium-Low |
| Flexibility | None | Batch-level | Real-time |
| Investment | Very High | High | Very High |
| Typical Application | IC fab, bottling | Job shop, batch machining | Mixed-model assembly |

*(3 marks for classification + examples; 4 marks for the comparison matrix with all four rows.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Groover's Automation Pyramid (6 levels):**

1. **Level 1 — Device Level:** Sensors (limit, proximity, vision) and actuators (motors, pneumatic cylinders). These are the building blocks that interface with the physical world.
2. **Level 2 — Machine Level:** CNC controllers, PLCs controlling individual machines using feedback from Level 1 devices.
3. **Level 3 — Cell/Workstation Level:** Multiple machines coordinated by a cell controller, typically with a robot. Performs an integrated sequence of operations.
4. **Level 4 — Department/FMS Level:** Multiple cells coordinated via DNC and material-handling systems (AGVs, conveyors). Includes AS/RS for inventory.
5. **Level 5 — Factory/CIM Level:** All departments integrated through an MES (Manufacturing Execution System), linking CAD/CAM/CAPP/QAS.
6. **Level 6 — Enterprise Level:** ERP and supply-chain integration linking the factory to customers and suppliers — the foundation of Industry 4.0.

**Integration:**
- Each upper level **commands** the level below through digital protocols (e.g., OPC-UA, MQTT, Ethernet/IP).
- Each lower level **feeds back** real-time production data (OEE, cycle times, alarms) to the level above.
- The pyramid is **closed-loop**: enterprise decisions (Level 6) translate to shop-floor commands (Level 2) within minutes, enabling true CIM.

*(2 marks for hierarchy; 3 marks for describing each level's role; 2 marks for explaining the bidirectional integration.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Page Before the Exam**

- ☐ **Three Approaches (the cornerstone):** Fixed, Programmable, Flexible.
- ☐ **Mnemonic:** *Fixed-Family*, *Programmable-Program*, *Flexible-Flow*.
- ☐ **Production Volume Sequence:** Fixed > Programmable > Flexible (typically).
- ☐ **Flexibility Sequence:** Flexible > Programmable > Fixed.
- ☐ **Changeover:** *None* for fixed; *stoppage required* for programmable; *no stoppage* for flexible.
- ☐ **Breakeven formula:** $N^* = \dfrac{C_{FA} - C_{MA}}{C_{MU} - C_{FU}}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- ☐ **Payback formula:** $P = \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta S}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- ☐ **Automation Pyramid:** Six levels — Device → Machine → Cell → FMS → Factory → Enterprise.
- ☐ **Industrial examples to remember for 14-mark answers:** IC fab (Fixed), CNC job shop (Programmable), BMW mixed-model line (Flexible).
- ☐ **Always end a numerical answer** with a one-line **decision rule** (justified/not justified).
- ☐ **Never confuse** "high variety" with "high volume" — variety drives flexibility choice; volume drives fixed choice.
- ☐ **CIM, FMS, DNC, AGV, AS/RS, MES, ERP** — know the full forms and one-line role of each.
- ☐ **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** is the modern extension of Level 6 enterprise integration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Drive Systems: Falling Weight, Inclined Belt, Spring Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Drive Systems: Falling Weight, Inclined Belt, Spring Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mechanical Drive Systems — Formal Definition

A **Mechanical Drive System** is a power-transmission arrangement that converts a primary energy input (gravitational, elastic, or frictional) into controlled mechanical motion at the output. In automated machinery, these systems form the *actuation backbone*, providing deterministic force, displacement, or torque profiles without requiring external electrical or hydraulic power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERUT743, Module 2):**
> *Mechanical drive systems* are categorized by their energy-storage and conversion principle. The three principal classes examined are:
> 1. **Gravitational Drives** — falling weight systems
> 2. **Frictional/Belt Drives** — inclined belt transmissions
> 3. **Elastic Potential Drives** — spring-driven mechanisms

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| System | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Falling Weight** | The weight hand of an antique grandfather clock | Gravity pulls the weight down at constant force; the descent unwinds a cord that drives the gear train |
| **Inclined Belt** | The walking treadmill or the luggage conveyor at an airport check-in counter | Belt inclined at angle θ carries the load upward against a component of gravity using friction between belt and pulley |
| **Spring System** | The wound-up mainspring of a wristwatch or the recoil of a mousetrap | Elastic potential energy is stored during winding and released gradually to produce motion |

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard engineering values** recur throughout this module:
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Hooke's Law constant:** $k$ measured in **N/m** (linear) or **N·mm** (torsional)
> - **Coefficient of friction (rubber belt on cast iron):** $\mu \approx 0.3$
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure** (for pneumo-mechanical hybrids): $101.325 \, \text{kPa}$

---

### 1.4 Why These Three Systems Are Grouped Together

All three belong to the family of **single-input, deterministic output** drives. Unlike servo-motors or hydraulic cylinders, they possess *no feedback loop* — the output is purely a function of geometry, mass, spring stiffness, and friction. This makes them **mathematically tractable** in the KTU board examination and **highly reliable** in low-cost automation (textile looms, packaging machines, time-keeping devices).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Falling Weight Drive System

#### 2.1.1 Principle of Operation

A mass $m$ suspended by a flexible cord wound around a drum of radius $R$ rotates the drum as it descends. The angular displacement $\theta$ of the drum and the linear displacement $x$ of the weight are kinematically coupled:

$$x = R\theta$$

#### 2.1.2 Force, Energy, and Power Analysis

- **Tractive force (constant):**
$$F = mg$$
- **Torque delivered to drum:**
$$T = mgR$$
- **Potential energy released over height $h$:**
$$E = mgh$$
- **Instantaneous power:**
$$P = mgv = \frac{mg \, dx}{dt}$$

#### 2.1.3 Governing Differential Equation

Applying Newton's second law (downward positive), with cord inextensible and no air drag:

$$m\ddot{x} = mg - F_{\text{load}}$$

For an ideal system (frictionless drum bearing) where $F_{\text{load}} = mg$ exactly balances inertia, the weight descends at **terminal velocity** $v_t$ — a property exploited in **escapement-regulated clocks**.

#### 2.1.4 Advantages and Limitations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's favorite KTU framing:** *"Why is a falling weight preferred over a coiled spring for tower clock drives?"*
> - **Answer cue:** Falling weight provides **constant force** over its full travel, whereas spring force decays linearly with deflection ($F = k(R_0 - R\theta)$ for spiral springs). Constant torque means simpler gear-train design.

---

### 2.2 Inclined Belt Drive System

#### 2.2.1 Geometry and Setup

Two pulleys of diameters $D_1$ (driver) and $D_2$ (driven) are mounted on parallel shafts separated by center distance $C$. The belt is wrapped over both pulleys and inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. The system is essentially a **flat-belt or V-belt drive** with an additional gravitational load component.

#### 2.2.2 Velocity Ratio and Slip

$$\text{V.R.} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} (1 - s)$$

where $s$ is the slip fraction (typically $0.01$ to $0.03$ for flat belts, $0.015$ to $0.05$ for V-belts).

#### 2.2.3 Belt Length (Open Belt, KTU Standard Formula)

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$$

For a **crossed belt:**

$$L_{\text{cross}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}$$

#### 2.2.4 Tension Analysis (Eytelwein's Formula)

The ratio of tight-side to slack-side tension is governed by the **angle of wrap** $\theta_w$ and coefficient of friction $\mu$:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_w}$$

For an open belt, the wrap angle on the smaller pulley is:

$$\theta_w = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_1 - D_2}{2C}\right)$$

#### 2.2.5 Inclination Effect

When the belt runs up an incline at angle $\alpha$, the effective load on the driven pulley increases by a factor of $\cos\alpha$ in the normal direction. The maximum transmissible power is:

$$P_{\max} = (T_1 - T_2) v_b \cos\alpha$$

where $v_b$ is belt linear velocity.

---

### 2.3 Spring Drive Systems

#### 2.3.1 Linear Spring (Hooke's Law)

$$F = k x$$

- **Strain energy stored:**
$$U = \frac{1}{2} k x^2 = \frac{F^2}{2k}$$
- **Spring constant for helical springs (Wahl's correction):**
$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$
where $G$ = shear modulus, $d$ = wire diameter, $D$ = mean coil diameter, $n$ = number of active coils.

#### 2.3.2 Torsional Spring

$$T = K \theta$$

- **Energy stored:**
$$U = \frac{1}{2} K \theta^2$$
- **For a helical torsion spring:**
$$K = \frac{E d^4}{116.7 \, D n}$$

#### 2.3.3 Spiral (Flat) Spring — Watch Mainspring Type

Used in watches, clocks, and record-player turntables. The torque decreases linearly with angular unwinding:

$$T = K(\theta_f - \theta)$$

where $\theta_f$ is the fully wound deflection and $\theta$ is the unwound angle. **This linear torque decay is the spring analog of a falling weight's constant force.**

#### 2.3.4 Constant-Force Spring

A pre-stressed strip coiled on a drum. Delivers **constant force** $F_0$ over its full extension. Used in counterbalances, window sash balancers, and tensioning devices:

$$F_0 = \frac{E b t^3 \, \sigma_y}{2L}$$

where $b$ = width, $t$ = thickness, $L$ = free length, $\sigma_y$ = yield stress.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | System | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Falling Weight | $F = mg$ | $m$=mass, $g$=9.81 m/s² | N |
| 2 | Falling Weight | $T = mgR$ | $R$=drum radius | N·m |
| 3 | Falling Weight | $E = mgh$ | $h$=drop height | J |
| 4 | Belt Drive | $\text{V.R.} = D_2/D_1 \cdot (1-s)$ | $D$=diameters, $s$=slip | — |
| 5 | Belt Drive | $L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1+D_2) + \frac{(D_1-D_2)^2}{4C}$ | $C$=center distance | m |
| 6 | Belt Drive | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta_w}$ | $\mu$=friction, $\theta_w$=wrap angle | rad |
| 7 | Linear Spring | $F = kx$ | $k$=stiffness, $x$=deflection | N |
| 8 | Linear Spring | $U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ | — | J |
| 9 | Helical Spring | $k = Gd^4/(8D^3n)$ | $G$=shear modulus | N/m |
| 10 | Torsional Spring | $T = K\theta$ | $K$=torsional stiffness | N·m/rad |
| 11 | Torsional Spring | $U = \frac{1}{2}K\theta^2$ | — | J |
| 12 | Spiral Spring | $T = K(\theta_f - \theta)$ | $\theta_f$=full wind angle | N·m |
| 13 | Constant-Force | $F_0 = \frac{Ebt^3\sigma_y}{2L}$ | $b,t,L$ = strip dimensions | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING RULE:** In the above formulas, all absolute-value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) have been replaced with $\vert$ and $\mid$ notation. The vertical pipe character $\vert$ is never used raw to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Falling Weight: Energy Balance and Drum Kinematics

**Problem statement:** A mass $m = 50 \, \text{kg}$ falls a distance $h = 2 \, \text{m}$ while unwinding a cord from a drum of radius $R = 0.2 \, \text{m}$. The drum is rigidly coupled to a gear of pitch radius $r_g = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ which drives a pinion of pitch radius $r_p = 0.02 \, \text{m}$. Find (a) the total energy delivered, (b) the total angular rotation of the pinion, and (c) the average torque transmitted at the pinion, assuming 100% efficiency.

#### Step 1 — Identify the energy released by the falling weight

The potential energy released as the weight descends $h$ meters under gravity is:

$$E_{\text{released}} = m g h$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$E_{\text{released}} = 50 \times 9.81 \times 2$$

$$E_{\text{released}} = 981 \, \text{J}$$

#### Step 2 — Determine the angular rotation of the drum

Since the cord is inextensible, the linear cord length unwound equals the linear drop of the weight. The angular rotation of the drum is therefore:

$$\theta_{\text{drum}} = \frac{h}{R}$$

$$\theta_{\text{drum}} = \frac{2}{0.2} = 10 \, \text{rad}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the gear-train velocity ratio to find pinion rotation

The gear ratio (gear drives pinion) is:

$$G = \frac{r_g}{r_p} = \frac{0.05}{0.02} = 2.5$$

This means the pinion rotates **2.5 times faster** than the gear. Hence:

$$\theta_{\text{pinion}} = G \cdot \theta_{\text{drum}} = 2.5 \times 10 = 25 \, \text{rad}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the average torque on the pinion using energy conservation

Energy delivered to pinion = Energy released by weight (100% efficiency assumed):

$$T_{\text{pinion,avg}} \cdot \theta_{\text{pinion}} = mgh$$

$$T_{\text{pinion,avg}} = \frac{mgh}{\theta_{\text{pinion}}}$$

$$T_{\text{pinion,avg}} = \frac{981}{25} = 39.24 \, \text{N·m}$$

#### Step 5 — Verify with torque-ratio method

Drum torque = $mgR = 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 = 98.1 \, \text{N·m}$

Ideal gear-train torque transmission: $T_{\text{pinion}} = T_{\text{drum}} \cdot G = 98.1 \times 2.5 = 245.25 \, \text{N·m}$ at the *gear* side. **Correction:** For a gear reducing speed from drum-side to pinion-side, the pinion torque is:

$$T_{\text{pinion}} = T_{\text{drum}} \cdot \frac{1}{G} \cdot \eta$$

For $\eta = 1$ and reduction (drum→gear→pinion, both speed-up):

$$T_{\text{pinion}} = \frac{98.1}{2.5} = 39.24 \, \text{N·m} \;\;\checkmark$$

> The two methods reconcile, validating the energy balance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inclined Belt: Tension and Power Transmission

**Problem statement:** A flat belt $150 \, \text{mm}$ wide runs over two pulleys, $D_1 = 300 \, \text{mm}$ (driver) at $1440 \, \text{rpm}$ and $D_2 = 600 \, \text{mm}$ (driven). Center distance $C = 1.5 \, \text{m}$, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$, belt mass $2.5 \, \text{kg/m}$, belt is inclined at $\alpha = 20°$ carrying a load of $W = 800 \, \text{N}$. Find (a) belt linear speed, (b) wrap angle on smaller pulley, (c) ratio of tensions, and (d) maximum power transmissible.

#### Step 1 — Compute belt linear velocity

$$v_b = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.3 \times 1440}{60}$$

$$v_b = 22.62 \, \text{m/s}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute wrap angle on smaller pulley (open belt)

$$\theta_w = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right)$$

$$\theta_w = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.6 - 0.3}{2 \times 1.5}\right)$$

$$\theta_w = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.3}{3.0}\right) = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}(0.1)$$

$$\theta_w = 3.1416 - 2(0.1002) = 2.9412 \, \text{rad} = 168.5°$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Eytelwein's formula for tension ratio

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_w} = e^{0.3 \times 2.9412} = e^{0.8824}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 2.416$$

#### Step 4 — Determine maximum tension from centrifugal effect

The effective centrifugal tension in the belt is:

$$T_c = m_b v_b^2 = 2.5 \times (22.62)^2 = 1279.4 \, \text{N}$$

Maximum allowable tight-side tension (assumed allowable working tension $T_1^{\text{max}} = 2500 \, \text{N}$):

$$T_1^{\text{max,eff}} = T_1^{\text{max}} - T_c = 2500 - 1279.4 = 1220.6 \, \text{N}$$

#### Step 5 — Compute maximum power transmissible

$$T_1 - T_2 = T_1 - \frac{T_1}{2.416} = T_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2.416}\right) = 0.586 \, T_1$$

$$P_{\max} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v_b \cdot \cos\alpha$$

$$P_{\max} = 0.586 \times 1220.6 \times 22.62 \times \cos 20°$$

$$P_{\max} = 0.586 \times 1220.6 \times 22.62 \times 0.9397$$

$$P_{\max} = 15\,071 \, \text{W} \approx 15.07 \, \text{kW}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Helical Spring: Stored Energy and Natural Frequency

**Problem statement:** A helical compression spring has wire diameter $d = 6 \, \text{mm}$, mean coil diameter $D = 60 \, \text{mm}$, $n = 8$ active coils, $G = 80 \, \text{GPa}$. Find (a) spring stiffness, (b) deflection under axial load $F = 200 \, \text{N}$, (c) energy stored, and (d) natural frequency of a $1.5 \, \text{kg}$ mass attached.

#### Step 1 — Compute spring stiffness

$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$

All dimensions must be in consistent SI units (meters):

$$d^4 = (0.006)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^4$$

$$D^3 = (0.06)^3 = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$$

$$k = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 1.296 \times 10^{-9}}{8 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 8}$$

$$k = \frac{103.68}{1.3824 \times 10^{-2}} = 7500 \, \text{N/m}$$

#### Step 2 — Deflection under load

$$x = \frac{F}{k} = \frac{200}{7500} = 0.02667 \, \text{m} = 26.67 \, \text{mm}$$

#### Step 3 — Energy stored

$$U = \frac{1}{2} k x^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 7500 \times (0.02667)^2$$

$$U = 3750 \times 7.111 \times 10^{-4} = 2.667 \, \text{J}$$

#### Step 4 — Natural frequency of mass-spring system

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{\text{load}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{7500}{1.5}} = \sqrt{5000}$$

$$\omega_n = 70.71 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{70.71}{6.2832} = 11.25 \, \text{Hz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This natural frequency must be **at least 5× the operating frequency** of the automated machine to avoid resonance. If the machine cycles at 2 Hz, the spring's $f_n$ of 11.25 Hz gives a safety margin of 5.6 — acceptable.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Falling Weight | Inclined Belt | Helical Spring |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Gravitational PE | Mechanical (rotational) | Elastic PE |
| **Force/Torque Profile** | Constant | Variable (function of slip, wrap) | Linear in deflection |
| **Energy Density** | Low (large mass) | Medium | High (compact) |
| **Self-Reset?** | No (needs rewinding) | N/A (continuous loop) | No (needs rewinding) |
| **Typical Application** | Tower clocks, pile drivers | Conveyor systems, machine tools | Vehicle suspension, watch mainsprings |
| **KTU Difficulty Level** | Easy (3-mark derivation) | Moderate (5–7 mark numerical) | Moderate (7-mark design) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Mechanical Drive Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ENERGY INPUT LAYER] --> B[FALLING WEIGHT MODULE]
    A --> C[INCLINED BELT MODULE]
    A --> D[SPRING MODULE]

    B --> B1[Mass m Suspended by Cord]
    B1 --> B2[Drum Radius R]
    B2 --> B3[Constant Force F equals mg]
    B3 --> B4[Drum Torque T equals mgR]
    B4 --> B5[Output: Gear Train or Cam]

    C --> C1[Pulleys D1 and D2 on Parallel Shafts]
    C1 --> C2[Belt Wraps with Angle theta_w]
    C2 --> C3[Friction mu Generates Tension T1 over T2]
    C3 --> C4[Inclination alpha adds Gravitational Load]
    C4 --> C5[Output: Driven Shaft Power P]

    D --> D1[Linear Spring kx]
    D --> D2[Torsional Spring K theta]
    D --> D3[Spiral Spring Linear Decay]
    D --> D4[Constant Force Spring F0]
    D1 --> D5[Output: Restoring Force or Stored PE]
    D2 --> D5
    D3 --> D5
    D4 --> D5

    B5 --> E[ACTUATED MECHANISM]
    C5 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Automated Machine Output]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Falling Weight Clock Energy Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Weight at Height h] --> stepB[Gravity Acts: F equals mg]
    stepB --> stepC[Cord Unwinds from Drum]
    stepC --> stepD[Drum Rotates by theta equals h over R]
    stepD --> stepE[Gear Train Multiplies Speed]
    stepE --> stepF[Escapement Regulates to 1 tick per second]
    stepF --> stepG[Hands Rotate on Dial]
    stepG --> stepH[Weight Reaches Floor]
    stepH --> stepI[Manual Re-winding Required]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Inclined Belt Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisA[Driver Pulley D1] --> axisB[Driven Pulley D2]
    axisA -. Tension T1 Tight Side .-> axisB
    axisB -. Tension T2 Slack Side .-> axisA
    axisA --- centerline[Center Distance C]
    axisA --- wrapAngle[Wrap Angle theta_w]
    axisA --- incAngle[Inclination alpha]
    centerline --> frictTrans[Frictional Power Transmission]
    wrapAngle --> eytelwein[Eytelwein Relation T1 over T2 equals exp mu theta_w]
    incAngle --> loadComp[Gravitational Load Component W cos alpha]
    frictTrans --> powerOut[Output Power equals T1 minus T2 times v cos alpha]
    eytelwein --> powerOut
    loadComp --> powerOut
```

### 4.4 Textual Schematic — Spring System Block Architecture

| Block | Function | Key Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Block A: Spring Element** | Stores elastic PE | $k$ or $K$ |
| **Block B: Mounting Frame** | Provides reaction | Rigid boundary |
| **Block C: Load Mass** | Receives/displaces energy | $m$ |
| **Block D: Damper (Optional)** | Dissipates energy | $c$ |
| **Block E: Output Linkage** | Transmits motion | cam, lever, gear |

> [!NOTE]
> When blocks C, D, and E are combined, the spring-mass-damper system becomes a **second-order oscillator** whose dynamic response is fully described by:
> - Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
> - Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{mk})$
> - Settling time: $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta \omega_n)$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q:** Define a *falling weight drive system* and state **two** advantages it has over a spring drive.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *falling weight drive system* is a mechanical power-transmission arrangement in which a suspended mass descends under gravity, unwinding a cord from a drum to produce rotational output.

**Two advantages over a spring drive:**
1. **Constant driving force** ($F = mg$, independent of position) versus a spring whose force decreases linearly with deflection.
2. **High energy capacity** — by increasing mass, very large energy reservoirs are obtained economically; springs saturate due to material limits.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q:** State **Eytelwein's formula** for belt drives. What is the significance of the wrap angle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_w}$$

where $T_1$ = tight-side tension, $T_2$ = slack-side tension, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $\theta_w$ = angle of wrap on the pulley (in radians).

**Significance of wrap angle (1 mark):**
A larger wrap angle increases the tension ratio exponentially, which directly increases the maximum transmissible power. Designers therefore use an **idler pulley** to increase $\theta_w$ on the smaller pulley when the natural wrap is less than $\pi$ rad.

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Always convert mm to m **before** substituting into $k = Gd^4/(8D^3n)$. Many students lose 2 marks by mixing units.
> 2. **Neglecting centrifugal tension $T_c$** in belt problems. If the belt linear speed exceeds 10 m/s, $T_c$ cannot be ignored.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of $\cos\alpha$** for inclined belt power.
> 4. **Sign convention error** in spring energy — always use $|x|$ or square the deflection.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**A mass of $80 \, \text{kg}$ is suspended by a cord wound around a drum of radius $0.25 \, \text{m}$ mounted on a horizontal shaft. The shaft is connected through a gear pair (gear ratio 3:1) to a pinion of pitch radius $40 \, \text{mm}$ that drives a rack.**

**(a)** Calculate the tractive force, drum torque, and energy released as the weight falls $3 \, \text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the rack is to lift a load of $1.2 \, \text{kN}$ through a vertical distance of $0.5 \, \text{m}$ against gravity, determine the overall efficiency of the system and the additional height the weight must fall. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Tractive force:**
$$F = mg = 80 \times 9.81 = 784.8 \, \text{N}$$

**Drum torque:**
$$T_{\text{drum}} = F \times R = 784.8 \times 0.25 = 196.2 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Energy released over 3 m drop:**
$$E = mgh = 80 \times 9.81 \times 3 = 2354.4 \, \text{J}$$

**Mark allocation:** 2 + 2 + 3 = 7

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Energy required to lift the load:**
$$E_{\text{useful}} = W \times h_{\text{lift}} = 1200 \times 0.5 = 600 \, \text{J}$$

**Overall efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{input}}} = \frac{600}{2354.4} = 0.2548 = 25.48\%$$

**Additional height** the weight must fall to provide exactly 600 J:
$$h_{\text{add}} = \frac{E_{\text{useful}}}{mg} = \frac{600}{784.8} = 0.7645 \, \text{m}$$

**Total drop** = $3 + 0.7645 = 3.7645 \, \text{m}$ (but only the *additional* drop beyond the original 3 m is what was asked).

$$h_{\text{add}} = 0.765 \, \text{m} \approx 765 \, \text{mm}$$

**Mark allocation:** 3 (efficiency) + 4 (additional height with full energy equation) = 7

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative]

**A flat belt $120 \, \text{mm}$ wide connects two pulleys of diameters $D_1 = 250 \, \text{mm}$ (driver, rotating at $1200 \, \text{rpm}$) and $D_2 = 500 \, \text{mm}$ (driven). The center distance is $1.2 \, \text{m}$, the coefficient of friction is $0.28$, and the belt mass per unit length is $2 \, \text{kg/m}$. The belt is inclined at $15°$ to the horizontal and carries a steady load such that the slack-side tension is $T_2 = 600 \, \text{N}$.**

**(a)** Calculate the belt length, linear speed, wrap angle on the smaller pulley, and tight-side tension. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the power transmitted and the net torque on the driven shaft. If a helical compression spring is to be used as a tensioner with stiffness $k = 50 \, \text{kN/m}$, find the deflection required to maintain the slack-side tension. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Belt length (open belt, KTU standard formula):**
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(1.2) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.25 + 0.5) + \frac{(0.5 - 0.25)^2}{4(1.2)}$$

$$L = 2.4 + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.75) + \frac{0.0625}{4.8}$$

$$L = 2.4 + 1.1781 + 0.01302 = 3.5911 \, \text{m}$$

**Belt linear speed:**
$$v_b = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 1200}{60} = 15.708 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Wrap angle on smaller pulley:**
$$\theta_w = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right)$$

$$\theta_w = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.25}{2.4}\right) = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}(0.1042)$$

$$\theta_w = \pi - 2(0.1044) = 2.9328 \, \text{rad} = 168.05°$$

**Tight-side tension** using Eytelwein (given $T_2$):
$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta_w} = 600 \times e^{0.28 \times 2.9328} = 600 \times e^{0.8212}$$

$$T_1 = 600 \times 2.2732 = 1363.9 \, \text{N}$$

**Mark allocation:** 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Centrifugal tension:**
$$T_c = m_b v_b^2 = 2 \times (15.708)^2 = 2 \times 246.74 = 493.48 \, \text{N}$$

**Net effective tension difference:**
$$T_1 - T_2 = 1363.9 - 600 = 763.9 \, \text{N}$$

**Power transmitted (with inclination correction):**
$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v_b \cdot \cos\alpha = 763.9 \times 15.708 \times \cos 15°$$

$$P = 763.9 \times 15.708 \times 0.9659 = 11\,592 \, \text{W} \approx 11.59 \, \text{kW}$$

**Torque on driven shaft:**
$$T_{\text{driven}} = \frac{P}{\omega_2} = \frac{P}{2\pi N_2 / 60}$$

Driven speed: $N_2 = N_1 \times D_1/D_2 = 1200 \times 250/500 = 600 \, \text{rpm}$

$$T_{\text{driven}} = \frac{11592 \times 60}{2\pi \times 600} = \frac{695520}{3769.9} = 184.5 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Spring deflection** required to maintain $T_2 = 600 \, \text{N}$ (assuming spring force = $T_2$):
$$x = \frac{T_2}{k} = \frac{600}{50\,000} = 0.012 \, \text{m} = 12 \, \text{mm}$$

**Mark allocation:** 2 (centrifugal) + 2 (power) + 2 (torque) + 1 (spring) = 7

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Falling Weight** delivers **constant force** $F = mg$; energy = $mgh$; ideal for clocks requiring uniform torque.
- [ ] The drum rotation $\theta = h/R$ couples linear drop to angular output.
- [ ] **Inclined Belt** design requires four formulas: V.R., $L$, Eytelwein ($T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta_w}$), and power with $\cos\alpha$ factor.
- [ ] Always include **centrifugal tension** $T_c = m_b v_b^2$ when $v_b > 10 \, \text{m/s}$.
- [ ] **Linear spring** obeys $F = kx$, energy $U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$.
- [ ] **Torsional spring** obeys $T = K\theta$, energy $U = \frac{1}{2}K\theta^2$.
- [ ] **Spiral spring** has linearly decaying torque — *closest spring analog* of falling weight.
- [ ] **Constant-force spring** delivers $F_0$ independent of extension — used in counterbalances.
- [ ] Helical spring stiffness: $k = Gd^4/(8D^3n)$ — **convert all mm to m** before substitution.
- [ ] Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ must be at least **5× the operating frequency** to avoid resonance.
- [ ] KTU mark distribution: 3-mark direct (Part A) and 14-mark numerical with internal choice (Part B), sub-parts at 7 marks each.
- [ ] Examiner pitfalls: unit mismatch, missing $\cos\alpha$, ignoring $T_c$, sign errors in spring energy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Drives and Electric Drives: Induction Motors, Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Drives and Electric Drives: Induction Motors, Stepper Motors ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Electromagnetic Drives — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electromagnetic Drives (EMDs):** Actuator systems that convert electrical energy into mechanical motion (linear or rotary) by exploiting the force generated between current-carrying conductors and magnetic fields, governed by the Lorentz Force Principle: **F = BIL (sin θ)** and the Fleming Left-Hand Rule. These are the *workhorses* of modern automation — found in CNC machines, robotic joints, conveyor systems, and automated assembly lines. They are non-contact, highly responsive, and offer clean, controllable actuation. > [!NOTE] > **Two Broad Families in this Module:** > 1. **Induction Motors (AC Drives)** — Continuous rotation, high-power, fixed/variable speed. > 2. **Stepper Motors (Pulse-Driven Drives)** — Discrete incremental rotation, precision positioning, open-loop control. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Magnetic Handshake" Imagine two magnets. One is fixed (stator), the other free to spin (rotor). If you could somehow *rotate* the magnetic field of the fixed magnet continuously, the free magnet would be dragged along, always trying to "catch up" with the rotating field. This is precisely the **principle of an induction motor** — the rotor never quite reaches the speed of the rotating field; it *slips* behind, and this slip is what produces torque. For a **stepper motor**, imagine the same setup, but the magnetic field is switched in *discrete jumps* (steps). The rotor rotates one tiny precise increment (e.g., 1.8°) and locks there, waiting for the next command. It's like a digital clock's second hand — moves in clean, countable ticks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must clearly distinguish between **continuous-rotation drives** (induction motors) and **discrete-step drives** (stepper motors). Confusing their control philosophies is a frequent valuation penalty. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference) - **Supply frequency (f)** — typically **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz**. - **Number of poles (P)** — always an **even integer** (2, 4, 6, 8…). - **Synchronous speed (Ns)** — speed of the rotating magnetic field. - **Slip (s)** — fractional difference between synchronous and rotor speed. - **Step angle (β)** — angular displacement per input pulse for stepper motors. - **Holding torque** — maximum torque a stepper can resist while energized but stationary. - **Detent torque** — residual holding torque in unenergized stepper (PM/Hybrid types). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Three-Phase Induction Motor — Operational Theory **Construction:** - **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with three-phase distributed windings placed 120° electrical apart. - **Rotor:** Two main types: - **Squirrel-cage rotor** — bars shorted by end-rings (robust, low-cost, most common). - **Wound/Phase-wound rotor** — slip rings allow external resistance insertion (better starting). **Working Principle — Step by Step:** 1. **Three-phase AC supply** → produces three sinusoidal currents displaced by 120° in time. 2. These currents flow in the three stator windings → produce a **combined magnetic field whose axis rotates** at the synchronous speed. 3. This rotating flux cuts the stationary rotor conductors → by **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced in the rotor bars. 4. Since the rotor circuit is closed, induced EMF drives **rotor currents**. 5. By **Lenz's Law / Lorentz Force**, these currents interact with the rotating stator field → produce a **torque** that pulls the rotor in the direction of field rotation. 6. The rotor accelerates but never reaches synchronous speed (otherwise no relative motion → no induced EMF → no torque). > [!NOTE] > This is why induction motors are *asynchronous* — the rotor speed is always *less* than the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field. ### 2.2 Stepper Motor — Operational Theory **Types:** | Type | Construction | Feature | |---|---|---| | **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron toothed rotor, no magnet | Rotor aligns to minimum reluctance path | | **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Permanent-magnet rotor | Holds position via magnetic detent | | **Hybrid** | Toothed rotor with axial magnet | Best of both: small step + holding torque | **Working Principle:** 1. Stator has multiple poles with **excited coils**. 2. Pulses from a driver sequentially energize stator phases. 3. Rotor (toothed or magnetized) **aligns to the energized phase** to minimize reluctance or align with magnetic axis. 4. Each pulse → rotor advances by one **step angle (β)**. 5. By sequencing phase energization, **bidirectional, incremental rotation** is achieved. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table. Nearly every KTU 2024 numerical on this topic is built from these 6 equations. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm; f in Hz, P = number of poles | | 2 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | | 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 4 | Actual rotor speed | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | rpm | | 5 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | 6 | Torque equation | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}$ | N·m | | 7 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{\,2}}{X_2}$ | N·m (independent of rotor R) | | 8 | Slip at max torque | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Starting torque (s = 1) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + X_2^{\,2}}$ | N·m | | 10 | Step angle (stepper) | $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | degrees; $N_s$ = stator teeth/pole count, $N_r$ = rotor teeth | | 11 | Stepping rate → speed | $n = \dfrac{\theta}{\beta \cdot t}$ | steps/sec → rps | | 12 | Holding torque | $T_h \propto \Phi \cdot I$ | N·m | > **Critical Pitfall:** Never confuse **$R_2$** (rotor resistance *per phase*, referred to stator) with **$X_2$** (rotor leakage reactance at standstill frequency). They scale differently with slip. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Induction motors** drive pumps, compressors, fans, conveyor belts — anywhere sustained rotary motion is required with minimal maintenance. - **Stepper motors** drive 3D printer axes, CNC feed drives, camera autofocus, and pick-and-place machines — anywhere precise open-loop positioning matters. - In **automated machines**, induction motors are typically paired with **VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)** for speed control, while stepper motors are paired with **microstepping drivers** for resolution enhancement. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed $N_s$ A rotating magnetic field completes one mechanical revolution when the supply AC completes **P/2 electrical cycles** (because P poles = P/2 pole-pairs). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Electrical cycles per second} &= f \\ \text{Mechanical revolutions per second} &= \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P} \\ \text{Mechanical revolutions per minute} &= \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 f}{P} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \;\; \text{rpm}} $$ **Conversion logic:** Each pole-pair produces one electrical cycle per mechanical revolution; with P/2 pole-pairs, we divide f by P/2 to get mechanical frequency, then multiply by 60 for rpm. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Equation of Induction Motor **Starting point:** Power transferred across the air-gap to the rotor is: $$ P_{ag} = 3 \cdot I_2^{\,2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} $$ **Rotor current** (from rotor equivalent circuit, with slip-modified reactance $sX_2$): $$ I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}} $$ **Substitute** into the air-gap power: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{ag} &= 3 \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^{\,2}}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} \\ &= \frac{3 s E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Mechanical (shaft) power** developed is rotor copper loss subtracted from air-gap power: $$ P_m = P_{ag} (1 - s) = \frac{3 s E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}(1 - s) $$ **Torque** is shaft power divided by mechanical angular speed $\omega_r = (1-s)\omega_s$: $$ \begin{aligned} T &= \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_{ag}(1-s)}{(1-s)\omega_s} = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} \\ &= \frac{1}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{3 s E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition for Maximum Torque Differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$. From the torque expression, define $K = \dfrac{3 E_2^{\,2} R_2}{\omega_s}$ (constant). Then: $$ T = K \cdot \frac{s}{R_2^{\,2} + s^2 X_2^{\,2}} $$ Apply quotient rule: $$ \frac{dT}{ds} = K \cdot \frac{(R_2^{\,2} + s^2 X_2^{\,2})(1) - s(2 s X_2^{\,2})}{(R_2^{\,2} + s^2 X_2^{\,2})^2} = 0 $$ Numerator must vanish: $$ \begin{aligned} R_2^{\,2} + s^2 X_2^{\,2} - 2 s^2 X_2^{\,2} &= 0 \\ R_2^{\,2} - s^2 X_2^{\,2} &= 0 \\ s^2 &= \frac{R_2^{\,2}}{X_2^{\,2}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}} $$ **Substitute** $s_m$ back into torque equation: $$ T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\frac{R_2}{X_2} \cdot E_2^{\,2} R_2}{R_2^{\,2} + \left(\frac{R_2}{X_2}\right)^2 X_2^{\,2}} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{R_2^{\,2} E_2^{\,2} / X_2}{2 R_2^{\,2}} = \frac{3 E_2^{\,2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^{\,2}}{2 \omega_s X_2} \quad \text{(independent of } R_2\text{)}} $$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Step Angle of a Stepper Motor For a multi-stack or hybrid stepper, the rotor has $N_r$ teeth and the stator phases produce $N_s$ effective pole-pairs (or step states). The smallest angular repeat is: $$ \beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{steps per tooth pass}} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_s \cdot N_r} $$ For a **variable-reluctance** stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $m$ stator phases each with $p$ teeth, the step angle becomes: $$ \boxed{\beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_r}} $$ **Conversion logic:** Each phase-energization shifts the rotor by $\dfrac{1}{m N_r}$ of a full revolution; multiplying by 360° yields the step angle in degrees. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Exam | Parameter | Induction Motor | Stepper Motor | |---|---|---| | Power supply | 3-phase / 1-phase AC | DC pulses | | Motion type | Continuous rotary | Discrete incremental steps | | Control mode | VFD / DOL starter | Open-loop pulse train | | Position accuracy | Low (open loop) | High (no feedback needed) | | Speed range | High (up to ~3000 rpm) | Low to medium | | Holding at zero speed | No (coasts) | Yes (holding torque) | | Cost & complexity | Moderate | Driver electronics needed | | Slip | Yes (asynchronous) | Not applicable | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Induction Motor — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3-Phase AC Mains
50 Hz, 415V] --> B[Stator Windings
120 electrical apart] B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field
Ns = 120f / P] C --> D[Relative Motion with Rotor
slip = s] D --> E[Induced EMF in Rotor
Faraday Law] E --> F[Rotor Currents
I2 = sE2 / Z2] F --> G[Lorentz Force on Bars
F = BIL sin theta] G --> H[Mechanical Torque
T proportional to s] H --> I[Coupled Mechanical Load
Fan, Conveyor, Pump] I --> J[Feedback to VFD
for speed control] J --> B ``` **Interpretation:** The diagram captures the energy-flow chain — from electrical input through magnetic coupling, induced currents, electromagnetic force, to mechanical output. The dashed feedback loop represents the VFD closed-loop speed control. --- ### 4.2 Torque-Slip Characteristic Curve (Mermaid rendering) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Slip s = 0] -->|Stability region| B[Slip s = sm
Max Torque] B -->|Operating region| C[Slip s = 1
Starting] D[Tmax horizontal line] -.-> B E[Stability zone: 0 to sm] -->|Positive slope| F[Stable operation] G[Instability zone: sm to 1] -->|Negative slope| H[Cannot run steadily] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Operational Insight:** A normal induction motor operates on the **stable region** (between $s = 0$ and $s = s_m$), where torque *increases* with slip. If the load pulls the motor past $s_m$ into the negative-slope region, the motor stalls. --- ### 4.3 Stepper Motor — Phases Sequencing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Controller Sends Pulse] --> B{Phase Selection Logic} B -->|Pulse 1| C[Phase A ON] B -->|Pulse 2| D[Phase B ON] B -->|Pulse 3| E[Phase C ON] B -->|Pulse 4| F[Phase D ON] C --> G[Rotor Advances by step angle beta] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H{More Pulses?} H -->|Yes| B H -->|No| I[Holding Torque Maintains
Last Position] ``` **Interpretation:** Each pulse from the controller selects a phase, advancing the rotor by exactly one step angle $\beta$. When pulses stop, the energized phase produces a **holding torque** that locks the shaft in position — a key advantage for open-loop positioning. --- ### 4.4 Variable Reluctance Stepper — Construction Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph StatorAssembly SA[Stator Pole 1
with Coil A] SB[Stator Pole 2
with Coil B] SC[Stator Pole 3
with Coil C] SD[Stator Pole 4
with Coil D] end subgraph RotorAssembly RA[Soft Iron Rotor
with multiple teeth] end SA -. Attracts nearest teeth .-> RA SB -. Attracts nearest teeth .-> RA SC -. Attracts nearest teeth .-> RA SD -. Attracts nearest teeth .-> RA ``` > [!NOTE] > In a VR stepper, the rotor has **no permanent magnet**. It simply moves to the position of *minimum magnetic reluctance* — i.e., where its teeth align most completely with the energized stator poles. This makes VR steppers low-cost but with no detent torque. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define "slip" in a three-phase induction motor. Why can the rotor never attain synchronous speed?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Slip $s$ is the relative difference between synchronous speed $N_s$ and actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$: $$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} $$ The rotor can never reach synchronous speed because if it did, there would be **no relative motion** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors, leading to **zero induced EMF**, **zero rotor current**, and consequently **zero torque** (Equation: $T \propto s$). Equilibrium is reached when developed torque equals load torque at a finite slip value, typically **2–8%** at full load. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any three differences between a stepper motor and an induction motor.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** | # | Stepper Motor | Induction Motor | |---|---|---| | 1 | Operates on **discrete digital pulses** | Operates on **continuous AC supply** | | 2 | Provides **precise open-loop position control** (no feedback needed) | Provides **continuous rotary motion**; position control requires encoder | | 3 | Has **holding torque** at standstill (shaft locks) | Has **no holding torque** — rotor coasts when de-energized | | 4 | Lower power rating (W to few kW) | Higher power rating (kW to MW) | --- ### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at 1440 rpm at full load. Calculate:** **(a)** Synchronous speed, slip, and rotor frequency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the rotor resistance is $0.5 \, \Omega$/phase and standstill rotor reactance is $1.5 \, \Omega$/phase, find the slip at maximum torque and the ratio of maximum torque to full-load torque. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution:** **(a) Synchronous speed, slip, rotor frequency:** $$ N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$ $$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \;\; (4\%) $$ $$ f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz} $$ **(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2)** --- **(b) Slip at maximum torque and torque ratio:** Given: $R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5 \, \Omega$. $$ s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333 \;\; (33.3\%) $$ For the torque ratio, we use: $$ \frac{T_{max}}{T} = \frac{R_2^{\,2} + (s X_2)^2}{2 s R_2 X_2} $$ At full load, $s = 0.04$: $$ (s X_2)^2 = (0.04 \times 1.5)^2 = (0.06)^2 = 0.0036 $$ $$ R_2^{\,2} = (0.5)^2 = 0.25 $$ $$ \text{Numerator} = 0.25 + 0.0036 = 0.2536 $$ $$ 2 s R_2 X_2 = 2 \times 0.04 \times 0.5 \times 1.5 = 0.06 $$ $$ \frac{T_{max}}{T_{fl}} = \frac{0.2536}{0.06} \approx 4.23 $$ $$ \boxed{T_{max} \approx 4.23 \times T_{fl}} $$ **(7 marks: 3 for $s_m$ + 4 for ratio)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]* **(a) Explain with a neat diagram the working of a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor. Compare its performance with a Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor. (7 marks)** **(b) A hybrid stepper motor has 200 rotor teeth and is excited by a 2-phase stator. Calculate the step angle. If the motor receives 1500 pulses per second, find the shaft speed in rpm. (7 marks)** --- **Solution:** **(a) Working of PM Stepper Motor and Comparison:** A PM stepper motor has a **permanently magnetized cylindrical rotor** (typically axially magnetized with N and S poles). The stator has projecting poles with concentrated windings. When the stator windings are energized in a specific sequence, the stator poles produce magnetic fields of alternating polarity. The rotor's permanent magnet experiences an attractive/repulsive torque that causes it to **align with the energized stator poles**. Sequential energization of phases (typically A → B → A̅ → B̅) produces **discrete angular steps** of rotation. The rotor can be reversed by reversing the phase sequence. **Comparison with VR Stepper Motor:** | Feature | PM Stepper | VR Stepper | |---|---|---| | Rotor | Permanent magnet | Soft iron, toothed | | Holding (detent) torque at zero excitation | **Yes** (from permanent magnet) | **No** | | Step size | Larger (typically 7.5°–90°) | Smaller (down to 0.9°–15°) | | Inertia | Higher | Lower | | Response / damping | Good | Poor at low speeds (oscillation prone) | | Cost | Higher (magnet) | Lower | | Resolution | Lower | Higher | **(7 marks: 3 for working + 4 for comparison table)** --- **(b) Step angle and shaft speed:** Given: $N_r = 200$ rotor teeth, $m = 2$ phases. $$ \beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{2 \times 200} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{400} = 0.9^{\circ} $$ For 1500 pulses/sec: $$ \text{Revolutions/sec} = \frac{\text{Pulses/sec}}{\text{Steps/rev}} = \frac{1500}{400} = 3.75 \text{ rps} $$ $$ N = 3.75 \times 60 = 225 \text{ rpm} $$ **(7 marks: 3 for step angle + 4 for shaft speed)** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing up $R_2$ and $X_2$:** $R_2$ is *rotor resistance per phase referred to stator*; $X_2$ is *standstill rotor reactance* — NOT the synchronous reactance. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in torque equations — the "3" arises because induction motors are 3-phase. Dropping it costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **In stepper problems**, students often use $N_s$ for synchronous speed instead of the **number of stator pole-pairs** in the step angle formula. Read the question carefully! > 4. **Forgetting units:** $N_s$ in rpm, $\omega_s$ in rad/s, $T$ in N·m. Mixing them yields numerical errors that examiners penalize. > 5. **In full-load slip problems**, do not assume $s = 0.04$ universally — always derive from given $N_s$ and $N_r$. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU Module 2** - ✅ Induction motor rotor **never reaches** synchronous speed — slip is essential for torque. - ✅ **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ — memorize cold. - ✅ **Slip** at full load is typically **2–8%**; at no load, $s \approx 0$. - ✅ **Maximum torque** is independent of rotor resistance $R_2$ — but slip at $T_{max}$ is proportional to $R_2$. - ✅ **Starting torque** of a cage motor is **1.5–2×** full-load torque (DOL starting). - ✅ **Stepper motor** = open-loop positioning; **holding torque** keeps shaft locked at last position. - ✅ **Step angle** $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_r}$ — for VR/PM types; for hybrid, use the rotor tooth count with the phase-tooth multiplication factor. - ✅ **VR steppers** have no detent torque (rotor is unmagnetized soft iron). - ✅ **Hybrid steppers** combine PM (detent torque) and VR (small step size) — industry favorite. - ✅ Induction motors are **asynchronous**; stepper motors are **synchronous** to the pulse train. - ✅ **Direction reversal** of stepper: reverse the phase excitation sequence. - ✅ **V/f control** is the standard method to vary induction motor speed. - ✅ Always convert **$N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s)** using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ before substituting into torque equations. --- *End of Module 2 Notes — Electromagnetic Drives and Electric Drives: Induction Motors, Stepper Motors*

topics.title: Brakes and Variable Moment of Inertia
Dynamic Analysis: Calculation of Selection Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Analysis: Calculation of Selection Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Analysis** of a mechanism is the study of forces, torques, energy, and power developed in the moving links of a machine when subjected to external loads, inertia effects, and motion-dependent forces. In the context of automated machines (PERUT743), **Selection Parameters** are the quantitative design outputs derived from this dynamic analysis — such as actuator force, fluid flow rate, working pressure, power requirement, and cycle time — that govern the correct selection of prime movers, actuators, valves, and pipelines.

In automated machine design, the mechanism is rarely operating at constant velocity. It accelerates, decelerates, and reverses. Hence, a **static force balance is insufficient**; the inertia forces ($m \cdot a$) of the moving links must be superimposed on the external load to determine the **maximum effective force** the actuator must deliver.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Elevator Problem**
> Imagine pushing an empty shopping cart (low mass) versus a fully loaded cart (high mass). The loaded cart needs much more push to start moving. Once both are rolling, the loaded cart needs extra force to **stop** too. Now, if the floor is greasy (low friction), the force needed is even more.
> This is exactly what dynamic analysis does: it calculates the **worst-case force** the actuator (push) must provide, considering:
> 1. **External load** (weight of goods)
> 2. **Inertia** (to start/stop the load)
> 3. **Friction** (resistance in guides)
> 4. **Gravity** (vertical motion component)
> Once this worst-case force is known, we size the cylinder bore, choose the working pressure, and estimate the compressor/pump rating.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Atmospheric pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa $= 1.01325$ bar $= 1.01325 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- **Standard pneumatic pressure (shop air):** $p_{air} = 6$ to **8 bar** (typically **6 bar** = $6 \times 10^5$ N/m²)
- **Standard hydraulic pressure:** $p_{hyd} = 50$ to **200 bar** (typically **100 bar**)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³
- **Density of mineral hydraulic oil:** $\rho_{oil} \approx 870$ kg/m³
- **Mechanical efficiency of cylinder (typical):** $\eta_m = 0.85$ to **0.95**
- **Coefficient of friction (sliding guides, lubricated):** $\mu = 0.05$ to **0.15**
- **Standard piston speeds:** $v_{pneu} = 0.1$ to **1.5 m/s** ; $v_{hyd} = 0.01$ to **0.5 m/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to compute selection parameters such as **Cylinder Bore Diameter (D)**, **Piston Rod Diameter (d)**, **Stroke Length (L)**, **Air/Oil Consumption (Q)**, **Pump/Compressor Power (P)**, and **Cycle Time (t)** for both **pneumatic** and **hydraulic** systems used in automated mechanisms.

### 1.4 Why Dynamic Analysis? — The "Why" Behind the Topic

In the previous module, students learned **kinematic analysis** (geometry of motion without considering forces). Module 2 takes the next step:

| Analysis Type | What is Considered? | What is Ignored? |
|---|---|---|
| Kinematic Analysis | Position, velocity, acceleration of links | All forces (assumed motion is prescribed) |
| Static Force Analysis | External loads, gravity, friction | Inertia of moving links |
| **Dynamic Analysis** | **External loads + Inertia + Friction + Gravity** | Vibration, flexibility of links |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If a machine operates at **low speeds** (less than 0.1 m/s) and carries **light loads**, dynamic effects may be ignored. But for high-speed pick-and-place robots, packaging machines, and press automation, neglecting inertia leads to undersized actuators, which stall mid-cycle — a common KTU exam scenario and a common real-world failure mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Dynamic Analysis

The dynamic analysis of an automated machine mechanism rests on three pillars derived from Newtonian mechanics and energy conservation.

#### Pillar 1: Newton's Second Law for Translating Bodies

For a body of mass $m$ moving with linear acceleration $a$ under the action of a net force $F_{net}$:

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a$$

When applied to an actuator driving a load, the net force the actuator must develop is:

$$F_{actuator} = F_{load} + F_{friction} + F_{inertia} + F_{gravity}$$

where each term is the **resistance** the actuator must overcome.

#### Pillar 2: Newton's Second Law for Rotating Bodies

For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis with angular acceleration $\alpha$ and moment of inertia $I$:

$$T_{net} = I \cdot \alpha$$

This is critical when the mechanism involves **rotary actuators** (rack-and-pinion, vane motors, or piston-crank arrangements).

#### Pillar 3: Energy Conservation (D'Alembert's Principle)

D'Alembert's principle converts dynamic problems into static problems by introducing an **inertia force** equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the acceleration-induced force:

$$F_{inertia} = -m \cdot a$$

This allows superposition of inertial and gravitational effects onto a free-body diagram.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six-Step Selection Procedure (KTU High-Yield Framework)**

**Step 1 — Identify the Mechanism and its Motion Profile**
Draw the kinematic diagram. Identify linear vs. rotary motion, total stroke, and acceleration/deceleration zones.

**Step 2 — Quantify the External Load**
Determine the mass $m_{load}$ of the payload plus the moving parts of the mechanism. Compute weight $W = m_{load} \cdot g$.

**Step 3 — Determine the Motion Parameters**
From kinematic analysis (or design specification):
- Maximum linear velocity $v_{max}$ (m/s)
- Acceleration time $t_a$ (s) and deceleration time $t_d$ (s)
- Acceleration $a = v_{max}/t_a$ (m/s²)
- Stroke length $L$ (m)

**Step 4 — Compute the Total Effective Force on the Actuator**
Apply the dynamic force equation in the worst-case (highest force) direction of motion.

**Step 5 — Size the Actuator (Cylinder Bore)**
Choose working pressure $p_{sup}$ from supply constraints (e.g., 6 bar for pneumatics). Then:
$$A_{piston} = \frac{F_{actuator}}{\eta_m \cdot p_{sup}}$$
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{piston}}{\pi}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Flow Rate, Power, and Cycle Time**
- Cylinder volume per stroke: $V_{stroke} = A_{piston} \cdot L$
- Flow rate required: $Q = \frac{V_{stroke}}{t_{stroke}}$
- Hydraulic/pneumatic power: $P = p \cdot Q$
- Cycle time: $t_{cycle} = t_{extend} + t_{retract} + t_{dwell}$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in over 80% of KTU Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piston Area (full) | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | $D$ = bore diameter |
| 2 | Annulus Area (rod side) | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | m² | $d$ = rod diameter |
| 3 | Effective Pushing Force | $F_{push} = p \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_m$ | N | During extension |
| 4 | Effective Pulling Force | $F_{pull} = p \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_m$ | N | During retraction |
| 5 | Load Force (horizontal) | $F_L = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | N | Friction-dominated |
| 6 | Load Force (vertical up) | $F_L = m \cdot g$ | N | Lifting against gravity |
| 7 | Inertia Force | $F_i = m \cdot a$ | N | During acceleration phase |
| 8 | Total Actuator Force | $F_T = F_L + F_i + F_{friction}$ | N | Worst case sum |
| 9 | Required Bore Diameter | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_T}{\pi \cdot p \cdot \eta_m}}$ | m | Solve for $D$ |
| 10 | Piston Velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | m/s | $Q$ = supply flow rate |
| 11 | Stroke Time | $t_s = \dfrac{L}{v_p}$ | s | Constant velocity assumed |
| 12 | Volume per Stroke | $V_s = A_p \cdot L$ | m³ | Single extension stroke |
| 13 | Air Consumption (per cycle) | $V_{cycle} = V_s + V_a$ | m³ | $V_a$ = annulus volume on retract |
| 14 | Free Air Delivery (FAD) | $Q_{FAD} = V_{cycle} \times n$ | m³/min | $n$ = cycles/min |
| 15 | Compressor Power | $P = \dfrac{p_{sup} \cdot Q_{FAD}}{\eta_c}$ | W | $\eta_c$ = compressor efficiency |
| 16 | Hydraulic Pump Power | $P = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600 \eta_p}$ | kW | $p$ in bar, $Q$ in LPM |
| 17 | Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | For laminar/turbulent check |
| 18 | Pressure Drop (Darcy) | $\Delta p = f \dfrac{L}{D_h} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Friction loss in pipe |
| 19 | Cushioning Kinetic Energy | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Deceleration energy to absorb |
| 20 | Cycle Time | $t_c = t_{ext} + t_{ret} + t_{dwell}$ | s | Total machine cycle |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> 1. **Cost Optimization:** Over-sizing an actuator wastes money (larger compressor, larger valve, more floor space). Under-sizing causes production-line stoppages.
> 2. **Energy Efficiency:** Properly sized systems use 20–40% less compressed air, directly reducing electricity bills.
> 3. **Safety:** Inaccurate dynamic analysis in press automation or lifting mechanisms can cause catastrophic failures — this is why standards like **ISO 4413** (hydraulics) and **ISO 4414** (pneumatics) mandate such calculations.
> 4. **Predictive Maintenance:** Knowing the expected inertia force helps in predicting wear rates of seals, bearings, and cylinders.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bore Diameter from Load and Pressure

> **Problem Setup (KTU Typical):** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder must push a mass of 80 kg horizontally. The friction coefficient is 0.2, acceleration is 1.5 m/s², and supply pressure is 6 bar. Determine the bore diameter assuming mechanical efficiency of 0.9.

**Step 1 — Identify all resistive forces.**

The total force the cylinder must overcome consists of three components:

$$F_{total} = F_{friction} + F_{inertia} + F_{gravity}$$

For **horizontal motion**, the gravitational component along the direction of motion is zero. Hence:

$$F_{total} = F_{friction} + F_{inertia}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the friction force.**

Friction force on a horizontal surface is the normal force times the coefficient of friction. The normal force equals the weight of the load:

$$F_{normal} = m \cdot g = 80 \times 9.81 = 784.8 \text{ N}$$

Therefore:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_{normal} = 0.2 \times 784.8 = 156.96 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the inertia force.**

By Newton's second law, the force required to accelerate the mass at $a = 1.5$ m/s² is:

$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = 80 \times 1.5 = 120 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the resistive forces.**

$$F_{total} = 156.96 + 120 + 0 = 276.96 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Equate to actuator force equation.**

The theoretical force a pneumatic cylinder can deliver at pressure $p$ with bore area $A_p$ is:

$$F_{cylinder} = p \cdot A_p$$

But accounting for mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ (which captures friction in seals, misalignment losses):

$$F_{cylinder,eff} = p \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_m$$

**Step 6 — Solve for piston area $A_p$.**

$$A_p = \frac{F_{total}}{p \cdot \eta_m}$$

Convert pressure to consistent SI units: $p = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$.

$$A_p = \frac{276.96}{6 \times 10^5 \times 0.9} = \frac{276.96}{5.4 \times 10^5} = 5.129 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Solve for bore diameter $D$.**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_p}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 5.129 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{2.0516 \times 10^{-3}}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{6.5306 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$D = 0.02555 \text{ m} = 25.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Apply engineering rounding and standard sizing.**

Cylinder bores come in standard ISO sizes: 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125 mm. We must select the **next higher** standard size to ensure the actuator can deliver the required force.

$$\boxed{D_{standard} = 32 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** With $D = 32$ mm, $A_p = \pi/4 \times 0.032^2 = 8.04 \times 10^{-4}$ m². The actual force delivered is $F = 6 \times 10^5 \times 8.04 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.9 = 434.3$ N, which is well above the required 276.96 N. The **margin** is $(434.3 - 276.96)/276.96 = 56.8\%$, which is acceptable for industrial pneumatic systems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pneumatic Air Consumption and Compressor Sizing

> **Problem Setup:** Using the same cylinder ($D = 32$ mm, rod $d = 12$ mm, stroke $L = 200$ mm), the machine operates at 10 cycles/min. Calculate the free air delivery (FAD) required and the compressor power if the overall efficiency is 0.7.

**Step 1 — Compute piston (full) area.**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.032)^2}{4} = 8.04 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute annulus (rod-side) area.**

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.032^2 - 0.012^2)$$

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.001024 - 0.000144) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.000880)$$

$$A_a = 6.912 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute extension stroke volume.**

This is the volume of air at supply pressure needed to push the piston to full extension:

$$V_{ext} = A_p \times L = 8.04 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2 = 1.608 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute retraction stroke volume.**

During retraction, the air enters the rod side, so the volume is:

$$V_{ret} = A_a \times L = 6.912 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2 = 1.382 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Total air consumed per cycle (at supply pressure).**

$$V_{cycle,sup} = V_{ext} + V_{ret} = 1.608 \times 10^{-4} + 1.382 \times 10^{-4} = 2.990 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Free Air Delivery (atmospheric conditions).**

Free air delivery is the volume of air at atmospheric pressure. Using the ideal gas relation $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$ (assuming isothermal process, which is the standard assumption for slow-acting pneumatic cylinders):

$$V_{cycle,FAD} = V_{cycle,sup} \times \frac{p_{sup}}{p_{atm}}$$

$$V_{cycle,FAD} = 2.990 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{6}{1} = 1.794 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cycle}$$

*(Note: We use gauge pressure ratio; technically $p_{sup} = 6 + 1 = 7$ bar absolute, $p_{atm} = 1$ bar absolute, but in industry the simplified 6:1 ratio is commonly used. We will use absolute pressures for rigor below.)*

For absolute pressures: $p_{sup,abs} = 7 \text{ bar}$, $p_{atm} = 1 \text{ bar}$:

$$V_{cycle,FAD} = 2.990 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{7}{1} = 2.093 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cycle}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by cycles per minute.**

$$Q_{FAD} = V_{cycle,FAD} \times n = 2.093 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 = 2.093 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$Q_{FAD} = 0.02093 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 20.93 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 8 — Add leakage and usage factor.**

Industrial practice multiplies by a factor of 1.2 to 1.5 to account for valve leakage, line losses, and other actuators:

$$Q_{FAD,design} = 1.3 \times 20.93 = 27.21 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 9 — Compressor power calculation.**

The theoretical isothermal power to compress air from $p_1$ to $p_2$ is:

$$P_{theor} = p_1 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$

With overall efficiency $\eta_c$:

$$P_{comp} = \frac{p_1 \cdot Q_1 \cdot \ln(p_2/p_1)}{\eta_c}$$

Here $p_1 = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $Q_1 = 27.21/60000$ m³/s $= 4.535 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, $p_2 = 7 \times 10^5$ Pa:

$$P_{comp} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times 4.535 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln(7)}{\eta_c}$$

$$\ln(7) = 1.9459$$

$$P_{comp} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times 4.535 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.9459}{0.7}$$

$$P_{comp} = \frac{89.43}{0.7} = 127.76 \text{ W} \approx 0.128 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{compressor} \approx 0.13 \text{ kW (minimum)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **A 0.13 kW compressor is very small — about 1/6 HP.** This shows the inherent power density advantage of pneumatics for low-force, high-speed applications. The same load in hydraulics would still be efficient, but pneumatic systems excel in clean, lightweight automation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydraulic Cylinder Sizing for a Vertical Lift

> **Problem Setup:** A hydraulic cylinder must lift a mass of 2000 kg vertically. The cylinder is single-acting (plunger type) with a vertical orientation. The supply pressure is 100 bar, mechanical efficiency is 0.95, and the load must be lifted in 3 seconds over a stroke of 0.5 m. Find the bore diameter and pump flow rate.

**Step 1 — Compute the load force (vertical lift against gravity).**

For vertical upward motion, the cylinder must overcome the full weight of the load:

$$F_{load} = m \cdot g = 2000 \times 9.81 = 19{,}620 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inertia force.**

The cylinder must accelerate the load from rest. The required velocity at the end of the stroke (assuming uniform acceleration profile, $v^2 = 2 a L$):

$$v = \frac{2L}{t} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{3} = 0.333 \text{ m/s}$$

Acceleration:

$$a = \frac{v}{t} = \frac{0.333}{3} = 0.111 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Inertia force:

$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = 2000 \times 0.111 = 222.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total effective force.**

Since this is a single-acting cylinder being supplied only on the piston side, the full piston area experiences the supply pressure. We ignore friction in guides for this simplified problem (assume frictionless guides):

$$F_{total} = F_{load} + F_{inertia} = 19{,}620 + 222.2 = 19{,}842.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for required piston area.**

$$A_p = \frac{F_{total}}{p \cdot \eta_m} = \frac{19{,}842.2}{100 \times 10^5 \times 0.95}$$

$$A_p = \frac{19{,}842.2}{9.5 \times 10^6} = 2.0886 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for bore diameter.**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_p}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2.0886 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{2.6594 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D = 0.05157 \text{ m} \approx 51.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Standard sizing.**

Standard hydraulic bores: 50, 63, 80, 100 mm. Choose next size up: $D = 63$ mm.

**Step 7 — Compute the required pump flow rate.**

Piston velocity (average):

$$v_{piston} = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.5}{3} = 0.1667 \text{ m/s}$$

Required flow rate:

$$Q = A_p \cdot v_{piston} = 2.0886 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1667 = 3.481 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to LPM (1 m³/s = 60,000 LPM):

$$Q = 3.481 \times 10^{-4} \times 60{,}000 = 20.89 \text{ LPM}$$

**Step 8 — Verify the design with the standard 63 mm bore.**

Actual area: $A_{actual} = \pi/4 \times 0.063^2 = 3.117 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
Actual force at 100 bar: $F_{actual} = 100 \times 10^5 \times 3.117 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.95 = 29{,}611$ N
Safety margin: $(29{,}611 - 19{,}842.2)/19{,}842.2 = 49.2\%$ ✓ (acceptable)

Updated flow for 63 mm bore: $Q = 3.117 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1667 = 5.196 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s $= 31.18$ LPM

$$\boxed{D_{bore} = 63 \text{ mm}, \quad Q_{pump} = 31.2 \text{ LPM at 100 bar}}$$

**Step 9 — Pump power calculation.**

$$P_{pump} = \frac{p \cdot Q}{600 \cdot \eta_p}$$

With $p = 100$ bar, $Q = 31.2$ LPM, $\eta_p = 0.85$:

$$P_{pump} = \frac{100 \times 31.2}{600 \times 0.85} = \frac{3120}{510} = 6.12 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{pump} \approx 6.12 \text{ kW (minimum)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Problem Scenarios in KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix to identify the type of problem and the correct formula chain.**

| Scenario | Given | To Find | Formula Chain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder sizing (horizontal) | $m, \mu, a, p, \eta_m$ | $D$ | $F_T = \mu mg + ma$; $D = \sqrt{4F_T/(\pi p \eta_m)}$ |
| Cylinder sizing (vertical lift) | $m, a, p, \eta_m$ | $D$ | $F_T = mg + ma$; $D = \sqrt{4F_T/(\pi p \eta_m)}$ |
| Piston velocity check | $Q_{pump}, D$ | $v_p$ | $v_p = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ |
| Cycle time calculation | $L, v_p$ | $t_s$ | $t_s = L/v_p$ |
| Air consumption | $D, d, L, n$ | $Q_{FAD}$ | $V_c = A_p L + A_a L$; $Q = V_c \times n \times (p_s/p_a)$ |
| Compressor power | $Q_{FAD}, p_s, \eta_c$ | $P$ | $P = p_1 Q_1 \ln(p_2/p_1)/\eta_c$ |
| Pump power | $p, Q, \eta_p$ | $P$ (kW) | $P = pQ/(600\eta_p)$ |
| Cushioning energy | $m, v$ | $KE$ | $KE = 0.5 m v^2$ |
| Pressure drop in pipe | $L, D_h, v, \rho, f$ | $\Delta p$ | $\Delta p = f(L/D_h)(\rho v^2/2)$ |
| Rotary actuator torque | $I, \alpha$ | $T$ | $T = I\alpha$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Dynamic Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Specifications] --> B[Kinematic Analysis]
    B --> C[Velocity and Acceleration Profile]
    C --> D[Identify Load Mass and Friction]
    D --> E[Compute Force Components]
    E --> E1[Ffriction = mu m g]
    E --> E2[Finertia = m a]
    E --> E3[Fgravity = m g sin theta]
    E1 --> F[Total Effective Force F_T]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Select Actuator Type}
    G --> G1[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    G --> G2[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    G1 --> H1[Choose Supply Pressure p_s]
    G2 --> H2[Choose Supply Pressure p_s]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Bore Diameter D]
    H2 --> I2[Compute Bore Diameter D]
    I1 --> J1[Verify Standard ISO Size]
    I2 --> J2[Verify Standard ISO Size]
    J1 --> K1[Compute Flow Rate Q]
    J2 --> K2[Compute Flow Rate Q]
    K1 --> L1[Compressor Sizing]
    K2 --> L2[Pump Sizing]
    L1 --> M1[Power and FAD Output]
    L2 --> M2[Power and LPM Output]
    M1 --> N[Final Selection Parameters]
    M2 --> N
    N --> O[Validation: Cycle Time Check]
    O --> P[Design Output Report]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Force Decomposition on an Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supply Pressure p] --> B[Cylinder Generates Fpush]
    B --> C{Force Balance on Piston}
    D[Load Fload] --> C
    E[Friction Ffric] --> C
    F[Inertia Finertia] --> C
    G[Gravity Component Fgrav] --> C
    C --> H{Factuator greater than Fsum}
    H -->|Yes| I[Actuator Selection OK]
    H -->|No| J[Increase D or Increase p]
    J --> B
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Cylinder Geometry and Areas

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Double-Acting Cylinder Cross Section]
    A1[Piston Rod Diameter d] --> A2[Annulus Area Aa]
    A3[Bore Diameter D] --> A4[Piston Full Area Ap]
    A4 --> A5[Extension Stroke Volume]
    A2 --> A6[Retraction Stroke Volume]
    end
    A5 --> B[Total Air Consumption per Cycle]
    A6 --> B
    B --> C[Free Air Delivery QFAD]
    C --> D[Compressor Sizing]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Pneumatic vs. Hydraulic Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application] --> B{Force Required}
    B -->|Less than 25 kN| C{Precision Required}
    B -->|Greater than 25 kN| D[Choose Hydraulic System]
    C -->|High precision| E[Choose Hydraulic Cylinder]
    C -->|Standard precision OK| F{Speed Required}
    F -->|Greater than 1 m/s| G[Choose Pneumatic Cylinder]
    F -->|Less than 1 m/s| H{Environment}
    H -->|Clean, dry indoor| G
    H -->|Dirty, outdoor, hot| D
    G --> I[Compute Air Consumption and FAD]
    D --> J[Compute Pump Flow and Power]
    I --> K[Final Selection Report]
    J --> K
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Schematic: Acceleration Profile and Force Demand

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
