**(iii) Natural Frequency (2 marks):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{31{,}640}{0.05}} = \sqrt{632{,}800} \approx 795.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

In Hz:
$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{795.5}{2\pi} \approx 126.6 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iv) Operating Speed Safety (1 mark):**

Required condition: $\omega_{op} \leq 0.6 \times \omega_n$

$$0.6 \times 795.5 = 477.3 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since the operating speed $\omega_{op} = 250$ rad/s is less than $477.3$ rad/s, the system **can be safely operated** without risk of resonance. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversions** — students frequently forget to convert mm to m before plugging into formulas. Always convert to SI base units first. Marks deducted: 0.5–1 per wrong unit.
> 2. **Forgetting squared terms in diameter ratios** — area ratio is $(d_2/d_1)^2$, not $(d_2/d_1)$. This single mistake can lose 2 full marks.
> 3. **Not sketching the diagram** — for 7-mark theory questions, a labelled diagram is mandatory. Students who skip it lose up to **2 marks** even if the explanation is correct.
> 4. **Confusing stiffness formula** — students sometimes use $k = EI/L^3$ (incorrect for cantilever tip load; correct formula is $k = 3EI/L^3$).
> 5. **Forgetting to verify energy conservation** — in numerical problems on hydraulic amplifiers, the examiner expects explicit verification $P_{in} = P_{out}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Amplifier** = power-modulating device; increases one quantity (force/torque) by decreasing another (velocity/displacement) — never creates energy.
- **Mechanical lever amplification:** $F_{out}/F_{in} = a/b$ (arm ratio); Class-III lever amplifies force, Class-II amplifies speed.
- **Toggle amplification:** $MA = \cot(\alpha)$; very large near dead-centre ($\alpha \to 0$).
- **Hydraulic amplification:** $F_{out}/F_{in} = A_2/A_1 = (d_2/d_1)^2$; velocity reduces by inverse square.
- **Pressure intensifier:** $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot (A_1/A_2)$; boosts pressure by stepped piston.
- **Pneumatic amplifier:** Same principle as hydraulic but compressible fluid; time-constant governed by $RC$.
- **Servo amplifier:** Transfer function $G(s) = K_a/(\tau_a s + 1)$; drives high-current motors from low-current signals.
- **Flexible cam system** = cam mechanism with intentional elastic deformation in cam or follower.
- **Cantilever follower stiffness:** $k = 3EI/L^3 = E w h^3 / (4 L^3)$ for rectangular cross-section.
- **Natural frequency of follower:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$; critical to avoid resonance.
- **Safe operation rule:** $\omega_{op} \leq 0.6 \omega_n$.
- **Advantages of flexible cam systems:** Lower Hertzian contact stress, vibration damping, tolerance insensitivity, energy storage.
- **Types of flexible cam systems:** flexible follower, flexible cam plate, both flexible, elastomeric, compliant.
- **Energy conservation check:** Always verify $P_{in} = P_{out}$ in numerical problems.
- **Standard units:** SI base throughout; mm must be converted to m; GPa to Pa.
- **Key ratio rule:** Area scales as **square** of diameter in circular pistons — never forget this in hydraulic amplifier problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Accuracy and Damping: Dynamic Accuracy and Vibrations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Accuracy and Damping: Dynamic Accuracy and Vibrations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dynamic Accuracy — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Accuracy** is defined as the degree of conformity of an actual output position, velocity, or acceleration of a mechanism to its theoretically desired (commanded) value, evaluated **under operating (in-motion) conditions** as opposed to static (rest) conditions.

In automated machinery, dynamic accuracy is a **time-dependent performance metric**. While a machine may be perfectly aligned and dimensionally accurate when stationary (static accuracy), during high-speed operation, inertial forces, elastic deformations, clearances in joints, and vibrations cause the actual output to deviate from the ideal trajectory. This deviation is the **dynamic error**.

Mathematically, dynamic accuracy can be expressed as:

$$\varepsilon_d(t) = \theta_{actual}(t) - \theta_{commanded}(t)$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_d(t)$ = dynamic error (rad or mm) at time $t$
- $\theta_{actual}(t)$ = actual output displacement
- $\theta_{commanded}(t)$ = ideal/programmed output displacement

The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value of this error over a cycle is a common KTU-exam metric:

$$\varepsilon_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T [\varepsilon_d(t)]^2 \, dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In PERUT743 Module 3, dynamic accuracy is treated as a *system-level property* that depends not only on kinematics but also on stiffness, damping, mass distribution, and excitation frequency of the automated machine.

## 1.2 Vibrations — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration** is the oscillatory motion of a particle, body, or system about an equilibrium position, characterized by its amplitude, frequency, and phase.

In automated machines, vibrations arise because of:
1. **Rotating unbalance** in spindles, cams, and gears.
2. **Reciprocating masses** in slider-crank, cam-follower, and pump mechanisms.
3. **Impacts and chatter** in high-speed pick-and-place robots.
4. **Resonance** when excitation frequency matches a natural frequency.
5. **Compliance errors** in linkages and flexures.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Cup on a Car Dashboard"**

Imagine placing a coffee cup on your car's dashboard. When the car is **stationary**, the cup sits perfectly still — that is **static accuracy**. Now start driving. Every bump, engine vibration, and road irregularity makes the cup shake, slosh, and sometimes spill. That shaking is **vibration**, and the difference between where you *want* the coffee to be (steady in the cup) and where it *actually* is (sloshing up the side) is the **dynamic error**.

In an automated machine, the "bumps" come from the machine's own moving parts (motors, cams, linkages), and the "coffee cup" is the **end-effector** or the **workpiece**. Damping is like the **shock absorbers in the car** — they absorb energy from the bumps and prevent the cup from oscillating wildly.

- **High stiffness (k)** → Less deformation → Better dynamic accuracy.
- **High damping (c)** → Vibration dies out quickly → Less settling time.
- **Low mass (m)** → Higher natural frequency → Easier to operate above disturbances.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

The following **bold** metrics are universally tested:

- **Natural Frequency (ωₙ)** — in **rad/s**
- **Damping Ratio (ζ)** — dimensionless
- **Damped Natural Frequency (ωd)** — in **rad/s**
- **Logarithmic Decrement (δ)** — dimensionless
- **Transmissibility (T)** — dimensionless
- **Critical Speed (N꜀)** — in **rpm**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 5 Damping Categories:** *"Un — Un — Un — Cr — Ov"* → **Un**derdamped, **Un**damped (no), **Un**damped (conservative), **Cr**itically damped, **Ov**erdamped.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Free Vibration of an SDOF Undamped System

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system is the simplest model of a vibrating mechanical element. It consists of:
- A mass **m** (kg)
- A spring of stiffness **k** (N/m)
- (Optionally) a damper **c** (N·s/m)

The equation of motion is derived by applying **Newton's Second Law** to the mass:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

The solution is **simple harmonic motion**:

$$x(t) = X\cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{\dot{x}_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Where the **natural circular frequency** is:

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \;\; \text{[rad/s]}}$$

And natural frequency in Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \;\; \text{[Hz]}$$

## 2.2 Free Vibration of an SDOF Damped System

Adding a viscous damper in parallel with the spring:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

Where the **damping ratio** is:

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \frac{c}{c_c}}$$

And **critical damping coefficient**:

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$$

The solution depends on the value of **ζ**:
- If **ζ < 1** → Underdamped (oscillatory decay)
- If **ζ = 1** → Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory return)
- If **ζ > 1** → Overdamped (slow non-oscillatory return)

**Damped natural frequency** (valid for ζ < 1):

$$\boxed{\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Displacement response for underdamped case:**

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

## 2.3 Forced Vibration with Harmonic Excitation

When a forcing function $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

The **steady-state amplitude** is:

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

Where the **frequency ratio**:

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}$$

**Phase angle:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$$

## 2.4 Resonance

Resonance occurs when the excitation frequency equals the damped natural frequency. The **peak amplitude ratio** is at:

$$r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

Maximum magnification factor (for very light damping, ζ ≈ 0):

$$MF_{max} \approx \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

## 2.5 Vibration Transmissibility (Isolation)

For a machine mounted on a flexible base, the **transmissibility** is the ratio of transmitted force to applied force:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

For **r > √2**, T < 1 → isolation region.

## 2.6 Logarithmic Decrement (Experimental Damping Measurement)

The **logarithmic decrement** is measured from successive peak amplitudes in free-decay vibration:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

For small damping (ζ < 0.2):

$$\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta \approx \frac{\delta}{2\pi}$$

## 2.7 Critical Speed of a Rotating Shaft

A shaft with a disc of mass **m** at its mid-span, supported on flexible bearings, has stiffness **k**:

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \;\;\text{rpm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Critical speed calculations are vital for **high-speed spindles** in CNC machines, **turbine rotors**, and **robot arm shafts**. Operating at or near critical speed causes catastrophic whirl and failure.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Linear, SDOF, undamped |
| 2 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | Viscous damping only |
| 3 | Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m | Defines ζ = 1 |
| 4 | Damped natural freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | ζ < 1 |
| 5 | Steady-state amplitude | $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m | Forced vibration |
| 6 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | dimensionless | — |
| 7 | Transmissibility | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | r > √2 isolates |
| 8 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n)$ | dimensionless | Used experimentally |
| 9 | ζ from log decrement | $\zeta = \delta / (2\pi)$ | dimensionless | ζ < 0.2 |
| 10 | Critical speed | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | rpm | Rotating shaft |
| 11 | Period of vibration | $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ | s | Damped |
| 12 | Energy decay per cycle | $\Delta E/E = 1 - e^{-2\delta}$ | — | Free vibration |
| 13 | Magnification factor | $MF = 1 / \sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | dimensionless | At resonance peak |
| 14 | Dynamic stiffness | $k_d = k\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | N/m | Frequency-dependent |
| 15 | Equivalent system mass (parallel) | $1/m_{eq} = 1/m_1 + 1/m_2$ | kg | Spring-mass combo |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always check whether the question asks for **undamped natural frequency (ωₙ)** or **damped natural frequency (ωd)**. Marks are lost when students confuse the two, especially when ζ > 0.1.

## 2.9 Engineering Applications in Automated Machines

- **CNC Machining Centers** — Spindle vibration monitoring prevents tool chatter and surface roughness defects.
- **Pick-and-Place Robots** — End-effector vibration after motion command causes positional errors; damping reduces settling time.
- **Cam-Follower Systems** — High acceleration profiles excite structural modes; stiffness and damping must be matched.
- **Packaging Machinery** — Reciprocating cams create periodic forcing; isolation mounts protect electronics.
- **Precision Positioning Stages** — Flexure-based stages need critical damping to avoid overshoot.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equation of Motion of an SDOF Spring-Mass-Damper System

### Setup
Consider a mass **m** resting on a frictionless surface, attached to a wall through a spring of stiffness **k** and a viscous damper of coefficient **c**. Let **x** be the displacement from the static equilibrium position.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Analysis

The forces acting on the mass are:
1. **Inertial force** (Newton's 2nd law): $F_I = m\ddot{x}$ (acts opposite to acceleration)
2. **Damping force** (opposes velocity): $F_D = c\dot{x}$
3. **Spring restoring force** (opposes displacement): $F_S = kx$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (D'Alembert's Principle)

For dynamic equilibrium, the sum of forces on the mass equals mass × acceleration:

$$m\ddot{x} = -c\dot{x} - kx$$

The negative signs indicate that spring and damping forces **oppose** the motion.

### Step 3 — Rearrange to Standard Form

Bring all terms to the LHS:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

> This is the **homogeneous, second-order, linear, ordinary differential equation** governing free vibration of an SDOF damped system.

### Step 4 — Divide by m to Obtain the Canonical Form

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

Define:

$$\frac{k}{m} = \omega_n^2 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{c}{m} = 2\zeta\omega_n$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \zeta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

The canonical form becomes:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

### Step 5 — Assume Exponential Solution

Let $x(t) = X e^{st}$. Substituting:

$$(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)X e^{st} = 0$$

The **characteristic equation** is:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

### Step 6 — Solve the Characteristic Equation (Quadratic Formula)

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

The roots depend on the value of $(\zeta^2 - 1)$:
- If $\zeta < 1$ → $s$ is complex → **underdamped** (oscillatory)
- If $\zeta = 1$ → repeated real root → **critically damped**
- If $\zeta > 1$ → two distinct real roots → **overdamped**

### Step 7 — Underdamped Solution (ζ < 1)

Let $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. The roots are:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_d$$

Therefore the displacement is:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right)$$

Where A and B are determined by initial conditions:
- At $t = 0$: $x(0) = x_0 \Rightarrow A = x_0$
- At $t = 0$: $\dot{x}(0) = v_0 \Rightarrow B = v_0/\omega_d$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(x_0 \cos\omega_d t + \frac{v_0}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right)}$$

This is the complete time response of an underdamped SDOF system — a **decaying sinusoid**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Amplitude of Forced Vibration

### Step 1 — Equation of Motion with Harmonic Forcing

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2 — Assume a Steady-State Trial Solution

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Derivatives:
- $\dot{x}_p = X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$
- $\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Equation of Motion

$$-mX\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + cX\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + kX \sin(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Expanding $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi$ and matching coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$:

**Coefficient of $\sin\omega t$:**

$$(k - m\omega^2)X \cos\phi + cX\omega \sin\phi = F_0$$

**Coefficient of $\cos\omega t$:**

$$-(k - m\omega^2)X \sin\phi + cX\omega \cos\phi = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Two Simultaneous Equations

Squaring and adding eliminates $\phi$:

$$(k - m\omega^2)^2 X^2 + (cX\omega)^2 = F_0^2$$

$$X^2\left[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2\right] = F_0^2$$

### Step 5 — Isolate the Amplitude X

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

### Step 6 — Normalize Using $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\zeta$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$:

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

This is the **standard steady-state amplitude** used universally in KTU problems.

### Step 7 — Phase Angle Derivation

Dividing the two coefficient equations:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Logarithmic Decrement and its Connection to Damping Ratio

### Step 1 — Express Successive Peak Amplitudes

For an underdamped system, peaks occur approximately at $t = 0, T_d, 2T_d, \ldots$ (where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$). The amplitude at the $n^{th}$ peak is:

$$x_n = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (nT_d)}$$

### Step 2 — Take the Ratio of Two Successive Peaks

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n nT_d}}{e^{-\zeta\omega_n (n+1)T_d}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

### Step 3 — Take the Natural Logarithm

$$\ln\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$\ln\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For **n cycles**, the log decrement is:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

For small ζ (< 0.2), $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$, so:

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta \approx \frac{\delta}{2\pi}}$$

This is the **engineering shortcut** used when measuring damping from free-decay tests.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Derivation of Transmissibility

### Step 1 — Equation of Motion of a Base-Excited System

Let $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$ be the base motion. The mass displacement is $x(t)$. The spring deformation is $(x - y)$ and the damper deformation rate is $(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$.

$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $y = Y\sin\omega t$

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{y} + ky = Y(c\omega\cos\omega t + k\sin\omega t)$$

### Step 3 — Force Transmitted to the Foundation

The total force transmitted to the base is the sum of spring and damper forces:

$$F_T = k(x - y) + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Steady-State Amplitudes

The amplitude of the transmitted force is:

$$F_T = Y\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Wait — let me re-derive cleanly. The ratio of transmitted amplitude to input amplitude is:

$$T = \frac{|F_T|}{|F_0|} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

This is the **transmissibility ratio**.

### Step 5 — Condition for Isolation (T < 1)

For T < 1:

$$(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 > 1 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 > 1$$

$$1 - 2r^2 + r^4 > 1$$

$$r^2(r^2 - 2) > 0$$

Therefore, isolation requires $r > \sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $\omega > \sqrt{2}\,\omega_n$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The √2 Rule:** A vibration isolator is effective **only** when the excitation frequency exceeds $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency of the isolated system. Below this, the isolator actually *amplifies* the transmitted force.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Undamped vs Damped vs Forced

| Property | Undamped Free | Damped Free | Forced Harmonic |
|----------|---------------|-------------|-----------------|
| EOM | $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin\omega t$ |
| Solution type | Pure sinusoid | Decaying sinusoid (ζ<1) | Steady-state sinusoid + transient |
| Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | $\omega$ (excitation) |
| Amplitude | Constant | Decays as $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ | $X = F_0/k \div \sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ |
| Energy | Conserved | Dissipated at rate $c\dot{x}^2$ | Input − Dissipated = Steady-state balance |
| Phase | 0 or π | 0 or π | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(2\zeta r / (1-r^2))$ |
| Critical case | None | ζ = 1 (critically damped) | ω = ωₙ (resonance) |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 50 kg machine component is supported on a spring of stiffness $2 \times 10^5$ N/m. When the system is given an initial displacement of 10 mm and released from rest, the amplitude reduces to 4 mm after 5 complete cycles. Find:
(a) The damping ratio ζ
(b) The logarithmic decrement δ
(c) The damped natural frequency
(d) The actual viscous damping coefficient c

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right) = \frac{1}{5}\ln\left(\frac{10}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{5}(0.9163) = 0.1833$$

**Step 3 — Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{2\pi} = \frac{0.1833}{6.2832} = 0.0292$$

**Step 4 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 63.25 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.000852} \approx 63.22 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Actual damping coefficient:**

$$c = 2\zeta m\omega_n = 2(0.0292)(50)(63.25) = 184.7 \;\; \text{N·s/m}$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Vibration Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **engineering workflow** for analyzing and mitigating vibration in an automated machine to achieve the desired dynamic accuracy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Motion Command"] --> B["Mechanism Kinematic Model"]
    B --> C["Equivalent Mass m"]
    B --> D["Equivalent Stiffness k"]
    B --> E["Equivalent Damping c"]
    C --> F["SDOF Lumped Parameter Model"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Equation of Motion m*x_ddot + c*x_dot + k*x = F(t)"]
    G --> H{"Type of Excitation?"}
    H -->|"Free"| I["Compute natural frequency wn and damping ratio zeta"]
    H -->|"Forced Harmonic"| J["Compute frequency ratio r = w / wn"]
    I --> K["Damped frequency wd = wn*sqrt 1 - zeta^2"]
    J --> L{"Check Resonance r approx 1?"}
    L -->|"Yes"| M["Amplify Vibration Add Damping Increase Stiffness"]
    L -->|"No, r > sqrt 2"| N["Vibration Isolation Effective"]
    L -->|"No, r < sqrt 2"| O["Tune Stiffness to Avoid Amplification"]
    K --> P["Time Response x t = e^(-zeta*wn*t) * cosine wd*t"]
    M --> Q["Design Output: Damping Coefficient"]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R["Verify Dynamic Error less than Tolerance"]
    R --> S["Final Machine Design with Specified Dynamic Accuracy"]
```

## 4.2 Damping Classification Decision Tree

The classification of an SDOF system by its damping ratio is critical for response prediction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Damping Coefficient c"] --> S1["Compute Critical Damping c_c = 2*sqrt k*m"]
    S1 --> S2{"Compare c with c_c"}
    S2 -->|"c = 0"| Z0["Undamped No Dissipation Pure Oscillation"]
    S2 -->|"0 < c < c_c"| Z1["Underdamped zeta between 0 and 1 Decaying Oscillation"]
    S2 -->|"c = c_c"| Z2["Critically Damped zeta = 1 Fastest Return without Oscillation"]
    S2 -->|"c > c_c"| Z3["Overdamped zeta greater than 1 Slow Return without Oscillation"]
    Z0 --> X0["Amplitude remains constant"]
    Z1 --> X1["Amplitude decays as e^(-zeta*wn*t)"]
    Z2 --> X2["Returns to equilibrium without overshoot"]
    Z3 --> X3["Returns slowly without oscillation"]
```

## 4.3 Forced Vibration Response Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Response Zones
        R0["Low Frequency r much less than 1 Quasi-Static X approx F0/k"]
        R1["Resonance r approx 1 Peak Amplitude"]
        R2["Isolation r greater than sqrt 2 Transmissibility less than 1"]
    end
    Z1["Stiffness-Dominated k governs"]
    Z2["Damping-Governed c dominates"]
    Z3["Inertia-Dominated m governs"]
    R0 --> Z1
    R1 --> Z2
    R2 --> Z3
```

## 4.5 Dynamic Accuracy Error Budget Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0["Total Dynamic Error epsilon_total"] --> E1["Kinematic Synthesis Error"]
    E0 --> E2["Elastic Deformation Error"]
    E0 --> E3["Backlash and Clearance Error"]
    E0 --> E4["Vibration-Induced Error"]
    E0 --> E5["Thermal Expansion Error"]
    E1 --> E11["Computed from Loop Closure Equations"]
```
