# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **damping ratio**. Mention its significance in the dynamic analysis of automated machines.

**Model Answer:**

The damping ratio $\zeta$ is defined as the ratio of the actual damping coefficient $c$ to the critical damping coefficient $c_c$ of a vibrating system:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n}$$

**Significance in automated machines:**
- It determines the **rate of decay** of free vibration: amplitude reduces by a factor $e^{-2\pi\zeta}$ per cycle.
- It governs whether the system is **underdamped (ζ < 1)**, **critically damped (ζ = 1)**, or **overdamped (ζ > 1)**.
- In pick-and-place robots and CNC machines, ζ close to 0.7 ensures **fastest settling** with minimal overshoot, improving dynamic accuracy.
- It controls the **resonance peak height**; higher ζ reduces peak amplitude by $1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$ at resonance.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is **logarithmic decrement**? Write its expression and explain how it is used to compute the damping ratio experimentally.

**Model Answer:**

The logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes (separated by $n$ cycles) in a free-decay vibration:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

**Experimental procedure:**
1. Give the system a small initial displacement $x_0$ and release it.
2. Record $n$ successive peak amplitudes $x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
3. Compute $\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln(x_0/x_n)$.
4. For lightly damped systems (ζ < 0.2), compute the damping ratio as:

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{2\pi}$$

This method is widely used to characterize the **damping properties** of materials, joints, and structural members in automated machinery.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing $\omega_n$ (undamped natural frequency) with $\omega_d$ (damped natural frequency) when $\zeta > 0.1$. Always compute $\omega_d$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s (multiply by $2\pi/60$) when computing critical speed or frequency ratio.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using the wrong stiffness/mass for parallel or series combinations.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In transmissibility problems, missing the condition $r > \sqrt{2}$ for effective isolation.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting units — **SI units strictly** (N, m, kg, s).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

A precision positioning stage for an automated assembly machine is modelled as a single degree of freedom system with mass $m = 12$ kg supported by a spring of stiffness $k = 3 \times 10^4$ N/m and a viscous damper.

**(a)** [7 Marks] The system is observed to have a damping ratio of $\zeta = 0.15$. Determine:
(i) The undamped and damped natural frequencies (in rad/s and Hz).
(ii) The actual damping coefficient $c$.
(iii) The critical damping coefficient $c_c$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A harmonic force $F(t) = 200\sin(80t)$ N is applied to the system. Determine:
(i) The frequency ratio $r$.
(ii) The steady-state amplitude of vibration.
(iii) The phase angle $\phi$.
(iv) Comment on whether the system is operating in the **isolation** or **amplification** region.

**Complete Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Undamped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^4}{12}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 50\sqrt{1 - 0.0225} = 50\sqrt{0.9775} = 49.44 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Hz:**

$$f_n = \frac{50}{2\pi} = 7.958 \;\; \text{Hz}, \quad f_d = \frac{49.44}{2\pi} = 7.870 \;\; \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Damping coefficient:**

$$c = 2\zeta m\omega_n = 2(0.15)(12)(50) = 180 \;\; \text{N·s/m}$$

**Step 5 — Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2(12)(50) = 1200 \;\; \text{N·s/m}$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Identify forcing parameters:**

$F_0 = 200$ N, $\omega = 80$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Frequency ratio:**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{80}{50} = 1.6$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state amplitude:**

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{200/30000}{\sqrt{(1-2.56)^2 + (2 \times 0.15 \times 1.6)^2}}$$

$$X = \frac{0.00667}{\sqrt{(-1.56)^2 + (0.48)^2}} = \frac{0.00667}{\sqrt{2.4336 + 0.2304}} = \frac{0.00667}{\sqrt{2.664}}$$

$$X = \frac{0.00667}{1.6322} = 4.086 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m} = 4.086 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Phase angle:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.48}{-1.56}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.3077)$$

Since denominator is negative, the angle is in the 2nd quadrant:

$$\phi = 180° - 17.1° = 162.9° \;\;\text{or}\;\; 2.843 \;\; \text{rad}$$

**Step 5 — Region of operation:**

Since $r = 1.6 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the system is in the **ISOLATION region**.

Transmissibility check:

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.2304}{2.4336 + 0.2304}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2304}{2.664}} = \sqrt{0.4620} = 0.680$$

Since $T < 1$, isolation is confirmed — the transmitted force is 68% of the applied force.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A high-speed rotor of mass 25 kg is mounted on a flexible shaft. The system has a stiffness of $1.5 \times 10^5$ N/m and is observed to vibrate with a logarithmic decrement of 0.4.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Determine:
(i) The damping ratio $\zeta$ and the damping coefficient $c$.
(ii) The undamped natural frequency and the damped natural frequency.
(iii) The critical speed of the rotor in rpm.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The amplitude of vibration in free decay is observed to reduce from 8 mm to 1.5 mm. Determine:
(i) The number of cycles required for this decay.
(ii) The time taken for the amplitude to reduce to 0.5 mm.
(iii) The percentage reduction in amplitude per cycle.

**Complete Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Damping ratio (from log decrement):**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{2\pi} = \frac{0.4}{2\pi} = \frac{0.4}{6.2832} = 0.0637$$

**Step 2 — Damping coefficient:**

$$c = 2\zeta m\omega_n$$

First, compute $\omega_n$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{25}} = \sqrt{6000} = 77.46 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

Therefore:

$$c = 2(0.0637)(25)(77.46) = 246.7 \;\; \text{N·s/m}$$

**Step 3 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 77.46\sqrt{1 - 0.00406} = 77.30 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Critical speed:**

$$N_c = \frac{60 \omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 77.46}{2\pi} = \frac{4647.6}{6.2832} = 739.7 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Number of cycles for decay from 8 mm to 1.5 mm:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

$$n = \frac{1}{\delta}\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right) = \frac{1}{0.4}\ln\left(\frac{8}{1.5}\right) = \frac{1}{0.4}(1.6740) = 4.185$$

So $n \approx 4.2$ cycles. For complete integer cycles, 5 cycles are needed.

**Step 2 — Time for amplitude to reduce to 0.5 mm:**

The damped period is:

$$T_d = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi}{77.30} = 0.0813 \;\; \text{s}$$

Number of cycles to reduce from 8 mm to 0.5 mm:

$$n = \frac{1}{0.4}\ln\left(\frac{8}{0.5}\right) = \frac{1}{0.4}(2.7726) = 6.93 \;\; \text{cycles}$$

Time required:

$$t = n \cdot T_d = 6.93 \times 0.0813 = 0.563 \;\; \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction in amplitude per cycle:**

Per cycle, amplitude reduces by a factor of $e^{-\delta}$:

$$\frac{x_{n+1}}{x_n} = e^{-\delta} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

Therefore, the reduction per cycle = $1 - 0.6703 = 0.3297 = 32.97\%$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU ESE)**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Dynamic Accuracy** = conformity of actual to commanded output **in motion** (RMS error metric).
- [ ] **Vibration** = oscillatory motion about equilibrium; characterized by amplitude, frequency, phase.
- [ ] **Damping Ratio ζ** = $c / c_c$ — dimensionless; defines under/critically/over-damped regimes.
- [ ] **Critical Damping $c_c$** = $2m\omega_n$ = $2\sqrt{km}$ — minimum damping for no oscillation.
- [ ] **Natural Frequency $\omega_n$** = $\sqrt{k/m}$ — intrinsic to mass-spring system.
- [ ] **Damped Frequency $\omega_d$** = $\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — actual oscillation frequency when ζ < 1.
- [ ] **Frequency Ratio r** = $\omega / \omega_n$ — controls forced response magnitude.

### Key Engineering Rules
- [ ] **√2 Rule:** Transmissibility T < 1 only when $r > \sqrt{2}$. Always check this in isolation problems.
- [ ] **Resonance:** Peak amplitude occurs near $r = 1$ (more precisely at $\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$).
- [ ] **Critical Damping for Fastest Settling:** ζ = 1 (theoretical); practical optimum is **ζ = 0.7** for control systems.
- [ ] **Log Decrement** = $2\pi\zeta$ (for small ζ); used in **experimental damping measurement**.

### Common KTU Numerical Patterns
- [ ] Compute $\omega_n$ from $k$ and $m$ first — it's the **foundation** for all subsequent calculations.
- [ ] Always state **units explicitly** (rad/s, Hz, N·s/m, m, mm).
- [ ] Convert rpm ↔ rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
- [ ] In forced vibration, plot or comment on the three regions: quasi-static, resonance, isolation.
- [ ] When system has **multiple springs/masses**, use parallel-series formulas:
  - Springs in parallel: $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$
  - Springs in series: $1/k_{eq} = 1/k_1 + 1/k_2$

### High-Frequency KTU Topics (Module 3)
- [ ] **Critical speed of shafts** — direct application of $\omega_c = \sqrt{k/m}$.
- [ ] **Vibration isolation of machine foundations** — transmissibility calculation.
- [ ] **Cam-driven system dynamics** — residual vibration after follower return.
- [ ] **Robot arm end-effector vibration** — modelled as SDOF at the tool point.
- [ ] **Dynamic error budget** — combine kinematic, elastic, clearance, and vibration errors.

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] **Don't confuse $k$** (spring stiffness, N/m) with $k_d$ (dynamic stiffness, frequency-dependent).
- [ ] **Don't confuse natural frequency** with **excitation frequency** — they are independent.
- [ ] **Don't forget** that damping does **not** change $\omega_n$ — only $\omega_d$ and the amplitude decay rate.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Damping ratio formula is $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$, not $c/\sqrt{km}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Note:** Module 3 of PERUT743 emphasizes the **practical integration** of vibration theory with machine design. Expect at least one **14-mark problem** combining $\omega_n$ calculation, damping measurement via log decrement, and either (a) forced vibration amplitude or (b) critical speed computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damping: Electrical and Mechanical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Damping: Electrical and Mechanical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Damping** in the context of automated mechanism design is defined as the *controlled dissipation of vibrational energy* from a dynamic system, achieved through mechanisms that convert kinetic energy into heat (thermal energy), electrical energy, or other irreversible forms. Mathematically, it is represented by the **damping coefficient** (also called the **viscous damping constant**), denoted by the symbol $c$, with units of $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, damping is broadly classified into two engineering families:

1. **Mechanical Damping** — Energy dissipation achieved purely through physical contact, friction, fluid shear, or internal material hysteresis.
2. **Electrical Damping** — Energy dissipation achieved through electromagnetic induction, eddy current generation, back-EMF feedback, or resistive heating of induced currents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In automated machinery, damping is not an optional feature. It is a *design specification* that directly governs machine positioning accuracy, settling time, servo-loop stability, and fatigue life. A mechanism without adequate damping is treated as *unacceptable* in any closed-loop automated system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a friend on a playground swing. If you stop pushing, the swing keeps oscillating for a long time before stopping — that is an **underdamped** system (low damping).

Now, picture the same swing lowered into a swimming pool of thick oil. Each oscillation would be drastically reduced because the viscous oil resists motion, and the swing would stop within a very few cycles. The oil provides **mechanical viscous damping**.

A third analogy: if you attached a small permanent magnet on the swing and let it swing near a copper plate, you would notice a similar quieting effect. Why? The moving magnet induces **eddy currents** in the copper plate, and by Lenz's Law, these currents generate a magnetic field that opposes motion. This is **electrical (eddy current) damping**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Both mechanical and electrical damping achieve the *same physical goal* — energy removal from the system — but through *different physical pathways*. This is why one of the most important skills in automated mechanism design is the ability to **convert** a mechanical damper into its **electrical analog** and vice versa, allowing designers to use powerful electrical circuit analysis tools to predict mechanical behavior.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following terms will recur throughout this module and must be memorized:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Damping coefficient | $c$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$ | Direct measure of energy dissipation rate |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta$ | dimensionless | Classifies system as under/critical/over-damped |
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Undamped oscillation rate |
| Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Actual oscillation rate with damping |
| Logarithmic decrement | $\delta$ | dimensionless | Measure of decay per cycle |
| Mass | $m$ | $\text{kg}$ | Inertia property |
| Stiffness | $k$ | $\text{N/m}$ | Restoring force per unit displacement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Damped Second-Order System

The vast majority of automated mechanisms (robotic arms, CNC slides, conveyor drives, pick-and-place heads) are mathematically modeled as a **mass–spring–damper system**. The governing differential equation for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) damped system subjected to an external forcing $F(t)$ is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

Where:
- $m$ = effective mass (kg) of the moving element
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient ($\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$)
- $k$ = effective stiffness ($\text{N/m}$)
- $x(t)$ = displacement from equilibrium
- $\dot{x}(t)$ = velocity
- $\ddot{x}(t)$ = acceleration

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Inertia Term ($m\ddot{x}$):** Represents Newton's Second Law. Without it, the system has no memory of acceleration.
- **Step 2 — Damping Term ($c\dot{x}$):** The energy-dissipation term. It is *proportional to velocity*, which is the defining characteristic of **viscous damping**.
- **Step 3 — Stiffness Term ($kx$):** Hooke's Law restoring force pulling the system back to equilibrium.
- **Step 4 — Forcing $F(t)$:** External input, could be a step, impulse, sine sweep, or arbitrary servo command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why proportional to velocity?** Energy dissipated by viscous friction (and by electrical resistance acting on induced currents) is computed as $P = F \cdot v$. If the resistive force $F$ is proportional to $v$, then $P \propto v^2$, which is always positive — energy is irreversibly lost as heat. This is the physical basis of *all linear damping models*.

---

## 2.2 The Characteristic Equation & Damping Classification

Setting $F(t) = 0$ and assuming a trial solution $x(t) = e^{st}$ leads to the **characteristic equation**:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

The two roots are:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

The discriminant $\Delta = c^2 - 4mk$ determines the system's dynamic class:

| Condition | Discriminant | Root Type | Physical Behavior |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $c^2 < 4mk$ | $\Delta < 0$ | Complex conjugates | **Underdamped** — system oscillates with decaying amplitude |
| $c^2 = 4mk$ | $\Delta = 0$ | Real, repeated | **Critically damped** — fastest return without oscillation |
| $c^2 > 4mk$ | $\Delta > 0$ | Real, distinct, negative | **Overdamped** — slow return, no oscillation |

---

## 2.3 The Damping Ratio $\zeta$ — The Most Important Non-Dimensional Number

Dividing the characteristic equation by $m$ and recognizing standard definitions:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}, \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

The equation becomes the canonical second-order form:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = \frac{F(t)}{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Most KTU problems in PERUT743 will give you $m$, $k$, and $c$, and ask for $\zeta$ and $\omega_d$. Memorize the four "magic" definitions: $\omega_n$, $\zeta$, $\omega_d$, and $c_c$ (critical damping coefficient).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Automated Machinery

| Industry Domain | Application | Damping Type Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CNC Machining Centers | Tool chatter suppression | Viscous fluid + eddy current |
| Robotic Surgery Arms | Tremor reduction | Electromagnetic back-EMF |
| Pick-and-Place Heads | Settling time reduction | Viscoelastic polymer pads |
| Servo Press Systems | Overshoot control | Hydraulic dashpot |
| Semiconductor Lithography | Vibration isolation | Electromagnetic active damping |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exam)

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rendering Safeguard:** The vertical bar $\vert$ is used in absolute-value and condition expressions to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Undamped oscillation rate |
| 2 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | dimensionless | Energy loss per cycle |
| 3 | Critical damping coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk}$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$ | Minimum $c$ to prevent oscillation |
| 4 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Actual oscillation rate when $\zeta < 1$ |
| 5 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless | Decay ratio of successive peaks |
| 6 | Decay envelope | $x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ | $\text{m}$ | Amplitude at time $t$ |
| 7 | Quality factor (Q-factor) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | dimensionless | Sharpness of resonance |
| 8 | Settling time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}$ | $\text{s}$ | Time to enter $\pm 2\%$ band |
| 9 | Peak time | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d}$ | $\text{s}$ | Time to first overshoot peak |
| 10 | Percent overshoot | $\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | $\text{\%}$ | Maximum overshoot magnitude |

---

## 2.6 Mechanical vs. Electrical Damping — Engineering Comparison

| Aspect | Mechanical Damping | Electrical Damping |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy sink | Heat via friction/viscosity | Heat via $I^2R$ in resistor/EMF |
| Linearity | Often non-linear (Coulomb) | Easily linear (ohmic) |
| Temperature sensitivity | High (oil viscosity) | Low (metal resistivity) |
| Tunability | Limited, mechanical rework | Easy, change resistor value |
| Reliability | Subject to wear, leakage | No contact, very reliable |
| Typical KTU application | Dashpots, viscoelastic pads | Eddy-current brakes, back-EMF motors |
| Governing law | Newton's friction laws | Ohm's + Faraday's + Lenz's law |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Damped Natural Frequency $\omega_d$

**Starting Point:** Characteristic equation of the undamped-free SDOF system:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

**Step 1 — Divide through by $m$ to normalize:**

$$s^2 + \frac{c}{m}s + \frac{k}{m} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the canonical definitions $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:**

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the quadratic formula:**

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $2\omega_n$ from inside the square root:**

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm 2\omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the factor of 2:**

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 6 — For the underdamped case ($\zeta < 1$), the expression under the root becomes negative. Convert to imaginary form using $i^2 = -1$:**

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the imaginary part as the damped natural frequency:**

$$\boxed{\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

This quantity is the angular frequency at which the *amplitude envelope decays* through the system's oscillation cycles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Logarithmic Decrement $\delta$

**Starting Point:** Underdamped free-response solution for displacement:

$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

**Step 1 — Identify two successive peak amplitudes.** Peaks occur when the cosine term equals $+1$, separated by one full period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$.

$$x_1 = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}, \quad x_2 = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_1 + T_d)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of consecutive peaks:**

$$\frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}}{e^{-\zeta\omega_n(t_1 + T_d)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$:**

$$\frac{x_1}{x_2} = e^{2\pi\zeta\omega_n/\omega_d}$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_d}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Invert to solve for $\zeta$ in terms of measured peaks (used in experiments):**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$$

This is the equation an experimentalist uses to *measure* damping from an oscilloscope trace.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electrical Analogy (Force–Voltage & Force–Current)

**Why an analogy?** Because electrical circuits are easier to analyze, design, and tune using well-developed theory (Laplace transforms, Bode plots, root locus), engineers routinely build **electrical analog circuits** to model mechanical systems.

**Starting Point:** Mechanical equation with displacement $x$ as the across-variable analog of voltage.

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the structural isomorphism.** Each term on the left-hand side has the form: *(Inertia property) × (2nd derivative of across-variable) + (Dissipative property) × (1st derivative) + (Storage property) × (variable) = (through-variable)*.

**Step 2 — Map mechanical quantities to electrical quantities using the Impedance Analogy (Force–Voltage):**

| Mechanical | Electrical | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Mass $m$ | Inductance $L$ | $L$ |
| Damper $c$ | Resistance $R$ | $R$ |
| Spring $k$ | Inverse Capacitance $1/C$ | $1/C$ |
| Displacement $x$ | Charge $q$ | $q$ |
| Velocity $\dot{x}$ | Current $i$ | $i$ |
| Force $F$ | Voltage $V$ | $V$ |

**Step 3 — Write the analogous series RLC electrical equation.** For a series RLC circuit driven by voltage $V(t)$:

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri + \frac{1}{C}\int i\,dt = V(t)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate once with respect to time to make it formally identical to the mechanical equation:**

$$L\frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}i = \frac{dV(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Compare term by term with the mechanical equation:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t) \quad\longleftrightarrow\quad L\frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}i = \frac{dV(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-to-One Mapping Established:**
> - $m \leftrightarrow L$
> - $c \leftrightarrow R$
> - $k \leftrightarrow 1/C$
> - $F(t) \leftrightarrow dV/dt$

**Step 6 — Derive the analogous natural frequency and damping ratio:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}, \quad \zeta = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$

These are *exactly* the formulas KTU students must know for filter design and resonance analysis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Back-EMF Damping in a DC Servomotor (Electrical Damping in Automation)

**Starting Point:** The armature circuit of a DC motor with armature resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, applied voltage $V_a$, and back-EMF $e_b = K_b \omega_m$:

$$V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + K_b \omega_m$$

**Step 1 — The electromagnetic torque produced:**

$$T_m = K_t i_a$$

**Step 2 — The mechanical equation of the rotor load (combined inertia $J$, viscous friction $B$):**

$$J\dot{\omega}_m + B\omega_m = T_m = K_t i_a$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state analysis:** Assume $L_a$ is small (negligible) and the motor has reached constant speed. Then $di_a/dt = 0$, and from the electrical equation:

$$i_a = \frac{V_a - K_b \omega_m}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the mechanical equation:**

$$J\dot{\omega}_m + B\omega_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a}(V_a - K_b \omega_m)$$

**Step 5 — Collect $\omega_m$ terms:**

$$J\dot{\omega}_m + \left(B + \frac{K_t K_b}{R_a}\right)\omega_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a}V_a$$

**Step 6 — Identify the effective electrical damping coefficient:**

$$\boxed{c_{\text{electrical}} = B + \frac{K_t K_b}{R_a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The term $K_t K_b / R_a$ is *electrical damping* added to the inherent mechanical friction $B$. By choosing a motor with high $K_t$ and low $R_a$, the designer can effectively make a *non-contacting* viscous damper — the *hallmark* of precision automation. This is why DC servomotors are the workhorse of automated machinery: **the back-EMF itself is the damper**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU Past Year Style):**
A robotic arm link has effective mass $m = 5 \text{ kg}$, stiffness $k = 2000 \text{ N/m}$, and viscous damping $c = 60 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}$. Determine:
(a) Natural frequency, (b) Damping ratio, (c) Damped natural frequency, (d) System class.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2000}{5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (b) — Damping ratio:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{5 \times 2000} = 2\sqrt{10000} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.3$$

**Part (c) — Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.09} = 20\sqrt{0.91} = 20 \times 0.9539 = 19.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (d) — Classification:** Since $\zeta = 0.3 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verifying with the alternative form of the discriminant:** $c^2 = 3600$ and $4mk = 4 \times 5 \times 2000 = 40000$. Since $c^2 < 4mk$, the system is underdamped. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Damping in Automated Machines

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Energy Dissipation in Automated Mechanisms] --> B[Mechanical Damping]
    A --> C[Electrical Damping]

    B --> B1[Viscous Damping]
    B --> B2[Coulomb Dry Friction Damping]
    B --> B3[Material Hysteresis Damping]
    B --> B4[Fluid Squeeze Film Damping]

    B1 --> B1A[Oil Dashpot in Hydraulic Press]
    B1 --> B1B[Air Damper in Pick and Place Head]

    B2 --> B2A[Sliding Joint of CNC Slide]
    B2 --> B2B[Brake Pad Engagement]

    B3 --> B3A[Rubber Mounts in Robotic Arm]
    B3 --> B3B[Viscoelastic Pads in Tool Holder]

    B4 --> B4A[Aerostatic Bearing in Precision Slide]
    B4 --> B4B[Squeeze Film Damper in Turbo Machinery]

    C --> C1[Back EMF Damping]
    C --> C2[Eddy Current Damping]
    C --> C3[Magnetorheological Damping]
    C --> C4[Shunt Resistor on Inductive Load]

    C1 --> C1A[DC Servomotor in Robotic Joint]
    C1 --> C1B[Brushless DC Drive in Conveyor]

    C2 --> C2A[Eddy Current Brake in Trolley]
    C2 --> C2B[Copper Damper Plate in Galvanometer]

    C3 --> C3A[Variable Stiffness Mount in Vehicle Seat]
    C3 --> C3B[Smart Damper in Active Suspension]

    C4 --> C4A[Freewheel Diode across Solenoid]
    C4 --> C4B[Snubber Network on Relay Contacts]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanical-to-Electrical Analogy Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Mechanical Domain
        M1[Mass m in kg]:::mech
        M2[Damper c in Ns per m]:::mech
        M3[Spring k in N per m]:::mech
        M4[Force F of t]:::mech
        M5[Displacement x of t]:::mech
    end

    subgraph Equivalence Layer
        EQ1[Impedance Analogy Mapping]:::eq
    end

    subgraph Electrical Domain
        E1[Inductance L in H]:::elec
        E2[Resistance R in ohm]:::elec
        E3[Capacitance C in F]:::elec
        E4[Voltage V of t]:::elec
        E5[Charge q of t]:::elec
    end

    M1 --- EQ1
    M2 --- EQ1
    M3 --- EQ1
    M4 --- EQ1
    M5 --- EQ1

    EQ1 --- E1
    EQ1 --- E2
    EQ1 --- E3
    EQ1 --- E4
    EQ1 --- E5

    M1 -. m maps to L .-> E1
    M2 -. c maps to R .-> E2
    M3 -. k maps to 1 over C .-> E3
    M4 -. F maps to V .-> E4
    M5 -. x maps to q .-> E5

    classDef mech fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000000
    classDef elec fill:#9CC4FF,stroke:#003080,color:#000000
    classDef eq fill:#C2F0C2,stroke:#006020,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence-Style Flow — Energy Flow Path in a Damped System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: External Force Applied to Mass] --> P2[Step 2: Mass Acquires Kinetic Energy]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Velocity Induces Damping Force]
    P3 --> P4{Mechanism Type}
    P4 -- Mechanical Path --> P5A[Step 4a: Viscous Shear Converts KE to Heat in Oil]
    P4 -- Electrical Path --> P5B[Step 4b: Motion Induces Back EMF or Eddy Currents]
    P5B --> P6B[Step 5b: Induced Current Dissipates in Resistance as I Squared R Heat]
    P5A --> P6A[Step 5a: Thermal Energy Radiates to Environment]
    P6A --> P7[Step 6: System Amplitude Decays Exponentially]
    P6B --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: Oscillation Envelope Reaches Steady State]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Style:** Direct, factual, 30–50 word answers. Memorize the key formula or definition.

---

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define damping ratio. What is its significance in the dynamic response of an automated mechanism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The damping ratio $\zeta$ is a dimensionless quantity that compares the actual damping coefficient $c$ of a system to its critical damping coefficient $c_c$. It is mathematically defined as $\zeta = c / c_c = c / (2\sqrt{mk})$.

**Significance:**
1. It classifies the system response — underdamped ($\zeta < 1$), critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), or overdamped ($\zeta > 1$).
2. It governs overshoot, settling time, and oscillation frequency in automated positioning systems.
3. It is the primary design parameter chosen by the engineer to balance speed and stability.

*(Exact word count in answer: ~50 words; mark allocation: 1 + 1 + 1.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between viscous damping and Coulomb damping with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Viscous Damping | Coulomb Damping |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Force law | $F_d = c\dot{x}$ (velocity-proportional) | $F_d = \mu N \cdot \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ (constant magnitude, direction reverses with velocity) |
| Linearity | Linear | Non-linear |
| Energy per cycle | Depends on amplitude squared | Independent of amplitude |
| Example | Hydraulic dashpot in a press brake | Friction pad in a robotic joint brake |

*(Mark allocation: 1 for distinction, 1 for force law, 1 for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries internal choice (OR). The 14 marks are split into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question B — Option A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A robotic manipulator link is modeled as a single-degree-of-freedom system with the following parameters:**

- Effective mass $m = 10 \text{ kg}$
- Spring stiffness $k = 4000 \text{ N/m}$
- Viscous damping coefficient $c = 100 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}$

**The link is initially displaced by $0.05 \text{ m}$ and released from rest.**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion and determine the damping ratio, natural frequency, and damped natural frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the complete time response $x(t)$. Calculate the time at which the amplitude decays to 1% of its initial value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Equation of motion (1 mark):** The undamped-free SDOF equation is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 10\ddot{x} + 100\dot{x} + 4000x = 0$$

**Step 2 — Natural frequency (2 marks):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{10}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Critical damping coefficient (1 mark):**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{10 \times 4000} = 2\sqrt{40000} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}$$

**Step 4 — Damping ratio (2 marks):**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25$$

**Step 5 — Damped natural frequency (1 mark):**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 20\sqrt{0.9375} = 20 \times 0.9682 = 19.365 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Form of the underdamped solution (1 mark):**

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply initial conditions to find $A$ and $B$ (3 marks):**

Given $x(0) = 0.05$ m and $\dot{x}(0) = 0$:

- At $t = 0$: $x(0) = A = 0.05$ m
- Differentiate $x(t)$:

$$\dot{x}(t) = -\zeta\omega_n e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t) + e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}(-A\omega_d\sin\omega_d t + B\omega_d\cos\omega_d t)$$

- At $t = 0$: $\dot{x}(0) = -\zeta\omega_n A + B\omega_d = 0$

$$B = \frac{\zeta\omega_n A}{\omega_d} = \frac{0.25 \times 20 \times 0.05}{19.365} = \frac{0.25}{19.365} = 0.01291 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Combined form using $X = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(B/A)$ (1 mark):**

$$X = \sqrt{0.05^2 + 0.01291^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.0001667} = \sqrt{0.0026667} = 0.05164 \text{ m}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.01291}{0.05}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.2582) = 14.48° = 0.2527 \text{ rad}$$

**Final form (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{x(t) = 0.05164 \, e^{-5t}\cos(19.365\,t - 0.2527) \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Time to decay to 1% of initial amplitude (1 mark):** The amplitude envelope is $X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$. Setting:

$$\frac{X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{X_0} = 0.01 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} = 0.01$$

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{\zeta\omega_n} = \frac{4.6052}{0.25 \times 20} = \frac{4.6052}{5} = 0.921 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly equate the *displacement* $x(t)$ to the *envelope* $X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ when solving for decay time. The envelope is the *outer boundary* of the oscillation. The actual displacement oscillates inside the envelope and may equal 1% of $X_0$ at a *different* time than the envelope. The question asks about amplitude decay, so the envelope formula is correct — but mark yourself for this distinction.

---

### Question B — Option B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **electrical analogy** (force–voltage type) of a mechanical mass–spring–damper system. Derive the corresponding electrical differential equation and identify the analogous quantities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC servomotor drives an automated conveyor. The motor has armature resistance $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$, torque constant $K_t = 0.5 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m/A}$, and back-EMF constant $K_b = 0.5 \text{ V}\cdot\text{s/rad}$. The total inertia referred to the motor shaft is $J = 0.02 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ and viscous friction is $B = 0.1 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s/rad}$. Compute the **effective damping coefficient** and comment on the dominance of electrical over mechanical damping. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Sketch of the analogy (2 marks):** A neat schematic showing:
- Mechanical side: mass $m$ on a spring $k$ with damper $c$ attached, force $F$ applied
- Electrical side: series RLC circuit with $L$, $R$, $C$ in series, voltage source $V$
- Two-side alignment table

**Step 2 — Mechanical equation (1 mark):**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

**Step 3 — Electrical equation of series RLC (1 mark):**

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri + \frac{1}{C}\int i\,dt = V(t)$$

Differentiating once:

$$L\frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}i = \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Term-by-term mapping and analogous quantities (3 marks):**

| # | Mechanical | Electrical |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mass $m$ (kg) | Inductance $L$ (H) |
| 2 | Damper $c$ (N·s/m) | Resistance $R$ ($\Omega$) |
| 3 | Spring $k$ (N/m) | Inverse capacitance $1/C$ (1/F) |
| 4 | Force $F$ (N) | Voltage $V$ (V) |
| 5 | Displacement $x$ (m) | Charge $q$ (C) |
| 6 | Velocity $\dot{x}$ (m/s) | Current $i$ (A) |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the back-EMF damping formula (1 mark):**

$$c_{\text{eff}} = B + \frac{K_t K_b}{R_a}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the electrical damping term (3 marks):**

$$\frac{K_t K_b}{R_a} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{2} = \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.125 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s/rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective damping (1 mark):**

$$c_{\text{eff}} = 0.1 + 0.125 = 0.225 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio analysis and conclusion (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\text{Electrical damping}}{\text{Mechanical damping}} = \frac{0.125}{0.1} = 1.25$$

The electrical damping term is **1.25 times** larger than the inherent mechanical viscous friction. This demonstrates that **back-EMF contributes more to the system's total damping than physical friction does**, a typical feature of well-designed servomotor-driven automated machinery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A very common error is to forget that the units of $K_t K_b / R_a$ are N·m·s/rad (i.e., torque per angular velocity), not N·s/m (linear). Because we are working at the motor shaft, all quantities are rotational. If the question had been about a translational load, you would need a gear ratio conversion. Always check whether the problem is *rotational* or *translational* before plugging in numbers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Memorize List — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Equation of motion** for SDOF damped system: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$
- [ ] **Three quantities to always compute first:** $\omega_n$, $c_c$, $\zeta$
- [ ] **Classification rule:** $\zeta < 1$ (under), $\zeta = 1$ (critical), $\zeta > 1$ (over)
- [ ] **Damped frequency formula:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ — only valid for $\zeta < 1$
- [ ] **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — and its inverse form to find $\zeta$ experimentally
- [ ] **Settling time** (2%): $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$
- [ ] **Peak overshoot:** $\%OS = 100 \cdot \exp(-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$
- [ ] **Impedance analogy mapping:** $m \to L$, $c \to R$, $k \to 1/C$, $F \to V$, $x \to q$, $\dot{x} \to i$
- [ ] **Back-EMF damping in DC motors:** $c_{\text{electrical}} = K_t K_b / R_a$
- [ ] **Eddy current damping** is non-contacting — used in galvanometers, precision scales, maglev trains
- [ ] **Coulomb damping** is non-linear — its force is constant in magnitude, opposite to velocity
- [ ] **Viscous damping** is linear — force proportional to velocity
- [ ] **Critical damping** is the boundary case that returns the system to equilibrium in minimum time *without* overshoot
- [ ] **In automated systems, electrical damping is preferred** because it is contactless, tunable, and temperature-stable compared to mechanical damping

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Rotational Transportation: Conveyors, Indexing Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear and Rotational Transportation: Conveyors, Indexing Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Linear and Rotational Transportation in Automated Machines

In the architecture of **Automated Manufacturing Systems (AMS)**, material handling constitutes the critical link between workstations. Transportation in automated machinery is classified into two principal categories based on the kinematic nature of the primary motion:

- **Linear Transportation (Translational Motion):** Material or workpieces are moved along a straight or curvilinear path using mechanisms such as **conveyors**, slides, or powered roller tracks. The dominant kinematic output is a translation vector $\vec{v}$ in m/s.
- **Rotational Transportation (Indexing Motion):** Workpieces are moved in discrete angular increments using **indexing tables** (also called rotary indexing fixtures or dial tables). The dominant kinematic output is an angular displacement $\theta$ in radians or degrees, with intermittent dwell periods for workstation operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under PERUT743 Module 4, students must demonstrate the ability to **select, configure, and analyze** both linear and rotational transportation sub-systems for automated cells. The emphasis is on **mechanism selection** rather than detailed structural design of structural frames.

### 1.2 Conveyors — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *conveyor* is a powered or gravity-assisted material-handling machine designed to move loads continuously or intermittently along a fixed horizontal, inclined, or vertical path using a **belt, chain, screw, roller, or vibratory** medium. The conveyor is the workhorse of linear transportation in FMC (Flexible Manufacturing Cell) environments.

### 1.3 Indexing Tables — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** An *indexing table* is a rotary positioning device that converts **continuous rotary input motion** into **intermittent rotary output motion** through a precisely controlled dwell-and-advance cycle. Each discrete angular advance is termed an *index*, and the stationary phase is called the *dwell*.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:** Imagine a busy commercial kitchen during peak hours. **Conveyors** are like the overhead sushi tracks or the dish-return belts in a buffet — they continuously ferry items linearly from the kitchen to the customer and back. **Indexing Tables** are like a **revolving spice rack** or a **rotating sushi plate on a turntable** — it rotates by a precise angular step, then pauses (dwell) so the chef or customer can access items, then rotates again. Just as the spice rack's "click-stop" mechanism ensures it doesn't overshoot, an indexing table uses Geneva mechanisms, cam-actuated ratchets, or NC servo drives to guarantee *exact* angular positioning before the next operation begins.

### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Velocity | $v$ | m/s | Throughput of conveyor |
| Belt Tension | $T_1, T_2$ | N | Determines power requirement |
| Mass Flow Rate | $Q_m$ | kg/s | Material handling capacity |
| Indexing Angle | $\theta_i$ | degrees/rad | Step size of table |
| Dwell Time | $t_d$ | s | Stationary interval |
| Indexing Time | $t_i$ | s | Active rotation interval |
| Number of Stations | $n$ | — | Workpiece capacity per cycle |
| Index Frequency | $f$ | Hz | Cycles per second |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Conveyors

Conveyors are classified by **load-carrying medium** and **drive principle**:

**A. Belt Conveyors**
- A continuous loop of **flexible belting** (rubber, PVC, fabric) driven by friction between belt and a powered pulley.
- Used for: packaged goods, bulk materials, assembly line parts.
- Drive: head pulley motor; tail pulley may be idler or take-up.

**B. Roller Conveyors**
- **Gravity Roller:** Non-powered, relies on potential energy. Inclination: typically 3°–8°.
- **Powered Roller (Motorized):** Each roller driven by individual motor or chain/belt from line shaft.
- Used for: cartons, totes, pallets in distribution centers.

**C. Chain Conveyors**
- Slats, attachments, or fixtures bolted to one or more chains running on tracks.
- **Types:** Slat conveyor, apron conveyor, en-masse conveyor, trolley conveyor.
- Used for: heavy loads, paint lines, assembly lines with fixtures.

**D. Screw (Auger) Conveyors**
- A helical screw rotates inside a trough, pushing material forward by **archimedean screw principle**.
- Used for: granular bulk material (grain, cement, plastic pellets).

**E. Vibratory Conveyors
- Material moves via **micro-throw** from electromagnetically or mechanically induced vibration.
- Used for: parts feeding in feeder bowls, chip removal.

**F. Pneumatic / Overhead Conveyors**
- Material suspended from overhead track (power-and-free conveyor) or transported in air streams (pneumatic tube).
- Used for: automobile body assembly, garment industry.

### 2.2 Classification of Indexing Tables

**A. Mechanical Indexing Mechanisms**

1. **Geneva Drive (Geneva Wheel / Maltese Cross)**
   - Most iconic indexing mechanism.
   - Driving member: a pinwheel with a single pin.
   - Driven member: a Geneva wheel with **radial slots**.
   - The pin engages the slot, producing **constant-velocity** or **modified-velocity** rotation.
   - Number of slots $n$ on Geneva wheel determines index angle: $\theta_i = \frac{360°}{n}$

2. **Geneva Cam (Barrel Cam) Indexing**
   - A barrel-shaped cam with a curved groove drives a roller follower on the indexing turret.
   - Provides fully **customizable motion profiles** (modified trapezoidal, sine, cycloidal).
   - Used in high-speed applications (e.g., bottle filling, packaging).

3. **Ratchet and Pawl Indexing**
   - Pawl engages ratchet teeth; spring-loaded for one-way indexing.
   - Simple, inexpensive, low precision.

**B. Servo-Driven / NC Rotary Tables**
- Closed-loop servo motor with absolute encoder.
- Arbitrary index angles achievable via CNC programming.
- High rigidity, high precision (arc-second accuracy).
- Backlash compensation via dual-pinion preloading.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for PERUT743 Module 4 — Memorize Before Examination**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Belt Velocity | $v = \pi D_p N / 60$ | $D_p$ = pulley diameter (m); $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Belt Power | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | $T_1$ = tight side tension (N); $T_2$ = slack side (N) |
| 3 | Belt Tension Ratio (Euler-Eytelwein) | $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient; $\theta$ = wrap angle (rad) |
| 4 | Mass Throughput | $Q_m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | $\rho$ = bulk density (kg/m³); $A$ = cross-section (m²) |
| 5 | Volume Throughput | $Q_v = A \cdot v$ | $A$ = m²; $v$ = m/s; result in m³/s |
| 6 | Inclined Belt Power (Net) | $P_{net} = (Q_m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\alpha) + (Q_m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \mu_r \cdot \cos\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = incline angle; $\mu_r$ = rolling resistance |
| 7 | Geneva Index Angle | $\theta_i = 2\pi / n$ | $n$ = number of slots |
| 8 | Geneva Pinwheel Angular Travel (Index) | $\phi_{pin} = \pi - 2 \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right) \cdot \frac{180}{\pi}$ | $R$ = center distance; $r$ = pin radius |
| 9 | Dwell Ratio | $k = t_d / t_i$ | $t_d$ = dwell time; $t_i$ = index time |
| 10 | Indexing Table Inertia Torque | $T_i = I \cdot \alpha$ | $I$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²); $\alpha$ = angular accel (rad/s²) |
| 11 | Servo Indexing Resolution | $\Delta\theta = 360° / (N_{encoder} \cdot G)$ | $N_{encoder}$ = pulses/rev; $G$ = gear ratio |
| 12 | Screw Conveyor Capacity | $Q_v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot s \cdot n \cdot \Psi \cdot 60$ | $D$ = screw dia (m); $s$ = pitch (m); $n$ = rpm; $\Psi$ = fill factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Euler-Eytelwein formula (Row 3) is *the* governing equation for all belt-and-pulley power transmission — applicable from a 1 cm micro-pulley in a watch to a 2 m conveyor head pulley. Geneva kinematics (Rows 7–8) appear in every intermittent-motion device from film projectors to pharmaceutical tablet presses.

### 2.4 Selection Logic — Why Choose What?

- **For high-speed continuous transfer of light/medium parts:** **Belt conveyor** (cost-effective, silent, easy maintenance).
- **For accumulating accumulation with zero back-pressure:** **Powered roller conveyor** with zero-pressure accumulation zones.
- **For in-process transfer between workstations with precise positioning:** **Chain conveyor with fixtures** (pallet transfer).
- **For 3 to 16 workpieces in a rotary cell:** **Geneva-driven indexing table** (passive dwell, robust).
- **For variable pitch, high-precision, CNC-integrated rotation:** **Servo-driven NC rotary table**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Belt Conveyor Power Requirement

**Problem Frame:** A horizontal belt conveyor must move $Q_m$ kg/s of material over a length $L$ at velocity $v$ m/s, against a frictional resistance $\mu_r$.

**Step 1 — Identify Resistances**

The total resistance $F_{total}$ opposing motion has three components:

$$
F_{total} = F_{belt} + F_{material} + F_{incline}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Each Term**

- Belt resistance (empty belt): $F_{belt} = m_{belt} \cdot g \cdot \mu_r$
- Material resistance: $F_{material} = Q_m \cdot L/v \cdot g \cdot \mu_r$ (mass on belt = mass flow × residence time)
- Incline resistance: $F_{incline} = (Q_m \cdot L/v + m_{belt}) \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$

**Step 3 — Sum the Resistances**

$$
F_{total} = \mu_r \cdot g \left( m_{belt} + \frac{Q_m \cdot L}{v} \right) + g \cdot \sin\alpha \left( \frac{Q_m \cdot L}{v} + m_{belt} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Power**

$$
P = F_{total} \cdot v
$$

$$
P = v \cdot g \left[ \mu_r \left( m_{belt} + \frac{Q_m \cdot L}{v} \right) + \sin\alpha \left( \frac{Q_m \cdot L}{v} + m_{belt} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify for Horizontal Conveyor ($\alpha = 0$)**

$$
P_{horizontal} = \mu_r \cdot g \cdot v \cdot m_{belt} + \mu_r \cdot g \cdot L \cdot Q_m
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The first term is the *no-load power* (depends on belt mass and velocity), the second is the *load power* (depends on mass flow and belt length). Designers often target the *no-load power* to be under 20% of total.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Geneva Drive — Pin Angular Travel During Indexing

**Problem Frame:** A Geneva wheel with $n$ slots is driven by a pinwheel of radius $r_p$ at center distance $C$. Determine the angular travel $\phi$ of the pinwheel during the index stroke.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup**

When the pin enters the radial slot of the Geneva wheel, the pin lies on the line connecting the two wheel centers. As the pinwheel rotates, the pin remains on a circle of radius $r_p$ about the pinwheel center. The slot is radial — it always passes through the Geneva wheel center.

**Step 2 — Entry and Exit Geometry**

At the moment of entry, the pin is at the line of centers. The slot makes an angle $\gamma$ with the line of centers such that:

$$
\sin\gamma = \frac{r_p}{C}
$$

$$
\gamma = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_p}{C}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Half-Index Rotation of Geneva Wheel**

The Geneva wheel rotates by half the index angle, $\theta_i/2 = \pi/n$, from the line of centers.

**Step 4 — Sum the Pinwheel Rotations**

The pinwheel rotates by:

- $\gamma$ to bring the pin to the slot centerline
- $\pi/n$ to rotate the Geneva wheel by half its index
- $\gamma$ to withdraw the pin from the slot

$$
\phi_{pin} = 2\gamma + \frac{\pi}{n} = 2\arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_p}{C}\right) + \frac{\pi}{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Degrees (KTU Standard)**

$$
\phi_{pin} = 2\arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_p}{C}\right) \cdot \frac{180°}{\pi} + \frac{180°}{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Dwell Angle**

The remaining pinwheel rotation is dwell:

$$
\phi_{dwell} = 360° - \phi_{pin}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** This formula lets you verify that a proposed Geneva design will deliver the desired dwell ratio. A 6-slot Geneva ($n=6$) with $r_p = C/2$ gives $\phi_{pin} \approx 60° + 30° = 90°$, i.e., 75% dwell — ideal for slow-assembly cells.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Indexing Table Servo Sizing

**Given:**
- Table: $D = 0.8$ m, mass $m_t = 50$ kg
- 6 stations ($n = 6$)
- Index angle: $\theta_i = 60° = \pi/3$ rad
- Index time: $t_i = 0.5$ s
- Load on table: $m_L = 10$ kg at radius 0.3 m

**Find:** Required motor torque, assuming constant acceleration profile.

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia**

For a solid disk table:

$$
I_t = \frac{1}{2} m_t \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot (0.4)^2 = 4.0 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

For the off-center load:

$$
I_L = m_L \cdot r^2 = 10 \cdot (0.3)^2 = 0.9 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

Total:

$$
I_{total} = I_t + I_L = 4.0 + 0.9 = 4.9 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Acceleration (Trapezoidal Profile)**

For a trapezoidal velocity profile (accel time = index time / 2):

$$
\alpha_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot \theta_i}{t_i^2} = \frac{2 \cdot (\pi/3)}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{2.094}{0.25} = 8.378 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration Torque**

$$
T_{accel} = I_{total} \cdot \alpha_{max} = 4.9 \cdot 8.378 = 41.05 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Friction Torque (assume 5% of total)**

$$
T_{friction} \approx 0.05 \cdot T_{accel} = 2.05 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Design Motor Torque (with 1.5 safety factor)**

$$
T_{motor} = 1.5 \cdot (T_{accel} + T_{friction}) = 1.5 \cdot 43.1 = 64.65 \text{ N·m}
$$

> **Result:** Select a servo motor with rated torque ≥ **65 N·m** and peak torque capability ≥ **130 N·m** for this indexing application.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Belt Conveyor | Geneva Indexing | NC Servo Indexing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drive Principle | Continuous friction | Pin-and-slot intermittent | Closed-loop servo |
| Position Repeatability | ±5 mm (typical) | ±0.05 mm | ±0.001 mm (arc-sec) |
| Max Stations | Continuous (∞) | 4–16 (practical) | Unlimited |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 2× | 5–10× |
| Maintenance | Belt replacement | Lubrication, pin wear | Encoder check |
| Dwell Ratio | N/A (continuous) | Fixed by geometry | Programmable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Conveyor System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Hopper] --> B[Feed Control Gate]
    B --> C[Drive Motor 3-Phase AC]
    C --> D[Coupling and Gear Reducer]
    D --> E[Head Pulley Drive Shaft]
    E --> F[Belt Loop Continuous]
    F --> G[Tail Pulley Idler with Take-Up]
    F --> H[Workpiece Carrier Pallet]
    H --> I[End Discharge Station]
    I --> J[Output Buffer]

    subgraph ControlLoop[Feedback Control Layer]
        K[VFD Variable Frequency Drive] --> C
        L[Tachogenerator Encoder] --> K
    end

    subgraph SafetyLayer[Protective Systems]
        M[Pull Cord E-Stop] --> K
        N[Slip Detector Tachometer] --> K
    end
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Geneva Indexing Table

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Prime Mover AC Motor] --> B[Speed Reducer Worm Gearbox]
    B --> C[Drive Shaft]
    C --> D[Pinwheel with Single Pin]
    D -->|Pin enters slot| E[Geneva Wheel Maltese Cross]
    E --> F[Indexing Turret Table]
    F --> G[Workpiece Fixtures at Stations]

    subgraph KinematicLayer[Geometric Constraints]
        H[Center Distance C] -.-> D
        I[Pin Radius r_p] -.-> D
        J[Number of Slots n] -.-> E
    end

    subgraph DwellState[Dwell Phase]
        K[Pin disengaged] -.-> F
        L[Workstation Operations] -.-> G
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — NC Servo Indexing Table

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host PLC or CNC Controller] -->|Position Command Pulse Train| B[Servo Drive Amplifier]
    B -->|3-Phase PWM| C[Servo Motor Brushless AC]
    C -->|Mechanical| D[Planetary Gearbox]
    D --> E[Tilt-Precision Bearing Crossed Roller]
    E --> F[Rotary Table Top with T-Slot Grid]
    F --> G[Fixtures and Workpiece]

    subgraph FeedbackSensors
        H[Absolute Encoder 23-bit] --> B
        I[Torque Sensor Optional] --> B
    end

    subgraph SafetyInterlocks
        J[Limit Switches Hard Stops] --> B
        K[Brake Released Confirmation] --> B
    end
```

### 4.4 Conveyor System Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A{Need Continuous Linear Transfer?}
    A -- Yes --> B{Part Weight?}
    A -- No --> X{Need Intermittent Angular Transfer?}
    X -- Yes --> Y[Indexing Table - Servo or Geneva]

    B -- Light to Medium Under 50 kg --> C{Accumulation Needed?}
    B -- Heavy Over 50 kg --> D[Chain Conveyor with Pallet]
    B -- Bulk Granular --> E[Screw Auger Conveyor]

    C -- Yes --> F[Powered Roller with ZPA Logic]
    C -- No --> G[Belt Conveyor with VFD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation Note:** The mermaid diagrams above intentionally abstract the *physical geometry* of the conveyor and indexing table into *functional blocks*, since visual rendering of physical 3D shapes (pulleys, belts, Geneva wheels) is not natively supported in text-based diagrams. The arrows represent *power/control flow*, not mechanical linkage. KTU 2024 examiners will accept this functional-level representation provided that the **input-process-output structure** is clearly delineated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PERUT743]`

**Differentiate between a gravity roller conveyor and a powered roller conveyor. State two industrial applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **gravity roller conveyor** is a non-powered material-handling system where the rollers are mounted in a frame at a slight downward slope (typically 3° to 8°). Workpieces move solely under the influence of **gravitational potential energy** with no external power source. Applications include: (i) carton transfer in warehouse receiving bays, (ii) parcel sorting in post offices.

A **powered roller conveyor** uses externally driven rollers — each roller has a small motor, or the rollers are coupled by chain/belt from a line shaft. Application of **zero-pressure accumulation (ZPA)** logic prevents workpiece collision. Applications include: (i) distribution center cross-belt sorters, (ii) automotive assembly line inter-station transfer.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PERUT743]`

**Define indexing. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Geneva drive mechanism having 4 slots.**

**Model Answer:**

**Indexing** is the process of converting continuous rotary motion into **intermittent rotary motion** with precise angular steps separated by dwell periods.

In a **4-slot Geneva drive**, the driving pinwheel rotates continuously. As its single pin enters one of the four radial slots of the Geneva wheel, it engages and rotates the Geneva wheel by **90°** (= 360°/4). The pin then disengages, and the Geneva wheel remains **stationary** (dwell) for 270° of pinwheel rotation. This cycle repeats, producing 4 indexes per revolution with a **3:1 dwell-to-index ratio**. The mechanism is used in indexing tables, film projectors, and small packaging machines.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** For a 14-mark question, you must answer **both sub-parts (a) and (b)** unless internal choice is explicitly provided. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives — the student answers ONE of them with BOTH sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper PERUT743]`

**Q. (a)** A horizontal belt conveyor is to transport 50 kg/s of crushed ore over a length of 30 m at a belt speed of 1.5 m/s. The empty belt mass per meter is 8 kg/m. The coefficient of friction between belt and idlers is 0.03. The drive pulley diameter is 0.5 m. Calculate:
1. Belt tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ (assume wrap angle = 180° and $\mu_{pulley} = 0.25$)
2. Power required at the drive pulley
3. Drive motor power if gearbox efficiency is 90%

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Belt + Material Mass on Conveyor**

Mass of empty belt:

$$
m_{belt} = 8 \cdot 30 = 240 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass of material on belt (residence time = $L/v$):

$$
m_{mat} = 50 \cdot \frac{30}{1.5} = 1000 \text{ kg}
$$

Total mass:

$$
m_{total} = 1240 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Frictional Resistance**

$$
F_{fric} = \mu_r \cdot m_{total} \cdot g = 0.03 \cdot 1240 \cdot 9.81 = 364.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Belt Tension on Slack Side (T₂)**

The slack side tension $T_2$ at the tail pulley is approximated by:

$$
T_2 = F_{fric} \approx 365 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Wrap Angle**

$$
\theta = 180° = \pi \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 5 — Tension Ratio (Euler-Eytelwein)**

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.25 \cdot \pi} = e^{0.7854} = 2.193
$$

**Step 6 — Tight Side Tension T₁**

$$
T_1 = 2.193 \cdot 365 = 800.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Power at Pulley**

$$
P_{pulley} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (800.4 - 365) \cdot 1.5 = 653.1 \text{ W} \approx 0.653 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Motor Power (with gearbox)**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{pulley}}{\eta_{gb}} = \frac{0.653}{0.9} = 0.726 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $T_1 \approx 800$ N, $T_2 \approx 365$ N, $P_{motor} \approx 0.73$ kW → Select a **1.0 kW** standard motor.

---

**(b)** A Geneva indexing table has 8 slots, a pinwheel radius of 50 mm, and a center distance of 100 mm. Calculate:
1. The indexing angle
2. The pinwheel angle during index
3. The dwell angle
4. The dwell ratio

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Indexing Angle**

$$
\theta_i = \frac{360°}{n} = \frac{360°}{8} = 45°
$$

**Step 2 — Geometry Check (must satisfy $r_p < C$)**

$r_p = 50$ mm, $C = 100$ mm → $r_p/C = 0.5$ → Valid.

**Step 3 — Compute $\gamma$**

$$
\gamma = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_p}{C}\right) = \arcsin(0.5) = 30°
$$

**Step 4 — Pinwheel Angle During Index**

$$
\phi_{pin} = 2\gamma + \frac{360°}{n} = 2 \cdot 30° + 45° = 105°
$$

**Step 5 — Dwell Angle**

$$
\phi_{dwell} = 360° - 105° = 255°
$$

**Step 6 — Dwell Ratio**

$$
k = \frac{\phi_{dwell}}{\phi_{pin}} = \frac{255}{105} \approx 2.43
$$

> **Final Answer:** Indexing angle = 45°, Pinwheel index angle = 105°, Dwell angle = 255°, Dwell ratio ≈ 2.43:1.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PERUT743]`

**Q. (a)** Explain with a functional block diagram the components and working of a **chain conveyor with powered fixtures** for an automotive body assembly line. Discuss the role of the **dog-and-safety-stop** mechanism in indexing chains.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **chain conveyor with powered fixtures** in an automotive body assembly line consists of:
1. **Drive unit:** Geared motor with VFD control for variable line speed.
2. **Chain:** Heavy-duty roller chain running on inverted-tooth sprockets.
3. **Cross-bars / Trolleys:** Rigid fixtures bolted to chain attachments to carry the car body shells.
4. **Track:** I-beam or enclosed track supporting the chain.
5. **Take-up unit:** Spring-loaded or screw-type to compensate for chain elongation.
6. **Sensors:** Proximity switches at workstations for synchronization with robots.

**Working:** The car body shell is loaded onto a fixture at the start, then moves continuously (or in programmed stop-and-go) past welding, painting, and inspection stations. Robots perform operations while the line indexes to the next station.

**Dog-and-Safety-Stop Mechanism:** The "dog" is a pivoting pawl on the chain carrier that engages with a fixed stop on the track to provide **positive positioning** at workstations. This prevents over-travel due to inertia, eliminates the need for fine servo control, and provides a mechanical safety barrier against runaway motion. A secondary "safety dog" is mounted on the opposite side for redundant locking.

---

**(b)** A **servo-driven NC rotary table** must perform 12 indexes per minute, each index being 30°, with a maximum positional error of ±10 arc-seconds. The load (workpiece + fixture) has an effective moment of inertia of 2.5 kg·m². Determine:
1. Indexing time per step (assume 50% of cycle = index, 50% = dwell)
2. Maximum angular velocity
3. Maximum angular acceleration (trapezoidal profile)
4. Peak torque requirement

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cycle Time**

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{12} = 5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Indexing Time**

$$
t_i = 0.5 \cdot 5 = 2.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular Displacement per Index**

$$
\theta_i = 30° = \frac{\pi}{6} = 0.5236 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Angular Velocity (Trapezoidal Profile)**

For a trapezoidal profile, average velocity $\bar{\omega} = \theta_i / t_i$, and $\omega_{max} = 1.5 \cdot \bar{\omega}$ (for symmetric accel/decel):

$$
\omega_{max} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{0.5236}{2.5} = 0.3142 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration Time = t_i / 2**

$$
t_{accel} = 1.25 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Angular Acceleration**

$$
\alpha_{max} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{t_{accel}} = \frac{0.3142}{1.25} = 0.2513 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Peak Torque**

$$
T_{peak} = I \cdot \alpha_{max} = 2.5 \cdot 0.2513 = 0.628 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 8 — Resolution Check (KTU 2024 Sub-Marking)**

Required resolution: $\pm 10$ arc-sec = $\pm 0.0028°$
Encoder choice: 23-bit (8,388,608 pulses/rev) at 1:1 gearing gives $\approx 0.000043°$ — well within spec.

> **Final Answer:** $t_i = 2.5$ s, $\omega_{max} \approx 0.314$ rad/s, $\alpha_{max} \approx 0.251$ rad/s², $T_{peak} \approx 0.63$ N·m. A small servo (e.g., 750 W class) is sufficient.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PERUT743 Module 4:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wrap-angle correction** in belt tension calculations. Many students write $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu}$ instead of $e^{\mu \theta}$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using diameter instead of radius** in moment-of-inertia formulas: $I = mr^2$ is correct; $I = mD^2$ is wrong (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Confusing dwell ratio and dwell angle** — students often divide in the wrong direction.
> 4. **Forgetting to include the load moment of inertia** in addition to the table's own inertia in servo sizing.
> 5. **Not stating units** in final answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Omitting the safety factor** in motor selection — examiners expect at least a 1.25–1.5× factor.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Linear & Rotational Transportation**

- ✅ **Conveyors** provide *continuous* linear transfer; **Indexing Tables** provide *intermittent* angular positioning.
- ✅ Six major conveyor types to remember: **Belt, Roller, Chain, Screw, Vibratory, Overhead**.
- ✅ Belt velocity: $v = \pi D N / 60$. **Memorize.**
- ✅ Euler-Eytelwein tension ratio: $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ — *the* key equation.
- ✅ Indexing angle for Geneva wheel: $\theta_i = 360°/n$ where $n$ = number of slots.
- ✅ Pinwheel index angle: $\phi_{pin} = 2 \arcsin(r_p/C) + 360°/n$ — derive on exam paper.
- ✅ Dwell ratio $k = \phi_{dwell}/\phi_{pin}$ — must be > 1 for practical assembly operations.
- ✅ For servomotor sizing, compute $I_{total}$ (table + load), then $T = I \alpha$ with trapezoidal profile and **1.5× safety factor**.
- ✅ NC rotary tables use **absolute encoders** (17 to 23 bit) for arc-second resolution.
- ✅ **Dog-and-safety-stop** is the mechanical safety feature on chain conveyors — *always mention* in line conveyor descriptions.
- ✅ **Zero-Pressure Accumulation (ZPA)** logic distinguishes powered roller conveyors used in modern distribution centers.
- ✅ Geneva drives are limited to ~16 stations practically; for more stations, use **barrel cam** indexing.
- ✅ Power flow for any conveyor: **Motor → Coupling → Reducer → Drive Pulley/Sprocket → Belt/Chain → Carried Load**.
- ✅ Always include **units** in final numerical answers — KTU 2024 valuation strictly checks this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies on Transportation Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies on Transportation Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automated Machines** and **Mechanism Design**, *Transportation Devices* refer to a specialized class of mechatronic subsystems engineered to **autonomously relocate workpieces, materials, tools, or products** between distinct workstations, processing zones, or storage locations within an industrial cell, without continuous human intervention.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PERUT743, transportation devices form the **material handling backbone** of any flexible manufacturing system (FMS). They integrate:

- **Mechanical Subsystems** (linkages, conveyors, actuators)
- **Sensing Subsystems** (proximity sensors, encoders, vision systems)
- **Control Subsystems** (PLCs, microcontrollers, servo drives)
- **Drive Subsystems** (electric motors, pneumatic cylinders, hydraulic actuators)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Case studies on transportation devices focus on **real-time industrial implementations** where students must analyze *kinematic chain selection*, *actuator sizing*, *payload capacity*, *cycle time optimization*, and *return on investment (ROI)*. The examination frequently tests the ability to **map functional requirements to mechanism selection**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern airport baggage handling system**:
- When you check in a suitcase at Delhi, it travels through tunnels, conveyor belts, sorting arms, and finally arrives at your flight in Kochi.
- You never see a person carrying your bag — but behind the scenes, **thousands of motors, sensors, and linkages** are working in harmony.
- Each "transport leg" is a *transportation device* — designed for a specific distance, speed, payload, and turning radius.

In a **manufacturing plant**, the same idea applies. A car body needs to move from the *welding station* → *painting booth* → *assembly line* → *testing bay*. Each leg of this journey is solved by a specific transportation device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A transportation device is **NOT a robot arm** (which manipulates objects in place) — it is a device whose **primary function is locomotion or transfer of mass** over a defined trajectory (linear, rotary, or curvilinear).

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

The following performance metrics are universally used to characterize any transportation device in board examinations:

| Performance Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Payload Capacity** | $m_p$ | **kg** | Maximum mass transferable per cycle |
| **Cycle Time** | $T_c$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for one complete transport operation |
| **Throughput** | $\Theta$ | **units/hour** | Number of items moved per unit time |
| **Operating Speed** | $v$ | **m/s** | Linear velocity of payload |
| **Power Consumption** | $P$ | **Watts (W)** | Electrical/mechanical power drawn |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | $\epsilon$ | **mm** | Deviation from target position |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Board questions often present a *transportation requirement* (e.g., "Move 500 kg pallet at 1.5 m/s across 20 m") and ask you to **select and justify a mechanism**. Always begin by computing the **required throughput** and **kinematic feasibility** before recommending a device.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Transportation Devices

Transportation devices in automated machinery can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

#### 2.1.1 By Trajectory Geometry
- **Linear Transporters**: Belt conveyors, roller conveyors, chain conveyors, linear actuators, shuttle cars.
- **Rotary Transporters**: Rotary tables, indexing turrets, turntables, carousels.
- **Curvilinear Transporters**: Curved belt conveyors, overhead monorail systems, vertical lifts.
- **Point-to-Point (Free Path)**: Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), mobile robots, overhead cranes.

#### 2.1.2 By Actuation Principle
- **Electrically Driven**: Servo-driven belts, stepper-driven rollers, linear motors.
- **Pneumatically Driven**: Air cylinders, vacuum grippers, air-bearing transporters.
- **Hydraulically Driven**: Hydraulic cylinders, hydraulic rotary actuators.
- **Mechanically Driven**: Cam-driven indexers, Geneva mechanisms, link conveyors.

#### 2.1.3 By Automation Level (per ISO 8373)
- **Level 1 — Manual Handling**: Hand carts, manual pallet jacks.
- **Level 2 — Semi-Automated**: Powered conveyors, lift tables.
- **Level 3 — Fully Automated**: AGVs, robotic shuttles, automated storage/retrieval systems (AS/RS).

#### 2.1.4 By Payload Class
| Class | Payload Range | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|
| Micro | < 1 kg | Vibratory feeders, small pneumatic pickers |
| Light | 1 – 50 kg | Belt conveyors, palletizing robots |
| Medium | 50 – 1000 kg | Roller conveyors, AGVs, chain conveyors |
| Heavy | > 1000 kg | Heavy-duty AGVs, overhead cranes, fork-lift AGVs |

### 2.2 Design Logic — The "WHY-HOW" of Selection

**Step 1 — Define the Functional Requirement (WHY)**
- What is the *mass* to be moved? What is the *distance*? What is the *required cycle time*?
- Example: A beverage plant needs to move 12,000 bottles/hour across 15 m.

**Step 2 — Determine the Kinematic Path (HOW)**
- Linear path → Belt/roller conveyor.
- Multi-stop with index → Geneva mechanism or servo indexer.
- Free navigation in 2D plane → AGV with magnetic/laser guidance.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Actuation Force/Power (HOW)**
- Use the **Newton-Euler equations** for the specific mechanism.
- Account for friction coefficient $\mu$, gravity component, and acceleration.

**Step 4 — Select Sensors and Control Architecture (HOW)**
- Position feedback: Incremental encoder (resolution 0.1 mm typical).
- Presence detection: Inductive proximity sensor (range 2–8 mm).
- Coordination: PLC with fieldbus (Profibus, EtherCAT) or industrial Ethernet.

**Step 5 — Validate against Constraints (WHY)**
- Safety (ISO 12100), energy efficiency, maintainability, cost.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU board examinations on transportation device design.**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Theta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Throughput from cycle time | Bottling, packaging lines |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta}$ | Power required to move load | Belt conveyor motor sizing |
| 3 | $F_{total} = m \cdot g \cdot \mu \cdot \cos\theta + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a$ | Total tractive force on inclined conveyor | Inclined transport |
| 4 | $T = (T_1 + T_2)$ | Effective belt tension | Conveyor belt design |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \cdot \alpha}$ | Belt tension ratio (Eytelwein equation) | Pulley design |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | Linear velocity from drum speed | Belt conveyor kinematics |
| 7 | $a = \dfrac{v_f - v_i}{t}$ | Linear acceleration | Motor torque sizing |
| 8 | $\tau = J \cdot \alpha + \tau_{friction}$ | Servo motor torque equation | Servo-driven shuttles |
| 9 | $P_{air} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{p_{supply} \cdot \eta_{cyl}}$ | Pneumatic cylinder power | Pneumatic pickers |
| 10 | $N_{Geneva} = \dfrac{t_{index}}{T_c - t_{index}}$ | Geneva wheel slots for given cycle | Indexing tables |

**Symbol Legend (used in formulas above):**

- $\Theta$ = Throughput (units/hr)
- $T_c$ = Cycle time (s)
- $F$ = Total tractive force (N)
- $v$ = Linear velocity (m/s)
- $\eta$ = Transmission efficiency (dimensionless, typically 0.85–0.95)
- $m$ = Mass (kg)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $\theta$ = Inclination angle (degrees)
- $a$ = Linear acceleration (m/s²)
- $T_1, T_2$ = Tight side and slack side belt tensions (N)
- $\alpha$ = Wrap angle on pulley (radians)
- $D$ = Drum/pulley diameter (m)
- $N$ = Drum rotational speed (RPM)
- $J$ = Moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $\tau$ = Torque (N·m)
- $p_{supply}$ = Pneumatic supply pressure (Pa)

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Note:** For numerical problems, KTU examiners award **partial marks** for correctly identifying the governing equation even if the final numerical answer is slightly off. Always **write the formula symbolically first**, substitute values, and then solve.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transportation device design underpins every modern industry vertical:

- **Automotive Assembly:** Power-and-free overhead conveyors transport car bodies through paint shops with positional accuracy of $\pm 5$ mm over track lengths exceeding 1 km.
- **E-Commerce Warehousing:** AGV fleets (e.g., Amazon Kiva robots) reduce order-picking time by **up to 70%** compared to manual systems.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Pneumatic tube transport systems move samples between labs at speeds of **7.5 m/s** with zero contamination.
- **Food & Beverage:** Stainless steel belt conveyors operate at throughputs of **60,000 cans/hour** in canning lines.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Automated FOUP (Front-Opening Unified Pod) transporters use linear motors for **vibration-free** wafer transport with $a < 0.1 \text{ m/s}^2$.
