## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Cycle Time and Throughput of a Linear Conveyor

**Problem Context:** A belt conveyor must transport 2,400 parts/hour across a distance $L = 12$ m, with constant acceleration/deceleration phases of $a = 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$ and a cruise velocity $v_c = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Define the Motion Profile**

The motion is a *trapezoidal velocity profile* with three phases:
- **Phase 1 — Acceleration:** from $0$ to $v_c$ over distance $d_1$
- **Phase 2 — Cruise:** at constant $v_c$ over distance $d_2$
- **Phase 3 — Deceleration:** from $v_c$ to $0$ over distance $d_3$

**Step 2 — Compute the Distance for Each Phase**

For Phase 1, using kinematics:

$$
d_1 = \frac{v_c^2}{2 \cdot a}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
d_1 = \frac{(1.2)^2}{2 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{1.44}{1.0} = 1.44 \text{ m}
$$

For Phase 3 (deceleration symmetric to acceleration):

$$
d_3 = \frac{v_c^2}{2 \cdot a} = 1.44 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cruise Distance**

$$
d_2 = L - d_1 - d_3 = 12 - 1.44 - 1.44 = 9.12 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cruise Time**

$$
t_2 = \frac{d_2}{v_c} = \frac{9.12}{1.2} = 7.6 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Acceleration Time**

$$
t_1 = \frac{v_c}{a} = \frac{1.2}{0.5} = 2.4 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Cycle Time**

$$
T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 2.4 + 7.6 + 2.4 = 12.4 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 7 — Throughput Verification**

$$
\Theta = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{12.4} \approx 290.32 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result Conflict:** The required throughput (2,400 parts/hr) far exceeds the achievable throughput (290 parts/hr) for a **single conveyor carrying one part at a time**. The design solution is to either (a) use a *continuous multi-part conveyor* with parts spaced at 1.8 m, or (b) use a *parallel multi-lane* arrangement. The KTU board expects students to **identify this conflict and propose remediation**.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Required Motor Power for Inclined Belt Conveyor

**Problem Context:** A belt conveyor must move $m = 800$ kg of packaged goods at $v = 0.8$ m/s up an incline of $\theta = 12°$. Coefficient of friction between belt and rollers is $\mu = 0.025$. System efficiency $\eta = 0.88$.

**Step 1 — Identify All Force Components**

The total tractive force on the belt is the sum of:
- Friction force (horizontal component of weight)
- Gravitational force component along incline
- Force to overcome load inertia (zero at steady-state cruise)

**Step 2 — Compute the Friction Force**

$$
F_{friction} = m \cdot g \cdot \mu \cdot \cos\theta
$$

Substituting values:

$$
F_{friction} = 800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.025 \cdot \cos(12°)
$$

$$
F_{friction} = 800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 0.9781
$$

$$
F_{friction} = 191.94 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Gravitational Force Component**

$$
F_{gravity} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

$$
F_{gravity} = 800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(12°)
$$

$$
F_{gravity} = 800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.2079
$$

$$
F_{gravity} = 1631.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Tractive Force**

$$
F_{total} = F_{friction} + F_{gravity}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 191.94 + 1631.6 = 1823.54 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Required Motor Power**

$$
P = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v}{\eta}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1823.54 \cdot 0.8}{0.88}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1458.83}{0.88} \approx 1657.76 \text{ W} \approx 1.66 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor (KTU Convention)**

Standard practice (and KTU board expectation) is to oversize the motor by a factor of **1.25 to 1.5** for variable load conditions:

$$
P_{selected} = 1.4 \cdot P = 1.4 \cdot 1.66 = 2.32 \text{ kW}
$$

The recommended motor is therefore a **standard 2.5 kW (3.35 HP)** three-phase induction motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** When asked for "minimum motor power," present the value **without** safety factor. When asked for "recommended motor rating," **include** the safety factor. This distinction is a common source of lost marks.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Geneva Wheel Slot Calculation

**Problem Context:** An automated bottling line uses a Geneva mechanism to index bottles into 8 filling stations. The indexing time must be $t_{index} = 1.5$ s, and the dwell time (stationary for filling) must be at least $t_{dwell} = 6.0$ s.

**Step 1 — Total Cycle Time**

$$
T_c = t_{index} + t_{dwell}
$$

$$
T_c = 1.5 + 6.0 = 7.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Slots (Stations)**

For a Geneva wheel with $n$ slots, each indexing event corresponds to $\frac{360°}{n}$ of driver rotation. The cycle time is the time for one full driver revolution:

$$
T_c = \frac{60}{N_{driver}}
$$

where $N_{driver}$ is the driver RPM. We can also express throughput as:

$$
\Theta = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{7.5} = 480 \text{ bottles/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Dwell Ratio**

The dwell ratio is:

$$
DR = \frac{t_{dwell}}{T_c} = \frac{6.0}{7.5} = 0.8
$$

For a Geneva wheel with $n$ slots, the maximum theoretical dwell ratio is:

$$
DR_{max} = 1 - \frac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Minimum n**

$$
1 - \frac{1}{n} \geq 0.8
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} \leq 0.2
$$

$$
n \geq 5
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

The minimum acceptable number of slots is $n = 5$. However, for **8 filling stations** (as specified in the problem), $n = 8$ is required, and the dwell ratio is:

$$
DR = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = 0.875
$$

This corresponds to a dwell time of:

$$
t_{dwell} = 0.875 \cdot 7.5 = 6.5625 \text{ s}
$$

This exceeds the minimum requirement of 6.0 s, so the design is **feasible**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Geneva Mechanisms?** Geneva wheels are widely used in **packaging, bottling, and assembly indexing** because they provide *positive locking* during dwell (no back-driving) and *intermittent motion* without the need for complex cam profiles. The KTU syllabus specifically tests Geneva kinematics because they combine **linkage analysis** (Module 1/2) with **intermittent motion design** (Module 4).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Transportation Device

The following decision matrix is what an experienced KTU examiner expects a student to construct when comparing alternatives.

| Selection Criterion | Belt Conveyor | Roller Conveyor | AGV | Pneumatic Shuttle | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput (units/hr)** | 1000+ | 600+ | 200 | 300 | 0.25 |
| **Payload (kg)** | 500+ | 2000+ | 1500+ | 50 | 0.20 |
| **Path Flexibility** | Low | Low | **High** | Medium | 0.20 |
| **Initial Cost (INR Lakhs)** | 5–10 | 4–8 | 30–80 | 8–15 | 0.15 |
| **Energy Efficiency** | **High** | High | Low | Medium | 0.10 |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | 0.10 |
| **Weighted Score (out of 5)** | 3.85 | 3.55 | 2.85 | 2.45 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For a high-throughput, fixed-path scenario (e.g., FMCG factory), the **belt conveyor scores highest**. For low-volume, flexible routing (e.g., a 3PL warehouse), the **AGV scores highest** despite higher cost. Always tie the recommendation to the **specific application context** given in the problem.

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transportation Cell

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions between subsystems** in a typical automated transportation cell, as studied in Module 4 case studies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Workpiece Entry"] --> node2["Vision Inspection Station"]
    node2 --> node3{"Infeed Conveyor\nPLC Decision"}
    node3 -->|"Accepted"| node4["Belt Conveyor A\nServo Driven"]
    node3 -->|"Rejected"| node5["Reject Diverter\nPneumatic"]
    node4 --> node6["Rotary Indexing Table\nGeneva Mechanism"]
    node6 --> node7["Assembly Station 1\nSCARA Robot"]
    node6 --> node8["Assembly Station 2\nSCARA Robot"]
    node6 --> node9["Assembly Station 3\nSCARA Robot"]
    node7 --> node10["Outfeed Conveyor B\nChain Driven"]
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11["Quality Check Station\nLaser Sensor"]
    node11 --> node12{"PLC Routing Logic"}
    node12 -->|"Pass"| node13["Output Palletizer"]
    node12 -->|"Rework"| node14["Recirculation Loop"]
    node14 --> node3

    ctrl1["Central PLC\nSiemens S7 1500"] -.->|Profibus| node3
    ctrl1 -.->|EtherCAT| node4
    ctrl1 -.->|EtherCAT| node6
    ctrl1 -.->|Digital IO| node5
    ctrl2["HMI Touchscreen"] -.->|Ethernet| ctrl1
    sensor1["Inductive Proximity\nSensor Array"] -.->|Feedback| node3
    sensor2["Incremental Encoder\n0.1 mm Resolution"] -.->|Feedback| node4
    sensor3["Vision Camera\nCognex In Sight"] -.->|Feedback| node2
```

### 4.2 Annotated Sequence Flow of a Pick-and-Place Transportation Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PLC as Central PLC
    participant Conv as Belt Conveyor
    participant Sens as Proximity Sensor
    participant Robot as SCARA Robot
    participant Dest as Outfeed Station

    PLC->>Conv: Start motor at 0.5 m/s
    Conv->>Sens: Part arrives at sensor zone
    Sens->>PLC: Trigger signal (DI high)
    PLC->>Conv: Stop motor (decel ramp)
    PLC->>Robot: Pick command (coordinates X,Y,Z)
    Robot->>Conv: Move to pick position
    Robot->>Robot: Vacuum gripper activates
    Robot->>Dest: Transport to outfeed
    Robot->>Dest: Place part, release vacuum
    Robot->>PLC: Cycle complete (DO pulse)
    PLC->>Conv: Restart cycle
```

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - In the **flowchart**, solid arrows represent *physical flow of workpieces*; dashed arrows represent *control/communication signals*.
> - The **Geneva mechanism** at node6 is a *classic intermittent motion device* — it advances one station every cycle, then dwells (locks) for the operation duration.
> - The **recirculation loop** (node14 → node3) is a common feature in *closed-loop manufacturing cells* for rework and quality recovery.
> - In the **sequence diagram**, notice the *handshake protocol* between PLC and conveyor: the conveyor must physically *acknowledge* stop completion before the robot descends. This prevents collision.

### 4.4 When Physical Schematics Are Required

For topics that demand **physical mechanical drawings** (e.g., Geneva wheel geometry, cam profiles), the Mermaid block diagrams above serve as a *functional substitute* because Mermaid does not natively render engineering drawings. In KTU examinations, students are expected to **hand-draw** the schematic on the answer sheet, clearly labeling:

- Pivot points
- Direction of rotation
- Velocity vectors
- Cam profile (for cam-driven indexers)
- Pin engagement angles (for Geneva wheels)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Drawing Tip:** Use a **sharp pencil, ruler, and compass**. Draw the *two extreme positions* of the driven member, then sketch the connecting path. Always indicate the **driver crank length $a$** and **center distance $b$** — these two parameters fully define the Geneva kinematics.

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define *Transportation Devices* in the context of automated machines. Differentiate between a *conveyor* and an *AGV* in terms of path flexibility and typical application.

**Model Answer:**

Transportation devices are mechatronic subsystems designed to **autonomously move workpieces, materials, or products** between workstations in an automated manufacturing cell.

| Aspect | Conveyor | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Flexibility** | Fixed path (linear or curved track) | Free path (2D plane navigation) |
| **Guidance Method** | Mechanical track or belt | Magnetic tape, laser, or SLAM |
| **Typical Application** | High-volume FMCG, bottling, assembly lines | Warehousing, 3PL logistics, hospitals |
| **Throughput** | Very high (1000+ units/hr) | Moderate (200 units/hr) |
| **Initial Cost** | Low to moderate | High (₹30–80 lakhs per unit) |

> [1 mark — Definition; 1 mark — Conveyor characteristics; 1 mark — AGV characteristics]

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** A bottling line requires 4500 bottles/hour. Each bottle is transported by a Geneva wheel with 6 slots. Calculate the **cycle time** and **indexing time** if the dwell time is 2.5 seconds.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**
- Throughput $\Theta = 4500$ bottles/hour
- Number of slots $n = 6$
- Dwell time $t_{dwell} = 2.5$ s

**Step 2 — Cycle Time:**

$$
T_c = \frac{3600}{\Theta} = \frac{3600}{4500} = 0.8 \text{ s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** The throughput must be matched to *one bottle per indexing event*. For $n = 6$ slots, the cycle time of 0.8 s represents the **time per station rotation**. The indexing time is a *fraction* of this.

**Step 3 — Indexing Time:**

For a Geneva wheel, the indexing time occupies $\frac{1}{n}$ of the cycle:

$$
t_{index} = \frac{T_c}{n} = \frac{0.8}{6} \approx 0.133 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$
t_{dwell} = T_c - t_{index} = 0.8 - 0.133 = 0.667 \text{ s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conflict Identified:** The required dwell time of 2.5 s **cannot be achieved** with $T_c = 0.8$ s. The student must conclude that either (a) the throughput is infeasible with a 6-slot Geneva, or (b) multiple parallel Geneva mechanisms are required. [1 mark — formula; 1 mark — calculation; 1 mark — feasibility comment]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**(a) (7 Marks)** A belt conveyor in a packaging plant must transport 600 kg of cartons at a constant speed of 1.2 m/s up an incline of $10°$. The coefficient of friction between the belt and the rollers is 0.03, and the system efficiency is 0.9. Calculate the **minimum motor power** required. Also state the **recommended motor rating** with a suitable safety factor.

**(b) (7 Marks)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, explain the **working principle of a Geneva mechanism** with 4 slots. Derive the relationship between the **driver crank length**, **center distance**, and **slot length** for a standard Geneva wheel. State two industrial applications.

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 600$ kg
- Velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s
- Incline $\theta = 10°$
- $\mu = 0.03$, $\eta = 0.9$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Friction Force:**

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_{friction} = 0.03 \cdot 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(10°)
$$

$$
F_{friction} = 0.03 \cdot 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9848
$$

$$
F_{friction} = 173.93 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational Force Component:**

$$
F_{gravity} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(10°)
$$

$$
F_{gravity} = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1736 = 1021.97 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Tractive Force:**

$$
F_{total} = F_{friction} + F_{gravity} = 173.93 + 1021.97 = 1195.90 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Power:**

$$
P_{min} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v}{\eta} = \frac{1195.90 \cdot 1.2}{0.9} = 1594.53 \text{ W} \approx 1.59 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommended Motor (with 1.4 safety factor):**

$$
P_{selected} = 1.4 \cdot 1.59 = 2.23 \text{ kW}
$$

**Conclusion:** Select a **standard 2.5 kW (3.35 HP)** three-phase induction motor with a VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) for soft-start and speed control.

> [2 marks — Friction calc; 2 marks — Gravity calc; 1 mark — Total force; 1.5 marks — Power formula; 0.5 marks — Final answer with safety factor]

**Model Answer for (b):**

The Geneva mechanism is an **intermittent motion device** that converts continuous rotation of a driver crank into indexed rotary motion of a driven wheel through **pin-and-slot engagement**.

**Working Principle:**
1. The driver crank rotates continuously at angular velocity $\omega_d$.
2. A **pin** mounted on the driver crank enters a **slot** in the driven wheel once per revolution.
3. As the pin travels through the slot, it rotates the driven wheel by $\frac{360°}{n}$ where $n$ is the number of slots.
4. When the pin exits the slot, a **locking disc** on the driver engages a concave arc on the driven wheel, holding it stationary (this is the *dwell phase*).

**Key Geometric Relationships (Standard Geneva):**

For a Geneva wheel with $n$ slots, the **center distance** $C$ between driver and driven shafts is:

$$
C = \frac{a}{\sin(\pi/n)}
$$

where $a$ is the **driver crank length** (pin offset from driver center).

The **slot length** $L_s$ is:

$$
L_s = a \cdot \sqrt{2 - 2 \cdot \cos(2\pi/n)} = 2 \cdot a \cdot \sin(\pi/n)
$$

**Derivation of Center Distance:**

Consider the position when the pin is at the **maximum engagement**, i.e., the line connecting the two shaft centers. The perpendicular distance from the driven shaft center to the pin is:

$$
r = a \cdot \sin(2\pi/n)
$$

For the slot to be tangent at this point, the perpendicular distance must equal the **slot's minimum width clearance** plus the offset. Setting up the geometry:

$$
\frac{C}{2} = \frac{a}{2 \cdot \sin(\pi/n)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = \frac{a}{\sin(\pi/n)}
$$

**Two Industrial Applications:**

1. **Bottle Filling Machines:** Geneva wheels index bottles through 6–12 filling heads with precise dwell for nozzle engagement.
2. **Film Projectors:** The original application — Geneva wheels advance film frames one at a time, dwelling for projection and advancing rapidly during the shutter-off interval.

> [1 mark — Diagram; 2 marks — Working principle; 2 marks — Derivation; 1 mark — Slot length formula; 1 mark — Applications]

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, describe the **case study of an Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) system** deployed in a 50,000 m² e-commerce warehouse. Discuss the guidance methods, control architecture, safety features, and economic justification (payback period).

**(b) (7 Marks)** Compare **belt conveyors, roller conveyors, and chain conveyors** with respect to (i) load capacity, (ii) inclination capability, (iii) energy efficiency, and (iv) maintenance. Recommend the most suitable type for (a) a rice mill handling 50 kg bags, and (b) a car assembly line transporting 1.2-tonne car bodies.

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Case Study: AGV Deployment in E-Commerce Warehouse**

**Background:**
A leading e-commerce company deploys a fleet of **40 AGVs** in a 50,000 m² fulfillment center to transport pick-totes from storage racks to packing stations. The AGVs replace a manual cart-pushing system that employed 60 workers.

**Guidance Method:** The warehouse uses **laser-guided navigation** (LGN). Each AGV is equipped with a rotating laser scanner that detects **reflective markers** mounted on warehouse columns. The scanner triangulates position with an accuracy of $\pm 10$ mm. The control system uses a pre-loaded digital map of the warehouse.

**Control Architecture:**
- **Fleet Management System (FMS):** Centralized server running traffic optimization algorithms. Assigns tasks to AGVs based on shortest path and battery state.
- **Onboard Controller:** Industrial PC with real-time OS, runs motion control and obstacle avoidance.
- **Communication:** Wi-Fi (5 GHz) for FMS communication; safety-rated wireless E-stop.

**Safety Features (per ISO 3691-4):**
1. **Safety-rated laser scanners** (PLd per ISO 13849) on all four sides — detect obstacles and trigger emergency stop.
2. **Bumper sensors** with 360° coverage as backup.
3. **Audible and visual alarms** (blue flashing light, horn).
4. **Speed zones** — AGV speed reduces from 1.5 m/s in open aisles to 0.3 m/s in congested areas.

**Economic Justification:**

| Parameter | Manual System | AGV System |
|---|---|---|
| Workers | 60 | 10 (supervisory) |
| Labor cost/year (₹) | 60 × 3 L = **180 L** | 10 × 4 L = **40 L** |
| AGV capex (40 units @ ₹25 L) | 0 | **1000 L** |
| Annual maintenance | 0 | **50 L** |
| **Annual savings** | — | **90 L** |

**Payback Period:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{Capex}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{1000}{90} \approx 11.1 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With additional savings from *reduced product damage* (₹30 L/year) and *increased throughput* (₹50 L/year — equivalent to 25% higher orders), the revised payback period is:

$$
\text{Payback}_{revised} = \frac{1000}{90 + 30 + 50} = \frac{1000}{170} \approx 5.9 \text{ years}
$$

This is within the industry benchmark of 5–7 years for AGV deployments.

> [2 marks — Guidance method; 2 marks — Control architecture; 1.5 marks — Safety; 1.5 marks — Economic calc]

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Belt Conveyor | Roller Conveyor | Chain Conveyor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Capacity** | Medium (up to 500 kg/m) | Heavy (up to 2000 kg/m) | Very Heavy (up to 5000 kg/m) |
| **Inclination** | Up to 30° (with chevron belt) | Up to 5° (gravity rollers) | Up to 45° (slats) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (continuous contact) | Medium (gravity-assisted possible) | Medium (chain friction losses) |
| **Maintenance** | Low (belt replacement every 2–3 years) | Low (bearing lubrication) | Medium (chain wear, lubrication) |

**Recommendation for (a) — Rice Mill, 50 kg bags:**

**Recommended: Belt Conveyor with chevron-pattern rubber belt**

**Justification:**
- Rice bags are *granular, deformable* loads that require continuous support — belt conveyors provide this.
- Inclination up to 20° is needed (multi-floor rice mills) — chevron belts offer this without slippage.
- Easy to clean (food-grade stainless steel option available) — important for hygiene.
- Energy-efficient for continuous operation.

**Recommendation for (b) — Car Assembly Line, 1.2-tonne car bodies:**

**Recommended: Chain Conveyor (specifically, a power-and-free overhead conveyor)**

**Justification:**
- Car bodies (1200 kg) exceed the practical limit of belt conveyors (≈500 kg/m).
- Chain conveyors are *industry standard* in automotive assembly (e.g., Ford, Toyota, Maruti Suzuki plants).
- Power-and-free design allows *accumulation* (cars can stop at workstations without stopping the entire line).
- Overhead mounting saves floor space and allows workers to access the car body from below.

> [2 marks — Comparison table; 2 marks — Rice mill justification; 1.5 marks — Car assembly justification; 1.5 marks — Technical depth and alternative considerations]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing **m/s** with **m/min** in the same calculation. Always convert to **SI base units** before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting Cosine Component:** When computing friction on an incline, students frequently write $\mu \cdot m \cdot g$ without the $\cos\theta$ factor. This inflates the friction force by **up to 50%**.
> 3. **Cycle Time Confusion:** Cycle time is the *time per station rotation* for a Geneva wheel, not the *time per part*. For $n$ slots, parts per cycle is $n$, not 1.
> 4. **No Safety Factor in Final Answer:** When the problem says "minimum power," do not include the safety factor. When it says "recommended motor rating," include the safety factor.
> 5. **Hand-drawn Diagram Skipped:** Even a rough diagram with labeled parts earns 1–2 marks. **Never skip the diagram**, even in numerical problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Case Studies on Transportation Devices**

**Conceptual Foundations:**
- ☐ Transportation devices = mechatronic subsystems for **autonomous material movement** in automated cells.
- ☐ Four classification axes: trajectory geometry, actuation principle, automation level, payload class.
- ☐ Selection criteria: throughput, payload, path flexibility, cost, energy, maintenance.

**Key Devices to Know Cold:**
- ☐ **Belt Conveyor** — for continuous linear transport of medium payloads, high throughput.
- ☐ **Roller Conveyor** — for heavy loads, can be gravity-powered (zero energy) for low-incline runs.
- ☐ **Chain Conveyor** — for very heavy loads, automotive assembly standard.
- ☐ **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** — for free-path 2D navigation, warehouse logistics.
- ☐ **Geneva Mechanism** — for intermittent indexing, packaging, and assembly.
- ☐ **Pneumatic Shuttle** — for clean environments, small payloads, fast cycles.

**Critical Formulas (memorize the symbol table, not the equations):**
- ☐ Throughput: $\Theta = 3600 / T_c$
- ☐ Motor Power: $P = F \cdot v / \eta$
- ☐ Tractive Force on Incline: $F = m g (\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$
- ☐ Belt Velocity: $v = \pi D N / 60$
- ☐ Geneva Center Distance: $C = a / \sin(\pi/n)$
- ☐ Geneva Slot Length: $L_s = 2a \sin(\pi/n)$

**Case Study Anchors (memorize at least 2):**
- ☐ **Amazon Kiva AGVs** — magnetic-guided (now vision-guided), 70% reduction in picking time.
- ☐ **Maruti Suzuki Assembly Line** — power-and-free overhead chain conveyor, 1.2-tonne car bodies, ±5 mm accuracy.
- ☐ **Bottling Plant Geneva Wheel** — 8-slot indexing, 480 bottles/hr typical.

**Numerical Problem Patterns:**
- ☐ Pattern 1: "Given m, v, θ, μ, η → Find motor power" (3-step calculation)
- ☐ Pattern 2: "Given throughput and dwell time → Find number of Geneva slots" (inequality-based)
- ☐ Pattern 3: "Given cycle time and parts/hour → Verify feasibility or propose redesign" (design conflict identification)

**Standard Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ☐ Mixing up *dwell time* and *indexing time* in Geneva problems.
- ☐ Forgetting to include **efficiency $\eta$** in power calculations.
- ☐ Omitting the **cosine factor** for friction on inclined conveyors.
- ☐ Failing to **identify design conflicts** when computed throughput < required throughput.
- ☐ Skipping the **labeled diagram** in descriptive answers.

**Industrial Standards Referenced in Examinations:**
- ☐ **ISO 8373** — Robot and robotic device vocabulary.
- ☐ **ISO 3691-4** — Safety of driverless industrial trucks (AGVs).
- ☐ **ISO 12100** — General principles for design — Risk assessment.
- ☐ **ISO 13849** — Safety-related parts of control systems (PL ratings).
- ☐ **EN 619** — Continuous mechanical handling equipment safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feeding and Orientation Devices: Feeding Systems (Liquid, Powder, Wire, Rod); Orientation (Passive, Active, Logical, and Electromagnetic Fields)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feeding and Orientation Devices: Feeding Systems (Liquid, Powder, Wire, Rod); Orientation (Passive, Active, Logical, and Electromagnetic Fields)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are Feeding Devices in Automated Machinery?

A **Feeding Device** (also termed a *Feeder* or *Parts Feeder*) is a sub-system of an automated machine whose fundamental function is to deliver workpieces (parts, raw stock, or materials) to the next workstation of an automated process in a **correctly timed, oriented, and quantifiable** manner. In the lexicon of KTU's PERUT743 syllabus, feeding is treated as the **bridge between bulk disordered material storage and ordered discrete-event assembly**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):**
> "A feeder is a mechanism that takes randomly arranged parts from a bulk supply, separates them, and presents them one at a time to the next station in a prescribed orientation and at a prescribed rate."

In broader engineering practice, feeding systems span the handling of materials in four primary physical states:
1. **Liquids** (oils, paints, resins, beverages, molten metals)
2. **Powders/Granular Solids** (sugar, cement, pharmaceutical powders, plastic pellets)
3. **Wires/Cables** (copper wire, steel cable, optical fibre, solder)
4. **Rods/Bar Stock** (cylindrical rods, square bars, tubular stock)

Each of these demands a unique mechanical or pneumatic strategy because of differences in **stiffness, surface friction, viscosity, and flow behaviour**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen Pipeline**
> Imagine a busy Indian restaurant kitchen during lunch rush.
> - The **bulk storage** is the refrigerator and rice bin — disordered.
> - The **feeding system** is the chef and the ladle — they scoop a controlled portion of curry (liquid), sprinkle measured pepper (powder), pull noodles (wire), and cut cucumber sticks (rod).
> - The **orientation** is the chef arranging each cucumber stick parallel on the cutting board so the customer receives a uniform plate.
> - The **next station** is the waiter's tray.
>
> Without controlled feeding, the kitchen cannot deliver plates uniformly. Similarly, automated assembly fails without controlled feeding.

## 1.3 What is Orientation in Automated Machines?

**Orientation** is the act of positioning a workpiece into a unique, repeatable geometric configuration (a "pose") before the next process begins. The KTU syllabus categorizes orientation devices into **four canonical classes**:

| Class | Mechanism | Energy Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | Geometry (chutes, slots, recesses) | Gravity / part weight | Bottle caps, screws |
| **Active** | Moving tooling (blades, fingers) | Pneumatic / servo motor | Bolts, springs |
| **Logical** | Sensors + PLC decision making | Electronics + software | Mixed-model assembly |
| **Electromagnetic Field** | Magnetic / electrostatic forces | Magnetic flux, electric field | Ferrous parts, foils |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Feeding

The following design metrics recur across all feeder classes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Feed Rate (ṁ or n):** measured in **parts/minute (ppm)** or **kg/hour**
> - **Cycle Time (Tc):** seconds per part
> - **Feed Efficiency (ηf):** **ηf = N_correct / N_total**, typically **85%–99%**
> - **Standard gravitational acceleration: g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Viscosity of water (reference): μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s at 20°C**

## 1.5 Why Feeding & Orientation Matter in PERUT743

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's PERUT743 framework, **feeding is classified as a "secondary motion"** of an automated machine. The **primary motion** performs the working stroke (e.g., pressing, welding, cutting), while the **secondary motion** delivers and aligns the workpiece. Without secondary motion, the primary motion has nothing to act upon. Hence, a poorly designed feeder bottlenecks the entire line and reduces the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Feeding Systems by Material State

### 2.1.1 Liquid Feeding Systems

Liquids have **zero shear modulus at rest** and deform continuously under applied stress. Feeding them requires either **gravitational head**, **positive-displacement pumping**, or **pressure differential**.

**Sub-types:**
- **Gravity feed tanks** — liquid flows under hydrostatic pressure difference.
- **Piston pumps** — deliver a fixed volume per stroke.
- **Peristaltic pumps** — compress a flexible tube to displace fluid.
- **Siphon feed** — used in small-volume dispensing.

**Core governing equation — Hagen–Poiseuille flow:**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, r^{4}}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across pipe (Pa)
- $r$ = internal pipe radius (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = pipe length (m)

> The **fourth-power dependence on radius** is critical: doubling the pipe diameter increases flow by **16×** at constant pressure.

### 2.1.2 Powder Feeding Systems

Powders are **particulate solids** that exhibit complex flow regimes: **gravity flow, funnelling, arching, and rat-holing**. Feeder design must overcome the **cohesive forces** between particles.

**Sub-types:**
- **Vibratory feeders** — electromagnetic vibration advances material along a track.
- **Auger (screw) feeders** — rotating helical screw pushes powder through a tube.
- **Rotary valve feeders** — used in pneumatic conveying.
- **Loss-in-weight feeders** — gravimetric, with closed-loop weight control.

**Core governing equation — Powder flow rate from a hopper (Beverloo's equation):**

$$W \;=\; C \, \rho_{b} \, \sqrt{g} \, (D - k \, d_{p})^{2.5}$$

Where:
- $W$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $C$ = discharge coefficient (dimensionless, typically **0.55–0.65**)
- $\rho_{b}$ = bulk density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $D$ = orifice diameter (m)
- $k$ = empirical constant ≈ **1.4**
- $d_{p}$ = particle diameter (m)

### 2.1.3 Wire Feeding Systems

Wires are **continuous 1-D flexible elements** characterized by high length-to-cross-section ratio. Feeding them requires **overcoming bending stiffness and friction in guide nozzles**.

**Sub-types:**
- **Roll-pair feeders** (drive rolls + idler rolls) — used in welding wire feed.
- **Capstan feeders** — wire wraps around a rotating drum.
- **Linear feeders** — for stiff wire stock.

**Core governing equation — Wire tension in a capstan (Euler–Eytelwein):**

$$\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \;=\; e^{\mu \theta}$$

Where:
- $T_{1}$ = tension on the tight side (N)
- $T_{2}$ = tension on the slack side (N)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between wire and drum
- $\theta$ = wrap angle (radians)

> This **exponential** relationship means a small increase in friction or wrap angle produces disproportionately large increases in traction.

### 2.1.4 Rod Feeding Systems

Rods are **rigid or semi-rigid linear stock** with finite bending stiffness. They are fed by **reciprocating collets**, **gravity chutes**, or **magazine-indexed escapements**.

**Sub-types:**
- **Magazine feeders** — rods stacked in a sloping magazine, advanced by escapement.
- **Pneumatic rod feeders** — rods blown through tubes.
- **Reciprocating finger feeders** — used in small-engine assembly.

**Core governing equation — Buckling load under axial feed (Euler):**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} \, E \, I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

Where:
- $P_{cr}$ = critical buckling load (N)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)
- $I$ = second moment of area (m⁴)
- $L_{e}$ = effective length (m)

> A slender rod fed axially through a long guide will **buckle** if the feeding force exceeds $P_{cr}$. Designers limit feed force to **< 0.5 × $P_{cr}$** for safety.

## 2.2 Orientation Devices — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Passive Orientation

Passive orientation **relies solely on part geometry and gravity**, with no powered actuators. Examples:
- **Vibratory bowl with cast track** — part geometry rejects wrong orientations.
- **Chute with curved section** — part rotates by sliding.
- **Slotted conveyor** — only correctly oriented parts fit slots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Passive orientation is **robust, inexpensive, and silent**, but it is **part-specific**. A change of part requires a complete tooling re-design.

### 2.2.2 Active Orientation

Active orientation uses **powered moving elements** (blades, fingers, deflectors) to flip or reorient parts that arrive in the wrong pose.
- **Blades and wipers** deflect wrongly oriented parts into a reject chute.
- **Robotic pick-and-place** picks randomly oriented parts and places them correctly.
- **Rotary pockets with trap cams** flip parts as they pass through.

### 2.2.3 Logical Orientation

Logical orientation uses **sensors, programmable logic controllers (PLCs), and software** to detect part orientation and command actuators to correct it.
- **Vision cameras** identify the part's pose.
- **PLC** decides what action is needed.
- **Actuator** rotates/repositions.

> This is the foundation of **Industry 4.0 / smart manufacturing**.

### 2.2.4 Electromagnetic Field Orientation

Electromagnetic orientation uses **magnetic flux, eddy currents, or electrostatic fields** to manipulate parts.
- **Permanent magnet chutes** orient ferromagnetic parts.
- **Electromagnetic coils** (solenoids) flip parts in step with a conveyor.
- **Electrostatic orientation** is used for thin films, foils, and non-ferrous conductive parts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is examinable in KTU ESE — memorize the form, units, and physical meaning of each equation.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX-friendly, no `vert` needed) | Physical Meaning | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille liquid flow | Liquid dispenser design |
| 2 | $W = C \rho_{b} \sqrt{g} \,(D - k d_{p})^{2.5}$ | Powder mass flow from hopper | Vibratory/auger feeder |
| 3 | $\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | Capstan friction (Euler–Eytelwein) | Wire feed traction |
| 4 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$ | Euler buckling of slender rod | Rod feeding through guides |
| 5 | $\eta_{f} = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}}$ | Feed efficiency | OEE calculation |
| 6 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality | Plant-level productivity |
| 7 | $T_{c} = \dfrac{60}{n}$ | Cycle time vs. feed rate (n in ppm) | Machine throughput |
| 8 | $\rho_{b} = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | Bulk density of powder | Hopper sizing |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These four feeder classes underpin **virtually every high-volume manufacturing industry**:
- **Pharmaceutical** — powder feeders for tablet presses; liquid feeders for syrups.
- **Automotive** — wire feeders for robotic welding; rod feeders for bolt assembly.
- **Packaging** — liquid fillers for beverages; powder fillers for detergent.
- **Electronics** — wire bonders; rod feeders for connector pins.

KTU examiners consistently ask: *"Why did the designer select this feeder class?"* The answer is always traced back to **part state, throughput, and accuracy** — the three pillars of feeder selection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille Liquid Flow Rate

We start from the **Navier–Stokes equation** for steady, laminar, fully developed flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a horizontal cylindrical pipe:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \;=\; \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left( r \frac{\partial v_{x}}{\partial r} \right) \right]$$

**Step 1: Assume fully developed flow**, so the pressure gradient is constant along $x$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} \;=\; -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \;=\; \text{constant}$$

**Step 2: Substitute and integrate twice with respect to r:**

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \frac{dv_{x}}{dr} \right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

Multiplying by $r$ and integrating once:

$$r \frac{dv_{x}}{dr} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta P \, r^{2}}{2 \mu L} + C_{1}$$

**Step 3: Apply the no-slip boundary condition** $v_{x} = 0$ at $r = R$ (pipe wall):

$$0 \;=\; -\frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{2 \mu L} + C_{1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{1} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{2 \mu L}$$

**Step 4: Divide by $r$ and integrate again:**

$$v_{x}(r) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta P \, r^{2}}{4 \mu L} + \frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,(R^{2} - r^{2})$$

This is the **parabolic velocity profile** — the velocity is maximum at $r=0$ (centre) and zero at $r=R$ (wall).

**Step 5: Compute the volumetric flow rate** by integrating over the cross-section:

$$Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} v_{x}(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^{2} - r^{2}) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} r - r^{3}) \, dr \;=\; \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L}\left[ \frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L}\!\left( \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} \right) \;=\; \frac{\pi \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$$

> **Final form:**

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \, \mu \, L}\,}$$

**Real-world implication:** A pharmaceutical liquid dispenser must increase pipe radius **only slightly** to dramatically increase delivery rate. Doubling $R$ multiplies $Q$ by **16×**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euler–Eytelwein Capstan Equation for Wire Feed

A wire of tension $T_{2}$ (slack side) wraps around a cylindrical drum over a small elemental arc $d\theta$. The wire is being pulled by tension $T_{1}$ on the tight side. Friction $\mu N$ resists motion, where $N$ is the normal force from the drum on the wire.

**Step 1: Force balance on the element:**

For the element subtending angle $d\theta$:
- Tangential direction: $dT = \mu N$
- Normal direction: $N = T \, d\theta$ (since the drum pushes the wire outward with a force equal to the tension times the subtended angle)

**Step 2: Substitute:**

$$dT \;=\; \mu \, T \, d\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dT}{T} \;=\; \mu \, d\theta$$

**Step 3: Integrate from $T_{2}$ at $\theta = 0$ to $T_{1}$ at $\theta = \theta_{wrap}$:**

$$\int_{T_{2}}^{T_{1}} \frac{dT}{T} \;=\; \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln\!\left(\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right) \;=\; \mu \theta$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate:**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \;=\; e^{\mu \theta}\,}$$

> **Design insight:** To increase wire-feed traction by 10×, the designer can either increase wrap angle from $\pi/2$ to $\pi/2 + \Delta\theta$ (costly) or increase friction $\mu$ by choosing a rubber-lined drum (cheap).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Euler Buckling of a Slender Rod in a Feed Tube

A slender rod of length $L_{e}$, pinned at both ends, is compressed axially by a force $P$. The governing differential equation for the deflected shape is:

$$E \, I \, \frac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -P \, y$$

**Step 1: Solve the ODE.** Assume solution $y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$, where $k^{2} = P/(EI)$:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -k^{2} y \;\;\text{(satisfied by the sine/cosine trial)}$$

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions** (pinned–pinned): $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L_{e}) = 0$.

From $y(0) = 0$: $\;B = 0$.
From $y(L_{e}) = 0$: $\;A \sin(k L_{e}) = 0$.

For a non-trivial solution ($A \neq 0$):

$$\sin(k L_{e}) \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k L_{e} \;=\; n \pi, \;\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 3: Solve for the critical (lowest) buckling load** ($n = 1$):

$$k_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{L_{e}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_{cr} \;=\; k_{cr}^{2} \, E I \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}\,}$$

**Step 4: Substituting $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$ for a solid circular rod of diameter $d$:**

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{L_{e}^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{3} E d^{4}}{64 L_{e}^{2}}$$

> **Numerical example (KTU-style):**
> A steel rod ($E = 200$ GPa, $d = 8$ mm, $L_{e} = 0.5$ m) yields:
>
> $$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{3} \cdot 200 \times 10^{9} \cdot (0.008)^{4}}{64 \cdot (0.5)^{2}} \;\approx\; 317 \text{ N}$$
>
> The safe feed force is **0.5 × 317 ≈ 158 N**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Feeder Class

The following matrix helps a designer pick the correct feeder class. This is a **high-yield KTU 14-mark answer**.

| Material State | Throughput Required | Accuracy Required | Recommended Feeder Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-viscosity liquid | Low (< 1 L/min) | High | Siphon + valve | Gravity, simple |
| High-viscosity liquid | High | High | Positive-displacement piston | Accurate volume |
| Free-flowing powder | High | Moderate | Vibratory feeder | Avoids arching |
| Cohesive powder | Moderate | High | Auger feeder | Overcomes cohesion |
| Fine wire (welding) | High | Moderate | Roll-pair feeder | Continuous delivery |
| Stiff wire (cable) | Low | High | Capstan feeder | High traction |
| Long slender rod | High | Moderate | Magazine + escapement | Standardized |
| Short rod | Low | High | Pneumatic rod feeder | Precise indexing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:**
> "If the material flows under its own weight, it is a *gravity feeder*; if it must be pushed, it is a *positive-displacement feeder*; if it must be vibrated, it is a *vibratory feeder*."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Automated Feeding System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["BULK STORAGE HOPPER OR BIN"]
    nodeB["PRIMARY FEEDING SUBSYSTEM"]
    nodeC["ORIENTATION SUBSYSTEM"]
    nodeD["INSPECTION AND VERIFICATION"]
    nodeE["DELIVERY CHUTE OR ESCAPEMENT"]
    nodeF["WORKSTATION INDEX TABLE"]
    nodeG["REJECT LANE FOR MISALIGNED PARTS"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- "PART CORRECTLY ORIENTED" --> nodeE
    nodeD -- "PART MISALIGNED" --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -. "SIGNAL FEEDBACK FOR SYNCHRONIZATION" .-> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Material leaves bulk storage, is metered by the feeder, oriented by the orientation subsystem, inspected by sensors (logical orientation), and either accepted or rejected. The arrow from the workstation back to the feeder represents the **synchronization signal** that paces feeding with downstream demand.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree — All Feeder Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["FEEDING AND ORIENTATION DEVICES"]

    root --> liquid["LIQUID FEEDERS"]
    root --> powder["POWDER FEEDERS"]
    root --> wire["WIRE FEEDERS"]
    root --> rod["ROD FEEDERS"]
    root --> orient["ORIENTATION DEVICES"]

    liquid --> L1["GRAVITY TANK"]
    liquid --> L2["PISTON PUMP"]
    liquid --> L3["PERISTALTIC PUMP"]
    liquid --> L4["SIPHON"]

    powder --> P1["VIBRATORY BOWL"]
    powder --> P2["AUGER SCREW"]
    powder --> P3["ROTARY VALVE"]
    powder --> P4["LOSS IN WEIGHT"]

    wire --> W1["ROLL PAIR FEEDER"]
    wire --> W2["CAPSTAN DRUM"]
    wire --> W3["LINEAR PUSHER"]

    rod --> R1["MAGAZINE ESCAPEMENT"]
    rod --> R2["RECIPROCATING COLLET"]
    rod --> R3["PNEUMATIC TUBE FEEDER"]

    orient --> O1["PASSIVE ORIENTATION"]
    orient --> O2["ACTIVE ORIENTATION"]
    orient --> O3["LOGICAL ORIENTATION"]
    orient --> O4["ELECTROMAGNETIC ORIENTATION"]

    O1 --> O1a["Chutes and slides"]
    O1 --> O1b["Vibratory bowl tracks"]

    O2 --> O2a["Blades and wipers"]
    O2 --> O2b["Pneumatic fingers"]

    O3 --> O3a["Vision system and PLC"]
    O3 --> O3b["Sensor based decision"]

    O4 --> O4a["Permanent magnet chute"]
    O4 --> O4b["Solenoid flipper"]
```

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Operational Flow of a Vibratory Bowl Feeder with Passive Orientation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Hopper as Bulk Hopper
    participant Bowl as Vibratory Bowl
    participant Track as Cast Orientation Track
    participant Orient as Passive Orientor
    participant Output as Output Chute
    participant Reject as Reject Lane

    Hopper->>Bowl: Powder or parts in bulk
    Bowl->>Bowl: Electromagnetic vibration at 50 to 100 Hz
    Bowl->>Track: Parts spiral upward along inclined track
    Track->>Orient: Each part encounters a notch or step
    Orient-->>Output: Correctly oriented part continues forward
    Orient-->>Reject: Wrongly oriented part falls back into bowl
    Output->>Output: Singulated part leaves at fixed rate
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — The Four Orientation Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Passive["PASSIVE ORIENTATION"]
        PA["Geometry only"] --> PB["Gravity + part weight"]
    end

    subgraph Active["ACTIVE ORIENTATION"]
        AC["Moving blade or finger"] --> AD["Pneumatic or servo actuation"]
    end

    subgraph Logical["LOGICAL ORIENTATION"]
        LG["Sensor detects pose"] --> LH["PLC decides correction"] --> LI["Actuator repositions"]
    end

    subgraph EM["ELECTROMAGNETIC ORIENTATION"]
        EM1["Magnetic flux"] --> EM2["Solenoid or permanent magnet flips part"]
    end

    Input["Randomly oriented part"] --> Passive
    Passive --> Output1["Correctly oriented part"]
    Input --> Active
    Active --> Output1
    Input --> Logical
    Logical --> Output1
    Input --> EM
    EM --> Output1
```

---

## 4.5 Force / Energy Schematic — Capstan Wire Feed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spool["WIRE SPOOL T2 SLACK"] --> drum["CAPSTAN DRUM"]
    drum -- "WRAP ANGLE THETA" --> drum
    drum --> outlet["OUTLET T1 TIGHT"]
    drum -- "FRICTION FORCE MU N" --> drum

    style spool fill:#FFD700
    style drum fill:#4682B4
    style outlet fill:#90EE90
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between $T_1$, $T_2$, $\mu$, and $\theta$ is given by the Euler–Eytelwein equation derived in Section 3.2. The capstan drum is the **traction-enhancing element** in wire feeders.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

### Q1. Define a "Feeding Device" and list the four primary classes of feeders used in automated machinery based on the physical state of the material handled. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A feeding device is a mechanism that receives bulk, randomly arranged parts or materials from a storage hopper and delivers them **one at a time, in a prescribed orientation, and at a prescribed rate** to the next workstation.

The four primary classes based on material state are:
1. **Liquid feeders** (gravity tanks, piston pumps, peristaltic pumps)
2. **Powder feeders** (vibratory bowls, auger screws, rotary valves)
3. **Wire feeders** (roll-pair feeders, capstan drums)
4. **Rod feeders** (magazine escapements, pneumatic tube feeders)

> Each class is selected based on the **physical state**, **throughput**, and **accuracy** required.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **Passive Orientation** and **Logical Orientation** devices with one example of each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Passive Orientation | Logical Orientation |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Gravity / part geometry only | Sensors + PLC + software |
| **Flexibility** | Part-specific; needs retooling | Adaptable via program change |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Speed** | Very high | Limited by sensor/decision speed |
| **Example** | Vibratory bowl with cast track | Vision-guided robot pick-and-place |

> Passive orientation is used in high-volume, single-product lines (e.g., bottle caps), whereas logical orientation is used in **flexible, mixed-model assembly** (e.g., automotive Industry 4.0 lines).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Choose ONE — 14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Module 4 Pattern]`**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of a **vibratory bowl feeder** for powder/part feeding. Discuss how **passive orientation** is achieved in such a feeder. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pharmaceutical powder with bulk density $\rho_b = 600$ kg/m³ is discharged through a circular orifice of diameter $D = 50$ mm. The particle diameter is $d_p = 1.0$ mm, and the empirical constants are $C = 0.58$, $k = 1.4$. Compute the **mass flow rate** of powder using Beverloo's equation. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

A **vibratory bowl feeder** consists of:
- A **bowl** with a helical track cast into its inner wall.
- An **electromagnetic vibrator** mounted beneath the bowl.
- A **base** with leaf springs that allow the bowl to oscillate vertically and torsionally.
- A **power supply** providing AC at 50 Hz (with half-wave rectification for 100 Hz mechanical pulses).

**Working principle:**
The electromagnetic coil pulls the bowl downward against the springs; the springs then snap the bowl back up. This **50–100 Hz vertical–torsional oscillation** causes parts resting on the track to **jump forward** in micro-steps, climbing the helical track.

**Passive orientation** is achieved by **machining specific features into the track**:
- **Notches** that only accept a particular feature of the part (e.g., the head of a screw).
- **Slots** that allow one orientation but reject the flipped one.
- **Steps** that only certain sized features can pass.
- **Recesses** that capture and flip the part as it climbs.

> Parts arriving in the wrong orientation **fall back into the bowl** and re-enter the track until they land correctly.

#### Model Solution — (b)

Given data:
- $\rho_b = 600$ kg/m³
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m
- $d_p = 1.0$ mm $= 0.001$ m
- $C = 0.58$
- $k = 1.4$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Compute $(D - k d_p)$:**

$$D - k \, d_p \;=\; 0.050 - (1.4)(0.001) \;=\; 0.050 - 0.0014 \;=\; 0.0486 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Raise to the power 2.5:**

$$(0.0486)^{2.5} \;=\; (0.0486)^{2} \cdot (0.0486)^{0.5} \;=\; (0.002362) \cdot (0.2204) \;\approx\; 5.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2.5}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sqrt{g}$:**

$$\sqrt{g} \;=\; \sqrt{9.81} \;\approx\; 3.132 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Apply Beverloo's equation:**

$$W \;=\; C \, \rho_b \, \sqrt{g} \, (D - k d_p)^{2.5}$$

$$W \;=\; (0.58)(600)(3.132)(5.21 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$W \;\approx\; 0.568 \text{ kg/s} \;\approx\; 2045 \text{ kg/hour}$$

> **Answer:** The mass flow rate is approximately **0.568 kg/s (≈ 2045 kg/hr)**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a schematic diagram, describe the **capstan-type wire feeder** mechanism used in welding applications. Derive the capstan (Euler–Eytelwein) equation relating tight-side tension, slack-side tension, friction coefficient, and wrap angle. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A copper wire (coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$ against a rubber-lined capstan drum) is wrapped over an arc of $\theta = \pi$ radians (180°). The wire is pulled against a tension $T_2 = 20$ N. Compute the **tight-side tension** $T_1$ and the **maximum feed force** the wire can sustain without slipping. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Construction:**
- A **drive motor** (often DC servo) rotates a **capstan drum**.
- A **rubber or polyurethane lining** on the drum provides high friction.
- The wire enters from the **pay-off spool** at tension $T_2$ (slack), wraps around the drum over angle $\theta$, and exits toward the welding torch at tension $T_1$ (tight).
- A **wire straightener** upstream removes residual coil curvature.

**Working:**
The drum rotates, dragging the wire forward via friction. Because the wrap is more than 180°, the friction force is multiplied by the capstan effect.

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.2 of these notes.)

Starting from $dT = \mu N$ and $N = T d\theta$, integrate to obtain:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} = e^{\mu \theta}\,}$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

Given:
- $\mu = 0.25$
- $\theta = \pi$ rad
- $T_2 = 20$ N

**Step 1: Compute the exponent:**

$$\mu \theta \;=\; (0.25)(\pi) \;=\; 0.7854$$

**Step 2: Compute the capstan ratio:**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} \;=\; e^{0.7854} \;\approx\; 2.193$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_1$:**

$$T_1 \;=\; 2.193 \times 20 \;=\; 43.87 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Maximum feed force = $T_1$:**

The maximum force that can be sustained at the tight side before slip is:

$$F_{max} \;\approx\; T_1 \;\approx\; \mathbf{43.9 \text{ N}}$$

> **Answer:** The tight-side tension is **≈ 43.9 N**, which is the maximum feed force the wire can sustain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in Beverloo's calculation — especially mixing m and mm.
> 2. **Confusing wrap angle in degrees vs radians** in the Euler–Eytelwein equation (always use radians in the exponent).
> 3. **Stating "passive orientation = gravity feed"** — wrong; passive orientation is by **geometry**, not by gravity alone.
> 4. **Mixing active and logical orientation** — remember: active = powered mechanical; logical = sensor + PLC software.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the no-slip boundary condition** when deriving Hagen–Poiseuille.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

### A. Feeding Systems
- ☐ A feeder delivers parts **one at a time, oriented, and on schedule**.
- ☐ **Liquid**: Hagen–Poiseuille $Q = \pi \Delta P R^{4} / (8\mu L)$ — fourth-power on radius.
- ☐ **Powder**: Beverloo $W = C \rho_b \sqrt{g} (D - k d_p)^{2.5}$ — applies to gravity discharge.
- ☐ **Wire**: Euler–Eytelwein $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ — exponential friction multiplier.
- ☐ **Rod**: Euler buckling $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ — limit feed force to $0.5 P_{cr}$.
- ☐ Feed efficiency $\eta_f = N_{correct}/N_{total}$; OEE $= A \times P \times Q$.

### B. Orientation Devices
- ☐ **Passive** = geometry + gravity; example: vibratory bowl with cast track.
- ☐ **Active** = powered moving tooling; example: pneumatic blade wiper.
- ☐ **Logical** = sensor + PLC decision; example: vision-guided pick-and-place.
- ☐ **Electromagnetic** = magnetic/electrostatic field; example: solenoid flipper for ferrous caps.

### C. Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ☐ Selection rule: match feeder to **material state, throughput, accuracy**.
- ☐ Use **gravity feed** if material flows easily; use **positive displacement** if not.
- ☐ Use **passive orientation** for high-volume dedicated lines; use **logical orientation** for flexible mixed-model lines.
- ☐ **Electromagnetic** orientation only works on **ferrous or conductive** parts.
- ☐ Always apply **safety factor 0.5** on Euler buckling for rod feed design.

### D. Common KTU Question Types
- ☐ "Compare passive and active orientation." (3-mark short answer)
- ☐ "Derive the capstan equation and apply it numerically." (7-mark derivation)
- ☐ "A powder has bulk density X. Find the flow rate from a hopper of diameter Y." (7-mark numerical)
- ☐ "Sketch and explain a vibratory bowl feeder." (7-mark descriptive)
- ☐ "Differentiate the four orientation classes in a table." (7-mark compare)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Systems and Manipulators: Automatic Assembling and Inspection Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functional Systems and Manipulators: Automatic Assembling and Inspection Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Automatic Assembly Systems — Formal Definition

An **Automatic Assembly System** is an integrated arrangement of workstations, parts feeders, transfer mechanisms, and control units that performs the joining of two or more components into a finished sub-assembly or product without direct human intervention in the joining operation. According to KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, it is a *Flexible Manufacturing Subsystem* whose primary goal is the **mechanical, thermal, or chemical joining** of mating parts with repeatable positioning accuracy (typically ±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm in standard cells, and ±0.01 mm in precision cells).

### 1.2 Automatic Inspection Systems — Formal Definition

An **Automatic Inspection System** is a coordinated set of sensors, gauging fixtures, vision hardware, and decision logic that automatically verifies the dimensional integrity, surface quality, presence/absence of features, and functional characteristics of a component or assembly. In KTU parlance, this aligns with **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** integration, **Machine Vision (MV)** systems, and **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Focus:**
> The module specifically links *manipulators* (industrial robots), *functional systems* (mechanisms that perform a defined utility), and *inspection* as a closed-loop feedback pillar of modern automated factories. Expect questions testing the **interdependence** between these three subsystems.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **packing a lunchbox** every morning:

- **Picking up the roti** (or bread) = the **manipulator's pick-and-place action**.
- **Placing it carefully beside the sabzi without spilling** = **automatic assembly** (precise mating).
- **Glancing at the box to confirm the compartments are filled correctly** = **automatic inspection** (verification).
- **If something is missing, stopping to add it** = **closed-loop feedback control**.

A factory cell does this thousands of times per hour, but with micron-level precision, force-controlled mating, and vision-based verification.

### 1.4 Design Philosophy of Functional Systems

A **functional system** in the KTU sense is *any mechanical arrangement whose purpose is defined by the function it performs, not by its kinematic structure*. Examples include:

| Functional System | Defined Purpose |
|---|---|
| Indexing Table | Stepwise positioning of parts |
| Bowl Feeder | Orienting and singulating parts |
| Pick-and-Place Unit | Transferring parts between stations |
| Vision Inspection Station | Verifying dimensional/geometric correctness |

> [!NOTE]
> The **manipulator** (industrial robot) is the *active* agent, while the **functional system** is the *supporting* infrastructure. They are complementary: the manipulator cannot assemble without feeders, and feeders cannot assemble without an active joining agent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Sub-Systems of an Automatic Assembly Cell

For exam answers, you must remember that an automatic assembling system is rarely a single machine — it is an orchestrated **six-station architecture**:

1. **Parts Feeding & Orienting System** — Bowls, hoppers, magazine escapements.
2. **Transfer / Transport System** — Conveyors, indexing tables, walking-beam.
3. **Assembly Mechanism (Manipulator)** — 4-axis SCARA, 6-axis articulated robot, or Cartesian gantry.
4. **Joining Process Module** — Snap-fit, press-fit, threaded fastening, adhesive dispense, ultrasonic weld.
5. **Inspection / Verification System** — Vision cameras, LVDTs, pneumatic gauges, torque sensors.
6. **Control System (PLC / PC-based)** — Sequencing logic, fault handling, recipe management.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Station

- **Why feed?** Parts arrive in bulk and need to be presented *one at a time* in a known orientation.
- **Why transfer?** Stations are spatially separated; the part must move with sub-millisecond repeatability.
- **Why a dedicated joining module?** Speed and force profile of joining differ from pick-and-place; specialised end-effectors reduce cycle time.
- **Why inspect?** Quality cannot be inspected *into* a product — it must be verified at every critical station (KTU emphasises **100% online inspection** as the new benchmark).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **rapid-revision arsenal**. Memorise units and boundary conditions; examiners love testing unit-conversion errors.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Production Rate | $P_r = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Parts per hour from cycle time | parts/hr |
| 2 | Cycle Time | $T_c = T_a + T_t + T_i$ | Assembly + transfer + inspection time | seconds |
| 3 | Uptime Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{op}}{T_{op} + T_{down}}$ | Availability of the cell | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $OEE = A \cdot P \cdot Q$ | $A$ availability, $P$ performance, $Q$ quality | dimensionless |
| 5 | Inspection Confidence | $C = 1 - \beta$ | $\beta$ is Type-II error (missed defect) | dimensionless |
| 6 | Singulation Rate | $R_s = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{T_{feed}}$ | Parts fed per second by bowl feeder | parts/s |
| 7 | Robot Repeatability | $\Delta r$ | Manufacturer-stated positioning scatter | mm |
| 8 | Press-Fit Force | $F_p = f \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot p$ | $f$ friction, $d$ dia, $L$ length, $p$ interference | N |
| 9 | Vision Resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Pixels}}$ | Millimetre per pixel of camera | mm/pixel |
| 10 | Throughput | $\text{Th} = \dfrac{N}{t}$ | Number of assemblies in time $t$ | assemblies/hr |

### 2.4 The Inspection Equation — Detail

For a **vision inspection station**, the smallest detectable feature $F$ is governed by:

$$F = \dfrac{\text{Field of View}}{\text{Number of Pixels in Sensor}} \cdot 2$$

The factor of **2** arises from the Nyquist sampling criterion: a defect must be sampled by *at least two* pixels to be reliably detected. KTU examiners frequently test this — remember the factor!

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: 90+ automated assembly stations per engine line; typical cycle time **60–90 seconds**.
- **Electronics (SMT)**: Pick-and-place machines mount 50,000 components per hour with **±0.04 mm** placement accuracy.
- **Pharmaceutical**: Vision inspection detects tablet defects at line speeds exceeding **200,000 units/hr**.
- **Aerospace**: Automated inspection with laser trackers achieves **±0.025 mm/m** volumetric accuracy.

### 2.6 The "Rule of Ten" in Inspection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Concept:**
> The cost of catching a defect increases **10×** at every subsequent stage. Catching a defect *in-process* costs ₹10; catching it after assembly costs ₹100; catching it after shipping costs ₹1000; catching it in the field costs ₹10,000. This is why **automatic in-line inspection** is non-negotiable in modern design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Production Rate from Cycle Time

**Statement:** Given a single-station assembly cell with cycle time $T_c$ in seconds, derive the production rate $P_r$ in parts per hour.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In one cycle, 1 part is produced.} \\
\text{In 1 second, } \dfrac{1}{T_c} \text{ parts are produced.} \\
\text{In 60 seconds (1 minute), } \dfrac{60}{T_c} \text{ parts are produced.} \\
\text{In 3600 seconds (1 hour), } \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \text{ parts are produced.} \\
\therefore P_r = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \;\; \left[ \dfrac{\text{parts}}{\text{hr}} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Conversion Logic:** The constant **3600** is the conversion factor from *seconds* to *hours* (60 × 60). This derivation appears in nearly every KTU Numerical Question on Module 4.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

**Statement:** OEE is the multiplicative product of three independent loss factors. Show how each is derived.

#### 3.2.1 Availability ($A$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \\
  &= \dfrac{T_{op} - T_{down}}{T_{op}} \\
  &= 1 - \dfrac{T_{down}}{T_{op}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.2.2 Performance ($P$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Parts Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}} \\
  &= \dfrac{T_{ideal} \cdot N}{T_{op}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.2.3 Quality ($Q$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{\text{Good Parts Produced}}{\text{Total Parts Produced}} \\
  &= 1 - \dfrac{N_{defect}}{N_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.2.4 Combined OEE

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \cdot P \cdot Q \\
    &= \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{down}}{T_{op}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{ideal} \cdot N}{T_{op}}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{defect}}{N_{total}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why multiplicative?** Because the three losses occur *independently*. A downtime event does not preclude a quality defect — they multiply rather than add.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Cycle Time to Production Rate

**Problem:** A single-station automatic assembly cell performs snap-fit insertion in **18 seconds**, transfer takes **4 seconds**, and vision inspection takes **3 seconds**. The cell operates for **7 hours** per shift with a **95%** uptime efficiency.

**Step 1: Calculate cycle time.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= T_a + T_t + T_i \\
    &= 18 + 4 + 3 \\
    &= 25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate theoretical production rate.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \\
    &= \dfrac{3600}{25} \\
    &= 144 \text{ parts/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate operating time per shift.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{op} &= 7 \times 3600 \times \eta \\
       &= 7 \times 3600 \times 0.95 \\
       &= 23{,}940 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate actual parts produced per shift.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{T_{op}}{T_c} \\
  &= \dfrac{23{,}940}{25} \\
  &= 957.6 \approx 957 \text{ parts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **957 parts per shift**, with a *maximum theoretical capacity* of 1008 parts (7 × 144). The deficit of 51 parts is the availability loss captured by $\eta = 0.95$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Inspection Methods

| Parameter | Contact (CMM) | Non-Contact (Vision) | Laser Triangulation | Eddy Current |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±0.001 mm | ±0.01 mm | ±0.005 mm | ±0.02 mm |
| Speed | Slow | Very Fast | Fast | Fast |
| Surface Restriction | None | Lighting dependent | Material dependent | Conductive only |
| Best Use | Final QA | 100% in-line | Micro-features | Crack detection |
| KTU Exam Cue | "Quality assurance" | "Real-time inspection" | "Micro-defects" | "Subsurface defects" |

---

### 3.5 Press-Fit Force — Derivation Insight

For an interference fit (KTU frequently tests this), the axial force required to press a pin of diameter $d$ into a hub of interference $p$ over length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= f \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot p \\
\text{Where } f &= \text{Coefficient of friction (typically 0.05–0.15 for steel)} \\
p &= \text{Diametral interference (mm)} \\
d &= \text{Nominal diameter (mm)} \\
L &= \text{Length of engagement (mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical:** For $d = 20$ mm, $L = 30$ mm, $p = 0.05$ mm, $f = 0.1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= 0.1 \cdot \pi \cdot 20 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.05 \\
    &= 0.1 \cdot 3.14159 \cdot 30 \\
    &= 9.42 \text{ N (in consistent units)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This force is then used to size the **pneumatic or servo press actuator** that performs the assembly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Automated Assembly & Inspection Cell

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** between the six subsystems described in Section 2. Each node represents a physical subsystem; each arrow represents a *signal or physical flow*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper / Bowl Feeder] -->|Oriented Part| B[Indexing Table]
    B -->|Part Transfer| C[Robot Manipulator SCARA 6 axis]
    C -->|Pick Place Action| D[Joining Station Press Snap Weld]
    D -->|Assembled Sub part| E[Vision Inspection Camera]
    E -->|Pass Signal| F[Conveyor Output]
    E -->|Reject Signal| G[Reject Bin]
    H[PLC Central Controller] -.->|Sequencing| A
    H -.->|Trigger| B
    H -.->|Trajectory| C
    H -.->|Force Profile| D
    H -.->|Image Acquire| E
    I[HMI Touchscreen] -.->|Recipe Select| H
    J[SPC Database] -.->|Quality Logs| E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows = **physical part flow** (left → right).
- Dashed arrows = **control/signal flow** (centre PLC outwards).
- The PLC is the *brain*; the manipulator is the *hand*; the inspection station is the *eye*.

---

### 4.2 Decision Logic Flow for Inspection Reject/Accept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Part Arrives at Inspection] --> L{Camera Acquires Image}
    L --> M{Defect Detected}
    M -- Yes --> N{Severity High?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Divert to Reject Bin]
    N -- No --> P[Mark as Rework]
    M -- No --> Q{Dimensional Within Tolerance}
    Q -- No --> R[Auto Adjust Station 2 Triggered]
    Q -- Yes --> S[Pass to Next Station]
    R --> T[Log to SPC Database]
    P --> T
    O --> T
    S --> U[Continue Assembly Line]
    T --> V[Statistical Control Chart Updated]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot-Based Inspection Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage["Input / Feeding"]
        AA[Bulk Part Bin] --> AB[Vibratory Bowl Feeder]
        AB --> AC[Singulation Track]
    end
    subgraph ManipulatorStage["Manipulator"]
        AC --> AD[Robot Pick Point]
        AD --> AE[6 Axis Articulated Arm]
        AE --> AF[End Effector Gripper]
    end
    subgraph InspectionStage["Inspection"]
        AF --> AG[Measuring Fixture LVDT]
        AF --> AH[Vision Camera Cognex]
        AH --> AI[Image Processor]
    end
    subgraph DecisionStage["Decision and Control"]
        AI --> AJ{Tolerance Pass?}
        AJ -- Pass --> AK[Conveyor to Next Station]
        AJ -- Fail --> AL[Reject Pusher Cylinder]
        AG --> AJ
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "draw a labelled diagram of an automated assembly system" in the 14-mark question, examiners award up to **4 marks** purely for a correctly labelled block diagram with arrows showing both *material* and *information* flow. Don't skip this!

---

### 4.4 Sub-System Interdependence Matrix

| Sub-System | Depends On | Constrains |
|---|---|---|
| Bowl Feeder | Part geometry, surface finish | Singulation rate |
| Manipulator | Feeder output timing | Cycle time ceiling |
| Joining Station | Robot payload, reach | Press force capacity |
| Inspection | Cycle time of upstream | Line throughput |
| PLC | All station I/O | Recipe flexibility |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "Automatic Assembly System" and list any four essential sub-systems it must contain.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An Automatic Assembly System is an integrated arrangement of mechanical workstations, parts feeders, transfer devices, and control units that performs the joining of components into a finished product without direct human intervention in the joining operation.

Four essential sub-systems:

1. **Parts feeding and orienting system** (e.g., bowl feeder, hopper).
2. **Transfer / transport system** (e.g., conveyor, indexing table).
3. **Joining mechanism / manipulator** (e.g., SCARA robot, press station).
4. **Inspection / verification system** (e.g., vision camera, CMM, LVDT).
5. **Control system** (PLC or PC-based sequencer).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing 4 sub-systems. Any 4 acceptable.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Contact and Non-Contact methods of automatic inspection with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Contact Inspection | Non-Contact Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Physical probe touches part | Energy wave interacts with part |
| Speed | Slow | Very fast |
| Examples | CMM, LVDT gauge, dial indicator | Vision camera, laser scanner, eddy current |
| Risk | May damage soft surfaces | No surface contact |

*Examples of Contact: Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM), Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT).*
*Examples of Non-Contact: Machine Vision System, Laser Triangulation Sensor.*

*(Award 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for examples on each side, 1 mark for distinguishing feature.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an automatic assembly system**. Explain the role of each sub-system in achieving 100% in-line inspection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-station automated assembly cell has an assembly operation time of **22 seconds**, transfer time of **5 seconds**, and vision inspection time of **3 seconds**. The cell operates for **8 hours per shift** with an availability of **92%**. Calculate: **(i)** cycle time, **(ii)** theoretical production rate, **(iii)** actual parts produced per shift, and **(iv)** overall equipment effectiveness if quality yield is 98%. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 Marks):**

The functional architecture of an automatic assembly system consists of **six interlinked sub-systems**:

1. **Parts Feeding System (1 mark)** — Accepts bulk components, orients them, and delivers them one-at-a-time to a pick station. Examples: vibratory bowl feeder, centrifugal feeder, magazine escapement.

2. **Transfer System (1 mark)** — Moves parts between stations with precise timing. Examples: indexing rotary table, walking-beam conveyor, pallet transfer line.

3. **Manipulator / Robot (1 mark)** — The active agent that picks, orients, and mates the components. SCARA robots are common for vertical insertion; 6-axis articulated arms are used for complex 3D trajectories.

4. **Joining Module (1 mark)** — Performs the actual fastening: snap-fit, press-fit, threaded fastening (screw-driving), ultrasonic welding, or adhesive dispensing.

5. **Inspection System (1 mark)** — Verifies geometric, dimensional, and surface integrity. **100% in-line inspection** means every part is checked before passing downstream — this is achieved by placing vision cameras immediately *after* each joining station.

6. **Control System (1 mark)** — A PLC orchestrates timing, sequencing, fault handling, and recipe selection. It also closes the feedback loop with the inspection station: a *reject* signal diverts the part to a rejection chute.

**Closing loop (1 mark):** The control system receives signals from the inspection station and adjusts downstream parameters (e.g., tightening torque, vision thresholds) — this is the **closed-loop quality architecture** that distinguishes modern cells from older open-loop systems.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_a = 22$ s (assembly)
- $T_t = 5$ s (transfer)
- $T_i = 3$ s (inspection)
- Shift duration = 8 hr
- Availability $\eta = 0.92 = 92\%$
- Quality yield $Q = 0.98$

**(i) Cycle Time (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= T_a + T_t + T_i \\
    &= 22 + 5 + 3 = 30 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical Production Rate (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \\
    &= \dfrac{3600}{30} \\
    &= 120 \text{ parts/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Actual Parts Produced per Shift (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{op} &= 8 \times 3600 \times 0.92 = 26{,}496 \text{ seconds} \\
N &= \dfrac{T_{op}}{T_c} = \dfrac{26{,}496}{30} \\
  &= 883.2 \approx 883 \text{ parts per shift}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.92 \quad \text{(given)} \\
P &= 1.0 \quad \text{(assuming no minor stoppages)} \\
Q &= 0.98 \quad \text{(given)} \\
OEE &= A \cdot P \cdot Q = 0.92 \times 1.0 \times 0.98 \\
    &= 0.9016 \approx 90.16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An OEE of 90.16% is **world-class** (the global benchmark is 85%). The bottleneck is the assembly time of 22 s — reducing it would directly improve throughput.

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of automatic inspection systems** based on contact and non-contact methods. Describe the working principle of a **machine vision system** with a labelled diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bowl feeder delivers parts at a rate of **45 parts/minute**. A robot picks each part in **1.2 seconds**, performs the assembly in **4.5 seconds**, and places it on the conveyor in **0.8 seconds**. A vision camera inspects each part in **0.5 seconds**. Calculate: **(i)** cycle time of the cell, **(ii)** maximum production rate, and **(iii)** whether the bowl feeder becomes a bottleneck. If yes, suggest a remedy. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Classification and Machine Vision (7 Marks):**

**Classification (4 Marks):**

Automatic inspection systems are classified into:

1. **Contact Type** (1 mark): Probe physically touches the part.
   - *Examples*: Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM), pneumatic gauge, LVDT.
   - *Advantage*: High accuracy (±0.001 mm).
   - *Disadvantage*: Slow, may damage soft parts.

2. **Non-Contact Type** (1 mark): Uses light, sound, or electromagnetic fields.
