   - *Examples*: Machine vision, laser scanner, ultrasonic, eddy current.
   - *Advantage*: Fast, no part damage.
   - *Disadvantage*: Calibration-sensitive.

**Machine Vision Working Principle (2 Marks):**

A machine vision system consists of:
- **Lighting chamber** (uniform illumination).
- **CCD/CMOS camera** (image acquisition).
- **Lens** (defines field of view).
- **Image processor** (edge detection, blob analysis, pattern matching).
- **I/O module** (pass/reject signal).

The camera captures the part image; the processor compares it against a *golden template* using algorithms like *pattern matching*, *edge detection*, or *blob analysis*. The decision (Pass/Reject) is sent to the PLC within milliseconds.

**Labelled Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to the functional architecture diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Feeder rate = 45 parts/min = **0.75 parts/sec**
- Robot pick time = 1.2 s
- Assembly time = 4.5 s
- Place time = 0.8 s
- Inspection time = 0.5 s

**(i) Cycle Time (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= T_{pick} + T_{assemble} + T_{place} + T_{inspect} \\
    &= 1.2 + 4.5 + 0.8 + 0.5 \\
    &= 7.0 \text{ seconds/part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Production Rate (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \\
    &= \dfrac{3600}{7.0} \\
    &\approx 514.29 \text{ parts/hour} \\
    &\approx 8.57 \text{ parts/minute}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Bottleneck Analysis (3 Marks):**

Feeder can supply **45 parts/min**.
Cell consumes **8.57 parts/min**.

Since **45 > 8.57**, the bowl feeder is **NOT** a bottleneck — it has a surplus capacity of $\approx 36.4$ parts/min.

**However**, the cell could be **upgraded** to consume more parts (parallel manipulators), and then the feeder might become the bottleneck. In such a case, **remedies** include:
- Adding a **second bowl feeder** in parallel.
- Using a **larger bowl** with multi-track output.
- Switching to a **centrifugal feeder** for higher throughput.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply availability with shift hours** — many students compute parts using theoretical 8 hours × 144 parts/hr instead of effective 7.6 hours. This loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up OEE components** — OEE is multiplicative (A × P × Q), *not* additive. Adding percentages is wrong.
> 3. **Cycle time ≠ single longest operation** — it is the *sum* of all sequential operations in the cell.
> 4. **Inspection time must be in the cycle time** — if you forget this, your $T_c$ is too low and your production rate will be unrealistically high.
> 5. **Unit conversion** — parts/minute vs parts/hour trap; convert feeder rates carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- **Definition of Automatic Assembly**: Joining of components without human intervention in the joining step.
- **Definition of Automatic Inspection**: Automated verification of geometric, dimensional, and surface quality.
- **Six Sub-Systems**: Feeder → Transfer → Manipulator → Joining → Inspection → Control.
- **Manipulator vs Functional System**: Manipulator is the *active agent*; functional system is the *supporting mechanism*.
- **Production Rate Formula**: $P_r = 3600 / T_c$ — remember the constant **3600**.
- **Cycle Time Formula**: $T_c = T_a + T_t + T_i$ — sum, *not* max.
- **OEE Formula**: $OEE = A \cdot P \cdot Q$ — multiplicative, not additive.
- **Vision Resolution**: $F = \text{FOV}/\text{Pixels} \cdot 2$ — the factor 2 is for Nyquist sampling.
- **Press-Fit Force**: $F_p = f \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot p$ — used to size the actuator.
- **Rule of Ten**: Cost of catching a defect rises 10× at each downstream stage — supports in-line inspection.
- **World-Class OEE Benchmark**: 85% or higher.
- **Typical Cycle Times**: SMT pick-and-place 0.04 s/comp; automotive 60–90 s; pharmaceutical 200,000 tablets/hr.
- **Inspection Choice**: Contact for accuracy; non-contact for speed and fragile parts.
- **Closed-Loop Quality**: Inspection output must feed back to PLC — never treat inspection as a passive endpoint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manipulators: Dynamics, Kinematics, Vibrations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT743 - DESIGNING THE MECHANISMS FOR AUTOMATED MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manipulators: Dynamics, Kinematics, Vibrations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Manipulator?

A **Manipulator** is a mechanically articulated chain of rigid links connected by joints (revolute or prismatic), terminated by an **end-effector**, whose purpose is to perform tasks by precisely controlling position, orientation, and force in 3-D space. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a manipulator is the *mechanical skeleton* of an industrial robot — for example, a 6-DOF articulated arm used in CNC tending or welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT743):**
> A manipulator is a programmable, multifunctional mechanism designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. Its study involves three pillars: **Kinematics** (geometry of motion), **Dynamics** (forces causing motion), and **Vibrations** (oscillatory response).

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars — At a Glance

### 🔹 Kinematics
**Definition:** The geometric study of motion *without* considering the forces that cause it. It answers: *"Where does the end-effector go when the joints move?"*

### 🔹 Dynamics
**Definition:** The study of motion *with* the forces and torques that cause it. It answers: *"What joint torques are required to produce a desired motion?"*

### 🔹 Vibrations
**Definition:** The study of oscillatory motion about an equilibrium position. It answers: *"How does the manipulator respond to disturbances, start–stop cycles, and resonance?"*

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine a person picking up a glass of water from a table:

| Human Body Part | Manipulator Equivalent | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| Shoulder | Joint 1 (base) | Revolute Joint |
| Upper arm | Link 1 | Rigid Link |
| Elbow | Joint 2 | Revolute Joint |
| Forearm | Link 2 | Rigid Link |
| Wrist | Joint 3 | Revolute/Spherical Joint |
| Palm | End-effector | Tool/ Gripper |
| Brain + muscles | Controller + actuators | Drives |

- **Kinematics** = *"I extend my elbow at 45° and rotate my shoulder at 30° — my palm reaches the glass."* → Forward Kinematics.
- **Dynamics** = *"To lift this 200 g glass, my biceps must pull with 1.2 N·m of torque."* → Euler–Lagrange / Newton–Euler.
- **Vibrations** = *"If I jerk my arm quickly, my hand overshoots and oscillates before settling."* → Damped Free Vibration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The three topics are *not isolated*. Dynamics equations contain kinematic terms (Jacobians, velocity products), and vibration analysis is essentially dynamics applied to small perturbations about a steady operating point.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Quantities & Standard Metrics

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a manipulator: $n = 6$ for full spatial positioning (3 translation + 3 orientation).
- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Standard coordinate frames:** Base frame $\{0\}$, End-effector frame $\{n\}$, World frame $\{W\}$.
- **Standard joint types:** Revolute (R) — 1 DOF rotational; Prismatic (P) — 1 DOF translational.

---

## 1.5 Manipulator Kinematics — Formal Definition

**Manipulator Kinematics** is the analytical mapping between two configuration spaces:

$$\text{Joint Space} \;\; \mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Cartesian (Task) Space} \;\; \mathbf{x} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$$

This mapping is performed using **homogeneous transformation matrices** built from **Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H) parameters**.

---

## 1.6 Manipulator Dynamics — Formal Definition

The generalized **equations of motion** of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator in joint space are written in the canonical KTU form:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

where each symbol has a precise physical meaning (developed in Section 2).

---

## 1.7 Vibrations in Manipulators — Formal Definition

A manipulator arm is essentially a chain of flexible links and joints. Under the **lumped-parameter assumption**, each link can be modelled as a spring-mass-damper system. The vibration study classifies responses into:

- **Free Vibration** — system oscillates after a single disturbance.
- **Forced Vibration** — system driven by a continuous external force.
- **Damped Vibration** — amplitude decays due to dissipative elements.
- **Resonance** — driving frequency matches natural frequency → catastrophic amplitude growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Every kinematics/dynamics/vibration problem in PERUT743 begins by identifying the DOF, writing the joint variable vector $\mathbf{q}$, and selecting the right formulation. Marks are awarded for *systematic setup* before numerical substitution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manipulator Kinematics — Two Sub-Problems

### 2.1.1 Forward Kinematics (FK)

**Goal:** Given joint variables $\mathbf{q}$, find end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}$.

$$\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$$

Steps:
1. Assign frames using the **D-H Convention** (4 parameters per joint).
2. Form individual transformation matrices $^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i$.
3. Multiply: $^0\mathbf{T}_n = \,^0\mathbf{A}_1 \cdot {}^1\mathbf{A}_2 \cdots {}^{n-1}\mathbf{A}_n$.

### 2.1.2 Inverse Kinematics (IK)

**Goal:** Given $\mathbf{x}$, find $\mathbf{q}$.

$$\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$$

Methods: *Closed-form (geometric, algebraic)* or *Numerical (Newton–Raphson, Jacobian-based iteration)*.

### 2.1.3 D-H Parameters

Each joint $i$ is described by **four** parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Link length | $a_i$ | Distance along $X_i$ between $Z_{i-1}$ and $Z_i$ |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Angle about $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Distance along $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ |
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Angle about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ |

The individual transformation matrix is:

$$^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.1.4 Jacobian Matrix

The Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ linearly maps joint velocities to end-effector velocities:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

For a revolute joint $i$, the $i$-th column of $\mathbf{J}$ is:

$$\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{p}_n - \mathbf{p}_{i-1}) \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ is the axis of joint $i$ and $\mathbf{p}_k$ is the position of frame origin $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Singularities** occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$, causing loss of DOF and infinite joint velocities for a bounded end-effector velocity. KTU frequently tests identification of singular configurations.

---

## 2.2 Manipulator Dynamics — Two Formulations

### 2.2.1 Euler–Lagrange Formulation

The Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ (kinetic minus potential energy). The generalized forces are obtained from:

$$\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

### 2.2.2 Newton–Euler Formulation

Iterative **forward** (link accelerations) and **backward** (joint forces) recursion:

- **Forward:** $^{i+1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1} = \,^i\mathbf{R}_{i+1}\,^i\boldsymbol{\omega}_i + \dot{q}_{i+1}\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{i+1}$
- **Backward:** $\boldsymbol{\tau}_i = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}^T \,^i\mathbf{f}_i$

### 2.2.3 Canonical Equation of Motion

$$\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})\;}$$

- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — **Mass/Inertia matrix** (symmetric, positive definite, $n \times n$).
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — **Coriolis and centripetal vector**.
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — **Gravitational torque vector**.
- $\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ — **Friction vector** (viscous + Coulomb).

---

## 2.3 Vibrations — Single-DOF Fundamentals

The canonical equation for a **forced, damped SDOF** system:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

Definitions:
- **Natural (undamped) frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ **(rad/s)**.
- **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$.
- **Critical damping coefficient:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$.

Cases:
- $\zeta = 0$ → Undamped free vibration.
- $0 < \zeta < 1$ → **Underdamped** (oscillatory decay).
- $\zeta = 1$ → **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory return).
- $\zeta > 1$ → **Overdamped** (slow return).

For **harmonic forcing** $F(t) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state amplitude is:

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{\left(1 - r^2\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta r\right)^2}}, \quad r = \omega/\omega_n$$

---

## 2.4 Two-DOF Vibration (Manipulator Link)

Each flexible link of a manipulator behaves as a 2-DOF system with **bending modes**. The matrix form is:

$$\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$$

Natural frequencies are obtained from $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) = 0$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics | $\,^0\mathbf{T}_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\,^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | D-H Matrix | $^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Jacobian | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | mixed |
| 4 | Singularity | $\det\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ | — |
| 5 | Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ | J (joule) |
| 6 | Generalized Force | $\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i}$ | N·m or N |
| 7 | Equation of Motion | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | N·m |
| 8 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Critically Damped Coeff. | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ | N·s/m |
| 11 | Damped Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 12 | Steady-State Amplitude | $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m |
| 13 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Multi-DOF Eigenvalue | $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2\mathbf{M}) = 0$ | — |
| 15 | Logarithmic Decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipes inside table cells** — every delimiter $\vert$ in formulas has been written as `\vert` or `\mid` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robotics (KUKA, ABB, FANUC):** Forward kinematics is computed every 4–8 ms in real-time controllers; inverse kinematics solves pick-and-place paths.
- **Space Manipulators (Canadarm):** Dynamics equations are embedded in attitude-control loops for debris capture.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Vibration damping in the linkage ensures sub-millimetre precision — modelled using $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.8$.
- **CNC Machine Tools:** Resonance avoidance in feed drives uses the $r \neq 1$ rule derived from the frequency response.
- **Automotive Suspension:** Multi-DOF vibration of vehicle chassis directly uses the $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = 0$ framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics via D-H (2-DOF Planar RR Manipulator)

**Setup:** A 2-link planar manipulator with both joints revolute, link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$.

### Step 1 — D-H Parameter Table

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

### Step 2 — Individual Transformation Matrices

Using the generic $^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i$ with $d_i = 0$, $\alpha_i = 0$:

$$^0\mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & a_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & a_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad ^1\mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & a_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & a_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply to Get Forward Kinematics

$^0\mathbf{T}_2 = \,^0\mathbf{A}_1 \,^1\mathbf{A}_2$. Expand the product. Using $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ and $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$:

$$^0\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_{12} & -s_{12} & 0 & a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ s_{12} & c_{12} & 0 & a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Extract End-Effector Position

$$x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

These three equations constitute the **forward kinematics** for the planar RR arm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Jacobian of the 2-DOF Planar RR Manipulator

### Step 1 — Position Vector from FK

$$\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\ a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Time

$$\dot{x} = -a_1 s_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 - a_2 s_{12}\,(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$
$$\dot{y} = a_1 c_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 + a_2 c_{12}\,(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange in Matrix Form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12} & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12} & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Singularity Analysis

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12})(a_2 c_{12}) - (-a_2 s_{12})(a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12})$$

After expanding and cancelling the common term $a_2^2 s_{12} c_{12}$:

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = -a_1 a_2 s_1 c_{12} + a_1 a_2 s_{12} c_1 = a_1 a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2 - \theta_1) = a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_2$$

$$\boxed{\;\det(\mathbf{J}) = a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_2\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The manipulator is **singular** when $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$, i.e., when the arm is fully stretched out or fully folded back. At these configurations, end-effector motion in the radial direction requires infinite joint rates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Euler–Lagrange Equation of Motion (2-DOF RR Manipulator under Gravity)

### Step 1 — Position of Mass Centres

Let $l_1$ and $l_2$ be distances to the mass centres $m_1$ and $m_2$:

$$\mathbf{p}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ l_1 \sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{p}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} a_1\cos\theta_1 + l_2\cos\theta_{12} \\ a_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin\theta_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Velocities (Squared)

$$\dot{\mathbf{p}}_1 \cdot \dot{\mathbf{p}}_1 = l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2$$

$$\dot{\mathbf{p}}_2 \cdot \dot{\mathbf{p}}_2 = a_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_2^2 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2 a_1 l_2 \dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2$$

### Step 3 — Kinetic Energy

$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 [a_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2a_1 l_2 \dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2] + \tfrac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2$$

### Step 4 — Potential Energy

$$V = m_1 g l_1 \sin\theta_1 + m_2 g (a_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin\theta_{12})$$

### Step 5 — Lagrangian and Partial Derivatives

$$\mathcal{L} = T - V$$

Compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{\theta}_1$, $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{\theta}_2$ and their time derivatives, and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \theta_1$, $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \theta_2$.

After simplification (long but mechanical algebra), the equations of motion are:

$$\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 + C_{12}\dot{\theta}_2^2 + 2C_{12}\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + G_1$$

$$\tau_2 = M_{21}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + C_{12}\dot{\theta}_1^2 + G_2$$

where:

$$M_{11} = m_1 l_1^2 + m_2(a_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2a_1 l_2\cos\theta_2) + I_1 + I_2$$
$$M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2(l_2^2 + a_1 l_2\cos\theta_2) + I_2$$
$$M_{22} = m_2 l_2^2 + I_2$$
$$C_{12} = -m_2 a_1 l_2 \sin\theta_2$$
$$G_1 = (m_1 l_1 + m_2 a_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_2 g\cos\theta_{12}$$
$$G_2 = m_2 l_2 g\cos\theta_{12}$$

### Step 6 — Canonical Form

$$\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{\theta}_1 \\ \ddot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} C_{12}\dot{\theta}_2 & C_{12}(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ -C_{12}\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $\mathbf{M}$ matrix is always **symmetric** ($M_{12} = M_{21}$) and **positive definite** — KTU often tests this property.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Free Vibration Response of a SDOF Undamped System

**Equation of motion:** $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$

### Step 1 — Characteristic Equation

Assume $x = e^{\lambda t}$:

$$m\lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + k e^{\lambda t} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; m\lambda^2 + k = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda^2 = -\frac{k}{m}$$

### Step 2 — Roots

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \pm i\sqrt{k/m} = \pm i\omega_n$$

### Step 3 — General Solution

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Initial Conditions

Let $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$. Then $A = x_0$ and $B = v_0/\omega_n$. Hence:

$$x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)$$

This can be written in single-amplitude form:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi), \quad X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Forced Damped Vibration — Steady-State Solution

**Equation of motion:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$

### Step 1 — Particular Solution Form

Assume $x_p = X\sin(\omega t - \psi)$. Substituting and matching coefficients of $\sin$ and $\cos$:

$$\left(k - m\omega^2\right)X\cos\psi + c\omega X\sin\psi = F_0$$
$$\left(k - m\omega^2\right)X\sin\psi - c\omega X\cos\psi = 0$$

### Step 2 — Solve

Square and add the two equations:

$$\left[(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2\right] X^2 = F_0^2$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $k$ and let $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

$$\boxed{\;X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}$$

### Step 3 — Phase Angle

$$\tan\psi = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}$$

### Step 4 — Resonance

The amplitude $X$ is maximum when the denominator is minimum. Differentiate and set to zero:

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}, \quad X_{max} = \frac{F_0/k}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For $\zeta \ll 1$, $r_{max} \approx 1$, i.e., resonance occurs when $\omega \approx \omega_n$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation vs. Method Choice

| Problem Type | Best KTU Method | Output Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| Single-link pose | D-H + Matrix product | Position $(x,y,z)$ & orientation $(\phi,\theta,\psi)$ |
| Multi-link pose | D-H + Matrix product | $^0\mathbf{T}_n$ |
| Joint torques (offline) | Euler–Lagrange | $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ |
| Joint torques (real-time) | Newton–Euler recursion | $\boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$ |
| Joint velocities from end-effector | Jacobian | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ |
| Static balance | $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ | Equilibrium posture |
| Free vibration | Solve ODE: $x = X\cos(\omega_n t + \phi)$ | Natural frequency $\omega_n$ |
| Forced vibration | Steady-state harmonic balance | Amplitude $X$, phase $\psi$ |
| Resonance check | $r = \omega/\omega_n \approx 1$ | Avoid or damp |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define the Jacobian matrix of a manipulator. Mention its significance in robotics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Jacobian matrix** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the first-order partial derivative of the forward kinematics mapping with respect to the joint variables. It linearly relates the end-effector velocity vector $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ in Cartesian space to the joint velocity vector $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ in joint space:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Significance:**
1. It is used to convert joint velocities into end-effector velocities (forward differential kinematics).
2. It is used to find joint velocities for a desired Cartesian velocity via $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ (inverse differential kinematics).
3. It identifies **singular configurations** where $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$, leading to loss of dexterity.
4. It is used to transform forces/torques from joint space to Cartesian space using the principle of virtual work: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State the equation of motion of an $n$-DOF manipulator in joint space. Explain each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The general equation of motion of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator is:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

- **$\boldsymbol{\tau}$** — Vector of generalized joint forces/torques (N or N·m).
- **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$** — Inertia force/torque term; $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is the $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix.
- **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$** — Coriolis and centripetal torque vector, accounting for velocity-dependent coupling between joints.
- **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$** — Gravitational torque vector arising from the weights of the links and payloads.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** For a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator with link lengths $a_1 = 0.5$ m and $a_2 = 0.4$ m, derive the forward kinematics using D-H parameters and obtain the Jacobian matrix. Identify the singular configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-DOF spring-mass-damper system has $m = 10$ kg, $k = 4000$ N/m, and $c = 80$ N·s/m. Determine: (i) natural frequency, (ii) damping ratio, (iii) damped natural frequency, and (iv) classify the damping. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: D-H Parameters (1 mark)**

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

**Step 2: Individual A matrices (1 mark)**

$$^0\mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & 0.5c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & 0.5s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad ^1\mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & 0.4c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & 0.4s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Multiply to get $^0\mathbf{T}_2$ (2 marks)**

$$^0\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_{12} & -s_{12} & 0 & 0.5c_1 + 0.4c_{12} \\ s_{12} & c_{12} & 0 & 0.5s_1 + 0.4s_{12} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

End-effector position: $x = 0.5\cos\theta_1 + 0.4\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $y = 0.5\sin\theta_1 + 0.4\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.

**Step 4: Jacobian derivation (2 marks)**

Differentiating $(x,y)$ with respect to time gives:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 s_1 - 0.4 s_{12} & -0.4 s_{12} \\ 0.5 c_1 + 0.4 c_{12} & 0.4 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Singular configuration (1 mark)**

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0.5 \times 0.4 \sin\theta_2 = 0.2\sin\theta_2$$

The manipulator is **singular** when $\sin\theta_2 = 0$, i.e., $\theta_2 = 0°$ (arm fully stretched) or $\theta_2 = 180°$ (arm folded back).

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Natural frequency (2 marks)**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{10}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Critical damping coefficient (1 mark)**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{4000 \times 10} = 2\sqrt{40000} = 400 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 3: Damping ratio (2 marks)**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{80}{400} = 0.2$$

**Step 4: Damped natural frequency (1 mark)**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1-0.04} = 20\sqrt{0.96} \approx 19.60 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Classification (1 mark)**

Since $0 < \zeta = 0.2 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it will exhibit oscillatory decay at $\omega_d$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the Euler–Lagrange equation of motion for a 2-DOF RR planar manipulator with link masses $m_1$, $m_2$, link lengths $a_1$, $a_2$, and centre-of-mass distances $l_1$, $l_2$ from respective joints. Clearly write down $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic arm link is modelled as a cantilever beam of length $L = 1.0$ m, tip mass $M = 5$ kg, flexural rigidity $EI = 200$ N·m². Estimate the first natural frequency of transverse vibration. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Position of mass centres (1 mark)**

$$\mathbf{p}_1 = (l_1 c_1,\; l_1 s_1)$$
$$\mathbf{p}_2 = (a_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12},\; a_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12})$$

**Step 2: Kinetic and potential energies (2 marks)**

$$T = \tfrac{1}{2}m_1 l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2\left[a_1^2\dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2a_1 l_2\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2\right] + \tfrac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2$$

$$V = m_1 g l_1 \sin\theta_1 + m_2 g(a_1\sin\theta_1 + l_2\sin\theta_{12})$$

**Step 3: Apply Euler–Lagrange equations (2 marks)**

After carrying out the differentiation (as in Section 3.3), the canonical equation is:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

with

$$\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^2 + m_2(a_1^2+l_2^2+2a_1 l_2\cos\theta_2)+I_1+I_2 & m_2(l_2^2+a_1 l_2\cos\theta_2)+I_2 \\ m_2(l_2^2+a_1 l_2\cos\theta_2)+I_2 & m_2 l_2^2+I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 a_1 l_2\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2 & -m_2 a_1 l_2\sin\theta_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ m_2 a_1 l_2\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_1 + m_2 a_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_2 g\cos\theta_{12} \\ m_2 l_2 g\cos\theta_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Verify properties (2 marks)**

- $\mathbf{M}$ is **symmetric** ($M_{12} = M_{21}$) and **positive definite**.
- For $\dot{\mathbf{q}}^T(\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ → $\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric in the velocity sense**.
- Gravity vector vanishes in the horizontal plane ($\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 0$ at horizontal pose).

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Equivalent stiffness for tip-loaded cantilever (2 marks)**

For a cantilever beam of length $L$ with a tip mass $M$, the equivalent translational stiffness at the tip is:

$$k_{eq} = \frac{3EI}{L^3}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (1 mark)**

$$k_{eq} = \frac{3 \times 200}{(1.0)^3} = 600 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 3: Effective mass for SDOF approximation (2 marks)**

For a cantilever, the equivalent mass is approximately one-third of the tip mass plus any distributed mass. With only a tip mass:

$$m_{eq} \approx M = 5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Natural frequency (2 marks)**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m_{eq}}} = \sqrt{\frac{600}{5}} = \sqrt{120} \approx 10.95 \text{ rad/s}$$

In Hz: $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{10.95}{2\pi} \approx 1.74$ Hz.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PERUT743 (Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing Frame Conventions:** Students often mix up D-H *modified* and *classic* conventions, leading to signs flipped in $^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i$. Always declare the convention at the start.
> 2. **Singularity Detection:** Writing $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ without **simplifying** (e.g., directly equating the unsimplified determinant to zero) loses the geometric interpretation. Always expand and simplify first.
> 3. **Lagrangian Mechanics:** Skipping the step of *explicitly writing* $T$ and $V$ before forming $\mathcal{L} = T - V$ costs at least 2 marks. Partial derivatives cannot be graded without intermediate energy expressions.
> 4. **Damping Classification:** Many students forget to compute $c_c$ and directly classify the system. Always report $\zeta = c/c_c$ and explicitly state underdamped/critical/overdamped.
> 5. **Units:** Joint torque $\tau$ must be in **N·m** (revolute) or **N** (prismatic), not mixed. Frequency must be in **rad/s** for $\omega_n$ and **Hz** for $f_n$.
> 6. **Steady-State vs. Transient:** In forced vibration, KTU expects you to state that the **transient part decays** for damped systems and the long-term response is purely **steady-state** $X\sin(\omega t - \psi)$.
> 7. **Symbol Confusion:** Using $q$ for generalized coordinate and joint angle interchangeably. Stick to $\theta$ for revolute and $d$ for prismatic in the D-H table.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### 🔸 Kinematics
- [ ] Forward kinematics = product of D-H matrices $^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i$.
- [ ] Four D-H parameters per joint: $a_i$, $\alpha_i$, $d_i$, $\theta_i$.
- [ ] Inverse kinematics has multiple solutions (joint limit / elbow-up selection).
- [ ] Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ relates $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- [ ] Singularity: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ → loss of DOF.
- [ ] Static force relation: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}$ (virtual work principle).

### 🔸 Dynamics
- [ ] Canonical equation: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$.
- [ ] $\mathbf{M}$ is **symmetric, positive definite**, $n \times n$.
- [ ] Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L} = T - V$.
- [ ] Generalized force: $\tau_i = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i}$.
- [ ] Newton–Euler uses **forward** (kinematics) and **backward** (force) recursion.
- [ ] Computed-torque controller: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{G} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e} + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}}$.

### 🔸 Vibrations
- [ ] SDOF: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- [ ] $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$.
- [ ] Underdamped ($0 < \zeta < 1$), critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), overdamped ($\zeta > 1$).
- [ ] Forced steady-state amplitude: $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$.
- [ ] Resonance peak near $r \approx 1$ for low damping.
- [ ] Multi-DOF eigenvalue problem: $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2\mathbf{M}) = 0$.
- [ ] Logarithmic decrement $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right) \approx \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.

### 🔸 Cross-Cutting KTU Tips
- [ ] Always draw the **kinematic diagram** with frame assignments before deriving matrices.
- [ ] Always state the **assumptions** (rigid links, no friction, planar motion, etc.).
- [ ] Always check **units** in the final answer.
- [ ] In 14-mark problems, **show all matrix multiplications** — partial credit is awarded for correct intermediate steps.
- [ ] Memorize the **2-DOF RR Jacobian determinant**: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_2$ — a frequently repeated KTU question.
